| UseHttpsRedirection | Redirect HTTP to HTTPS                |
| UseRouting          | Matches incoming request to endpoints |
| UseAuthentication   | Identifies the user                   |
| UseAuthorization    | Checks user permissions               |
| UseCors             | Enables cross-origin requests         |
| UseStaticFiles      | Serves CSS, JS, Images                |
| UseExceptionHandler | Handles unhandled exceptions          |

Each middleware has a specific responsibility.

---

## 8. Middleware vs Controller

| Middleware                 | Controller                      |
| -------------------------- | ------------------------------- |
| Runs for every request     | Runs only for matched endpoints |
| Handles common tasks       | Handles business logic          |
| Configured in Program.cs   | Located in Controllers folder   |
| Executes before controller | Executes after middleware       |
| Can stop the pipeline      | Usually returns a response      |

---

# 6. Code Example

```csharp
var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args);

builder.Services.AddControllers();

var app = builder.Build();

app.UseHttpsRedirection();

app.UseAuthentication();

app.UseAuthorization();

app.MapControllers();

app.Run();
```

---

## Code Explanation

```csharp
app.UseHttpsRedirection();
```

Redirects HTTP requests to HTTPS.

---

```csharp
app.UseAuthentication();
```

Checks whether the user is authenticated.

---

```csharp
app.UseAuthorization();
```

Checks whether the authenticated user has permission to access the requested resource.

---

```csharp
app.MapControllers();
```

Routes the request to the appropriate controller action.

---

```csharp
app.Run();
```

Starts the application and begins processing incoming HTTP requests.

---

# 7. Real Project Usage ⭐

In my ASP.NET Core projects, middleware handled common concerns before requests reached the controllers. HTTPS redirection ensured secure communication, authentication validated users, authorization checked access permissions, and routing directed requests to the correct API endpoints. Keeping these responsibilities in middleware allowed controllers to focus only on business logic, making the application cleaner and easier to maintain. 

---

# 8. Best Practices

* Keep each middleware focused on a single responsibility.
* Place middleware in the correct order.
* Register authentication before authorization.
* Avoid writing business logic in middleware.
* Use built-in middleware whenever possible.
* Create custom middleware only when necessary.
* Keep `Program.cs` organized by grouping related middleware.

---

# 9. Common Mistakes

* Registering middleware in the wrong order.
* Forgetting to call the next middleware when required.
* Placing `MapControllers()` before authentication or authorization middleware.
* Writing business logic inside middleware.
* Creating one middleware that performs multiple unrelated tasks.
* Assuming every middleware always executes; short-circuiting can stop the pipeline early.

---

# 10. Key Points to Remember

* Middleware processes every HTTP request and response.
* Requests travel from top to bottom through the pipeline.
* Responses travel back from bottom to top.
* Middleware order is critical.
* Middleware can execute code before and after the next middleware.
* Middleware can stop the pipeline by returning a response.
* Controllers should contain business logic, while middleware should handle cross-cutting concerns.

---

# 11. Summary

Middleware is one of the core building blocks of ASP.NET Core. Every incoming request passes through a sequence of middleware components before reaching the controller. Each middleware has a specific responsibility, such as logging, authentication, authorization, or HTTPS redirection. Because middleware executes in the order it is registered, placing it in the correct sequence is essential for the application to work correctly. Using middleware properly keeps controllers clean, improves maintainability, and provides a centralized way to handle common functionality across the entire application.

---

**Next:** **Part 2 – Interview Preparation**, which will include:

* **6–8 Interview Questions** (each with **10–12 line answers**)
* **2–3 Project-Based Interview Questions**
* **8–10 Rapid Fire Questions**
* **2–3 Follow-up Questions**
* **Interview Tips**
* **Revision Notes**
* **Keywords**

# Module 2 – The Host & Request Pipeline

# Topic 3 – What Middleware Is and Why Order Matters (Part 2 – Interview Preparation)

---

# ⭐ Possible Interview Questions

## Q1. What is Middleware in ASP.NET Core?

### Answer

Middleware is a software component that processes every HTTP request and HTTP response in an ASP.NET Core application. Every request sent by the client passes through a series of middleware components before reaching the controller. Each middleware performs a specific task such as logging, authentication, authorization, exception handling, or HTTPS redirection. A middleware can execute code before and after the next middleware in the pipeline. It can also stop the pipeline and return a response without calling the next middleware. This helps centralize common functionality instead of repeating it in every controller. Middleware keeps controllers clean by separating cross-cutting concerns from business logic. It is configured inside **Program.cs** using methods like `UseAuthentication()` and `UseAuthorization()`. Proper middleware configuration is essential for every ASP.NET Core application.

---

## Q2. Explain the ASP.NET Core Request Pipeline.

### Answer

The Request Pipeline is the sequence of middleware through which every HTTP request passes before reaching the controller. When a client sends a request, the first middleware receives it and performs its task. It then passes the request to the next middleware until it finally reaches the controller. After the controller generates a response, the response travels back through the middleware in reverse order before being sent to the client. This allows middleware to process both requests and responses. The pipeline is configured in **Program.cs**. The order of middleware determines how requests are handled. Incorrect ordering can cause authentication, authorization, routing, or exception handling to fail. Therefore, understanding the request pipeline is fundamental to ASP.NET Core development.

---

## Q3. Why is Middleware Order Important?

### Answer

Middleware executes in the exact order in which it is registered in **Program.cs**. Because each middleware depends on the previous one, incorrect ordering can cause unexpected behavior. For example, authentication should always execute before authorization. If authorization runs first, the application does not know who the user is and access may be denied incorrectly. Similarly, routing should occur before endpoint execution. Some middleware also performs work after the next middleware completes, so changing the order affects both request and response processing. Following Microsoft's recommended middleware order ensures security, reliability, and predictable request handling. Proper ordering is one of the most important best practices in ASP.NET Core.

---

## Q4. What is the difference between Middleware and Controllers?

### Answer

Middleware and controllers have different responsibilities in an ASP.NET Core application. Middleware executes for every incoming request regardless of which endpoint is being accessed. It handles common tasks such as logging, authentication, authorization, exception handling, and HTTPS redirection. Controllers, on the other hand, execute only after routing selects a matching endpoint. Their responsibility is to implement business logic, interact with services or databases, and return responses to the client. Middleware is configured in **Program.cs**, while controllers are placed inside the **Controllers** folder. Using middleware for common functionality keeps controllers smaller, cleaner, and easier to maintain.

---

## Q5. What is Short-Circuiting in Middleware?

### Answer

Short-circuiting occurs when a middleware stops the request pipeline without calling the next middleware. Instead of forwarding the request, it immediately returns a response to the client. A common example is authentication middleware. If a request contains an invalid or missing token, the middleware returns **401 Unauthorized** without allowing the request to reach the controller. This improves performance because unnecessary middleware and controller execution are skipped. Short-circuiting is also used in exception handling, static file serving, and custom middleware. It is an important concept because not every request reaches the controller. Middleware has complete control over whether processing continues.

---

## Q6. Name some built-in middleware available in ASP.NET Core.

### Answer

ASP.NET Core provides several built-in middleware components for common tasks. `UseHttpsRedirection()` redirects HTTP requests to HTTPS. `UseRouting()` matches incoming requests to endpoints. `UseAuthentication()` verifies the user's identity, while `UseAuthorization()` checks whether the authenticated user has permission to access the requested resource. `UseCors()` enables cross-origin requests from frontend applications. `UseStaticFiles()` serves images, CSS, and JavaScript files. `UseExceptionHandler()` handles unhandled exceptions and returns user-friendly error responses. Each middleware performs a single responsibility and is added to the request pipeline in **Program.cs**.

---

## Q7. Can Middleware Execute Code Before and After the Next Middleware?

### Answer

Yes. One of the unique features of middleware is that it can execute code both before and after the next middleware in the pipeline. Before calling the next middleware, it can inspect or modify the incoming request. After the next middleware completes, it can inspect or modify the outgoing response. This capability is useful for logging request execution time, measuring application performance, adding response headers, compressing responses, and auditing requests. This behavior is possible because middleware wraps around the next middleware like a chain. It gives developers complete control over both request and response processing.

---

# ⭐ Project-Based Interview Questions

## Q1. How was Middleware used in your project?

### Answer

In my ASP.NET Core projects, middleware handled common functionality before requests reached the controllers. We used HTTPS redirection to ensure secure communication, authentication middleware to validate users, authorization middleware to enforce access permissions, and routing middleware to direct requests to the correct endpoints. This allowed controllers to focus only on business logic while middleware handled cross-cutting concerns. The startup configuration in `Program.cs` was organized so middleware executed in the correct order, making the application secure and easier to maintain. 

---

## Q2. What would happen if Authentication and Authorization middleware were registered in the wrong order?

### Answer

If `UseAuthorization()` is placed before `UseAuthentication()`, authorization executes before the user has been identified. As a result, the application cannot determine who the user is and access may be denied even for valid users. In our projects, we always configured authentication before authorization to ensure the user's identity was established before permission checks were performed. Following the correct middleware order prevented authentication-related issues during API execution. 

---

# ⭐ Rapid Fire Questions

1. What is middleware? → A component that processes HTTP requests and responses.
2. Where is middleware configured? → `Program.cs`
3. Which middleware redirects HTTP to HTTPS? → `UseHttpsRedirection()`
4. Which middleware authenticates users? → `UseAuthentication()`
5. Which middleware checks permissions? → `UseAuthorization()`
6. Which executes first, Authentication or Authorization? → Authentication
7. Can middleware stop the pipeline? → Yes
8. What is stopping the pipeline called? → Short-circuiting
9. Does every request pass through middleware? → Yes
10. Do responses also pass through middleware? → Yes, in reverse order.

---

# ⭐ Follow-up Questions

### Q1. Can we create our own middleware?

**Answer**

Yes. ASP.NET Core allows developers to create custom middleware by creating a class that accepts a `RequestDelegate` in its constructor and implements an `InvokeAsync()` method. Custom middleware is useful for logging, request validation, adding custom headers, measuring execution time, or implementing application-specific functionality. After creating it, the middleware is registered in `Program.cs` using `app.UseMiddleware<MiddlewareName>()`.

---

### Q2. What happens if middleware does not call the next middleware?

**Answer**

If middleware does not call the next middleware, the request pipeline stops at that point. The middleware can generate and return a response directly to the client. This behavior is known as **short-circuiting** and is commonly used for authentication failures, exception handling, static file serving, and custom validation logic.

---

# ⭐ Interview Tips

* Always explain that **every request passes through middleware before reaching the controller**.
* Remember the request and response flow:

  * **Request:** Top → Bottom
  * **Response:** Bottom → Top
* Mention **Authentication must come before Authorization** whenever discussing middleware order.
* Clearly differentiate **middleware (cross-cutting concerns)** from **controllers (business logic)**.
* Be ready to explain **short-circuiting**, as it is a common follow-up interview question.

---

# ⭐ Revision Notes

* Middleware processes every request and response.
* Middleware is configured in `Program.cs`.
* The request pipeline is a chain of middleware.
* Middleware executes in the order it is registered.
* Responses travel through the pipeline in reverse order.
* Middleware can execute code before and after the next middleware.
* Middleware can stop the pipeline (short-circuiting).
* Controllers handle business logic; middleware handles cross-cutting concerns.

---

# ⭐ Keywords

**Middleware, Request Pipeline, Response Pipeline, Program.cs, Use(), Authentication, Authorization, HTTPS Redirection, Routing, Short-Circuiting, Cross-Cutting Concerns, RequestDelegate, Custom Middleware**

---

# Module 2 – The Host & Request Pipeline

# Topic 4 – The Request Pipeline (Use, Map, Run) – Part 1 (Learning)

---

# 1. Introduction

In the previous topic, we learned that every HTTP request passes through a series of middleware components before reaching the controller. This sequence is called the **Request Pipeline**.

But how do we build this pipeline?

ASP.NET Core provides three important extension methods:

* **Use()**
* **Map()**
* **Run()**

These methods determine **how requests move through the pipeline**, **whether they continue**, **whether they branch into another pipeline**, or **whether they stop completely**.

Understanding these three methods is essential because almost every ASP.NET Core application uses them.

> **Interview Tip:** A common interview question is **"What is the difference between Use(), Map(), and Run()?"**

---

# 2. Definition

The **Request Pipeline** is the sequence of middleware that processes an incoming HTTP request and generates an HTTP response.

The methods **Use()**, **Map()**, and **Run()** are used to build and control this pipeline.

### One-Line Interview Definition

> The Request Pipeline is the ordered sequence of middleware that processes HTTP requests, and it is built using methods such as `Use()`, `Map()`, and `Run()`.

---

# 3. Why Do We Need the Request Pipeline?

Without a request pipeline:

* Requests cannot be processed.
* Middleware cannot execute.
* Authentication cannot happen.
* Authorization cannot happen.
* Routing cannot work.
* Controllers cannot be reached.

The request pipeline provides:

* Organized request processing
* Middleware execution
* Request validation
* Response generation
* Better maintainability
* Extensibility

---

# 4. How It Works

```text
Client
   │
HTTP Request
   │
Use()
   │
Use()
   │
Use()
   │
MapControllers()
   │
Controller
   │
HTTP Response
   │
Client
```

Each middleware decides whether:

* Continue to the next middleware
* Return a response immediately
* Branch into another pipeline

---

# 5. Key Concepts

## 1. What is Use()?

`Use()` adds middleware to the request pipeline.

It allows the request to continue to the next middleware by calling `next()`.

General Syntax

```csharp
app.Use(async (context, next) =>
{
    // Before next middleware

    await next();

    // After next middleware
});
```

### Flow

```text
Request
   │
Before
   │
Next Middleware
   │
After
   │
Response
```

### Example

```csharp
app.Use(async (context, next) =>
{
    Console.WriteLine("Before Request");

    await next();

    Console.WriteLine("After Response");
});
```

### Output

```text
Before Request

Controller Executes

After Response
```

### When Do We Use Use()?

Common examples

```csharp
app.UseHttpsRedirection();

app.UseAuthentication();

app.UseAuthorization();

app.UseCors();

app.UseStaticFiles();
```

Almost every middleware in ASP.NET Core is registered using `Use()`.

---

## 2. What is Run()?

`Run()` adds **terminal middleware**.

A terminal middleware **does not call the next middleware**.

It immediately returns the response.

Syntax

```csharp
app.Run(async context =>
{
    await context.Response.WriteAsync("Hello ASP.NET Core");
});
```

### Flow

```text
Request
    │
Run()
    │
Response Returned
```

The request stops here.

Nothing after `Run()` executes.

---

### Example

```csharp
app.Run(async context =>
{
    await context.Response.WriteAsync("Pipeline End");
});

app.UseAuthentication();
```

Output

```text
Pipeline End
```

`UseAuthentication()` never executes because `Run()` already ended the pipeline.

---

## 3. What is Map()?

`Map()` creates a **branch** in the request pipeline.

Instead of processing every request, it processes only requests matching a specific path.

Syntax

```csharp
app.Map("/admin", adminApp =>
{
    adminApp.Run(async context =>
    {
        await context.Response.WriteAsync("Admin Page");
    });
});
```

---

### Request

```text
GET /admin
```

Output

```text
Admin Page
```

---

### Request

```text
GET /employee
```

The `/admin` branch is skipped because the URL doesn't match.

---

### Flow

```text
Incoming Request
       │
Is URL /admin?
      / \
    Yes  No
    │     │
Admin   Main Pipeline
Branch
```

---

## 4. Difference Between Use(), Map(), and Run()

| Feature               | Use()      | Map()         | Run()          |
| --------------------- | ---------- | ------------- | -------------- |
| Adds Middleware       | ✅          | ❌             | ❌              |
| Creates Branch        | ❌          | ✅             | ❌              |
| Stops Pipeline        | ❌          | Usually No    | ✅ Always       |
| Calls Next Middleware | ✅          | Depends       | ❌              |
| Common Use            | Middleware | URL Branching | Final Response |

---

## 5. Execution Flow Using Use()

Example

```csharp
app.Use(async (context, next) =>
{
    Console.WriteLine("Middleware A");

    await next();

    Console.WriteLine("Middleware A End");
});

app.Use(async (context, next) =>
{
    Console.WriteLine("Middleware B");

    await next();

    Console.WriteLine("Middleware B End");
});

app.Run(async context =>
{
    Console.WriteLine("Controller");

    await context.Response.WriteAsync("Done");
});
```

Execution

```text
Middleware A

Middleware B

Controller

Middleware B End

Middleware A End
```

Notice the response travels in reverse order.

---

## 6. Real ASP.NET Core Request Pipeline

```csharp
app.UseHttpsRedirection();

app.UseRouting();

app.UseAuthentication();

app.UseAuthorization();

app.MapControllers();

app.Run();
```

Flow

```text
Client
   │
HTTPS
   │
Routing
   │
Authentication
   │
Authorization
   │
Controller
   │
Response
```

This is the pipeline you'll commonly see in ASP.NET Core Web API projects.

---

## 7. Choosing the Right Method

Use **Use()** when:

* You want middleware to execute before and after the next middleware.
* The request should continue through the pipeline.

Use **Map()** when:

* You want different pipelines for different URL paths.
* For example, `/admin`, `/api`, or `/health`.

Use **Run()** when:

* You want to end the pipeline.
* No further middleware should execute.

---

# 6. Code Example

```csharp
var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args);

var app = builder.Build();

app.Use(async (context, next) =>
{
    Console.WriteLine("Before");

    await next();

    Console.WriteLine("After");
});

app.Run(async context =>
{
    await context.Response.WriteAsync("Hello ASP.NET Core");
});

app.Run();
```

---

## Code Explanation

* `Use()` adds middleware to the pipeline.
* `await next()` passes the request to the next middleware.
* Code after `await next()` executes while the response returns.
* `Run()` is terminal middleware.
* Once `Run()` executes, the pipeline ends.
* The final `app.Run()` starts the web application and begins listening for requests. **Note:** This `app.Run()` is different from the terminal middleware overload above—it starts the host itself.

---

# 7. Real Project Usage ⭐

In my ASP.NET Core projects, the request pipeline was configured in `Program.cs` using middleware such as HTTPS redirection, authentication, authorization, and controller mapping. Authentication and authorization were placed in the correct order so secured APIs worked properly. Controller endpoints were mapped after middleware configuration, ensuring every request was processed correctly before reaching the business logic. 

---

# 8. Best Practices

* Register middleware in the correct order.
* Use `Use()` for reusable middleware.
* Use `Map()` when different URL paths require different processing.
* Use `Run()` only as terminal middleware.
* Keep the pipeline simple and readable.
* Group related middleware together.
* Test the pipeline after changing middleware order.

---

# 9. Common Mistakes

* Calling `Run()` too early in the pipeline.
* Forgetting `await next()` inside `Use()` middleware.
* Confusing `Map()` with routing.
* Registering authentication after authorization.
* Assuming `Map()` executes for every request.
* Writing business logic inside middleware.

---

# 10. Key Points to Remember

* The request pipeline processes every HTTP request.
* `Use()` adds middleware and normally continues the pipeline.
* `Map()` creates a separate pipeline branch for matching paths.
* `Run()` is terminal middleware and ends the pipeline.
* Middleware order affects application behavior.
* Responses travel back through middleware in reverse order.
* `app.Run()` (host startup) is different from `Run(RequestDelegate)` (terminal middleware).

---

# 11. Summary

The Request Pipeline is the core mechanism through which ASP.NET Core processes HTTP requests. It is built using **Use()**, **Map()**, and **Run()**. `Use()` adds middleware that can execute before and after the next middleware, `Map()` creates separate branches for specific URL paths, and `Run()` creates terminal middleware that ends the pipeline. Understanding the purpose and behavior of these methods is essential for building secure, maintainable, and efficient ASP.NET Core applications.

---

**Next:** **Part 2 – Interview Preparation**

* **6–8 Interview Questions** (10–12 line answers each)
* **2–3 Project-Based Interview Questions**
* **8–10 Rapid Fire Questions**
* **2–3 Follow-up Questions**
* **Interview Tips**
* **Revision Notes**
* **Keywords**

# Module 2 – The Host & Request Pipeline

# Topic 4 – The Request Pipeline (Use, Map, Run) – Part 2 (Interview Preparation)

---

# ⭐ Possible Interview Questions

## Q1. What is the Request Pipeline in ASP.NET Core?

### Answer

The Request Pipeline is the sequence of middleware that processes every incoming HTTP request in an ASP.NET Core application. When a client sends a request, it enters the pipeline and passes through multiple middleware components one by one. Each middleware can inspect, modify, or validate the request before passing it to the next middleware. Eventually, the request reaches the appropriate controller or endpoint. After the controller generates a response, the response travels back through the same middleware in reverse order before being sent to the client. The pipeline is configured in `Program.cs` using methods such as `Use()`, `Map()`, and `Run()`. The order of middleware in the pipeline is very important because each middleware depends on the previous one. A correctly configured request pipeline ensures proper authentication, authorization, routing, exception handling, and response generation.

---

## Q2. Explain the difference between `Use()`, `Map()`, and `Run()`.

### Answer

`Use()`, `Map()`, and `Run()` are methods used to build the ASP.NET Core request pipeline, but each has a different purpose. `Use()` adds middleware to the pipeline and normally calls the next middleware using `await next()`, allowing both request and response processing. `Map()` creates a separate branch in the pipeline based on the request path, such as `/admin` or `/health`. Only requests that match the specified path execute that branch. `Run()` creates terminal middleware that immediately generates a response and ends the pipeline without calling the next middleware. After `Run()` executes, no additional middleware is processed. Choosing the correct method depends on whether you want to continue the pipeline, branch it, or stop it completely.

---

## Q3. What is the purpose of `Use()`?

### Answer

`Use()` is used to add middleware to the ASP.NET Core request pipeline. Middleware registered using `Use()` can execute code before passing the request to the next middleware and also after the next middleware completes. This allows developers to inspect requests, modify responses, log execution time, or perform authentication and authorization. The request continues through the pipeline only when `await next()` is called. If `next()` is not called, the request stops at that middleware. Most built-in middleware, such as `UseAuthentication()`, `UseAuthorization()`, `UseCors()`, and `UseHttpsRedirection()`, are registered using `Use()`. It is the most commonly used method for building the request pipeline.

---

## Q4. What is `Map()` and when would you use it?

### Answer

`Map()` is used to create a separate branch in the request pipeline based on a specific URL path. When a request matches the specified path, ASP.NET Core executes the middleware inside that branch. If the path does not match, the request continues through the main pipeline. This is useful when different sections of an application require different middleware or behavior. For example, an `/admin` area may have its own middleware, while `/api` requests continue through the normal API pipeline. `Map()` helps organize large applications by separating different request flows without affecting the rest of the application. It improves maintainability and readability when different URL paths require different processing.

---

## Q5. What is `Run()` in ASP.NET Core?

### Answer

`Run()` is used to create terminal middleware in the ASP.NET Core request pipeline. Terminal middleware immediately generates a response and does not call any additional middleware. Once `Run()` executes, the request pipeline ends, and no further processing occurs. It is commonly used to return a simple response or to create the final middleware in a custom pipeline. Developers should be careful not to place `Run()` too early because any middleware registered after it will never execute. There is also another overload of `app.Run()` without parameters, which starts the web application itself. Understanding the difference between these two usages is important during interviews.

---

## Q6. What happens if `Run()` is placed before other middleware?

### Answer

If terminal `Run()` middleware is placed before other middleware, it immediately handles the request and returns a response to the client. Since it does not call the next middleware, the remaining middleware in the pipeline is skipped completely. This means authentication, authorization, routing, or controller execution will never occur. As a result, the application may not behave as expected. Developers should place terminal middleware only when they intentionally want to stop the pipeline. Proper middleware ordering ensures that all required processing occurs before the response is returned. This is why `Run()` should be used carefully in real applications.

---

## Q7. How does the request and response flow through the pipeline?

### Answer

When a client sends an HTTP request, it enters the ASP.NET Core request pipeline and passes through middleware in the order they are registered. Each middleware performs its task and usually calls the next middleware. Eventually, the request reaches the controller or endpoint where the business logic executes. After the controller returns a response, the response travels back through the same middleware in reverse order. This allows middleware to perform work both before and after the controller executes, such as logging execution time or modifying response headers. This two-way execution is one of the key features of ASP.NET Core middleware. Understanding this request-response flow is essential for configuring middleware correctly.

---

# ⭐ Project-Based Interview Questions

## Q1. How was the request pipeline configured in your project?

### Answer

In my ASP.NET Core projects, the request pipeline was configured in `Program.cs`. We registered middleware for HTTPS redirection, authentication, authorization, and then mapped controller endpoints. The middleware order was carefully maintained so that users were authenticated before authorization checks occurred. Controllers only handled business logic because common functionality such as security and request processing was handled by middleware. This approach kept the application organized, secure, and easier to maintain as the project grew. 

---

## Q2. Have you ever faced an issue because of middleware order?

### Answer

Yes. During development, an incorrectly configured middleware order can cause APIs to fail unexpectedly. For example, if authorization is placed before authentication, authorized users may still receive unauthorized responses because their identity has not yet been established. In our projects, we always followed Microsoft's recommended middleware order and verified the request pipeline after making changes. This prevented security and routing issues during development. 

---

# ⭐ Rapid Fire Questions

1. What builds the request pipeline? → `Use()`, `Map()`, and `Run()`
2. Which method adds middleware? → `Use()`
3. Which method creates a branch? → `Map()`
4. Which method creates terminal middleware? → `Run()`
5. Does `Use()` normally call the next middleware? → Yes
6. Does `Run()` call the next middleware? → No
7. Can `Map()` create a separate pipeline? → Yes
8. In which file is the pipeline configured? → `Program.cs`
9. Do responses travel in reverse order? → Yes
10. Is middleware order important? → Yes

---

# ⭐ Follow-up Questions

### Q1. Can multiple `Map()` methods exist in one application?

**Answer**

Yes. An ASP.NET Core application can have multiple `Map()` branches for different URL paths such as `/admin`, `/api`, `/health`, or `/reports`. Each branch can have its own middleware pipeline and request handling logic. This helps organize applications with different functional areas while keeping the main pipeline clean and maintainable.

---

### Q2. What is the difference between `app.Run()` and `Run(RequestDelegate)`?

**Answer**

`Run(RequestDelegate)` creates terminal middleware that handles a request and stops the pipeline. On the other hand, `app.Run()` without parameters starts the ASP.NET Core application and begins listening for incoming HTTP requests using the configured web server. Although both use the name `Run`, they serve different purposes and should not be confused.

---

# ⭐ Interview Tips

* Remember the comparison:

  * **Use()** → Continue the pipeline.
  * **Map()** → Create a branch.
  * **Run()** → End the pipeline.
* Explain that middleware executes **top to bottom** for requests and **bottom to top** for responses.
* Mention the difference between **terminal `Run()` middleware** and **`app.Run()` that starts the application**—this is a favorite interview question.
* Give a practical example, such as an `/admin` branch with `Map()`.

---

# ⭐ Revision Notes

* Request Pipeline processes every HTTP request.
* `Use()` adds middleware and usually continues the pipeline.
* `Map()` creates separate pipelines for matching URL paths.
* `Run()` creates terminal middleware.
* `app.Run()` starts the application.
* Request flows top → bottom.
* Response flows bottom → top.
* Middleware order directly affects application behavior.

---

# ⭐ Keywords

**Request Pipeline, Use(), Map(), Run(), Terminal Middleware, Branch Pipeline, RequestDelegate, Program.cs, Middleware Order, Request Flow, Response Flow, ASP.NET Core Pipeline**

---

# Module 2 – The Host & Request Pipeline

# Topic 5 – Built-in Middleware (Routing, Authentication, Authorization, Static Files, HTTPS Redirection, CORS, etc.) – Part 1 (Learning)

---

# 1. Introduction

ASP.NET Core provides many **built-in middleware** that perform common tasks required by almost every web application and Web API.

Instead of writing code for tasks like:

* Routing requests
* Authenticating users
* Checking permissions
* Redirecting HTTP to HTTPS
* Serving static files
* Allowing cross-origin requests
* Handling exceptions

ASP.NET Core provides ready-to-use middleware.

Each middleware has a **single responsibility**, and together they form the **Request Pipeline**.

> **Interview Tip:** Most ASP.NET Core interviews ask about the purpose of built-in middleware and their execution order.

---

# 2. Definition

Built-in Middleware are pre-built middleware components provided by ASP.NET Core that perform common request-processing tasks without requiring developers to write custom middleware.

### One-Line Interview Definition

> Built-in middleware are ready-made components provided by ASP.NET Core to handle common HTTP request and response processing tasks.

---

# 3. Why Do We Need Built-in Middleware?

Imagine building every application from scratch.

For every request you would need to:

* Check if HTTPS is used.
* Authenticate the user.
* Verify permissions.
* Find the requested endpoint.
* Handle exceptions.
* Serve images and CSS files.
* Allow frontend applications from another domain.

Writing this logic repeatedly would make applications complex.

Built-in middleware provides:

* Reusable functionality
* Better security
* Better performance
* Cleaner code
* Easy configuration
* Standard implementation

---

# 4. How It Works

Example pipeline

```text
Client
   │
HTTP Request
   │
UseHttpsRedirection()
   │
UseRouting()
   │
UseCors()
   │
UseAuthentication()
   │
UseAuthorization()
   │
MapControllers()
   │
Controller
   │
Response
   │
Client
```

Each middleware performs its own task before passing the request to the next middleware.

---

# 5. Key Concepts

---

# 1. HTTPS Redirection Middleware

### What is it?

This middleware automatically redirects HTTP requests to HTTPS.

Example

```text
http://localhost:5000/api/employees
```

↓

```text
https://localhost:5001/api/employees
```

---

### Why do we need it?

HTTPS provides

* Encryption
* Secure communication
* Data protection
* Prevents data interception

---

### Code

```csharp
app.UseHttpsRedirection();
```

---

### Request Flow

```text
HTTP Request
      │
HTTPS Middleware
      │
Redirect to HTTPS
      │
Continue Pipeline
```

---

### Real Project Usage

Almost every production ASP.NET Core application enables HTTPS to secure API communication between clients and servers.

---

# 2. Routing Middleware

### What is it?

Routing middleware finds the endpoint that matches the incoming request.

Example

Request

```text
GET /api/products
```

Routing finds

```text
ProductsController

↓

GetProducts()
```

---

### Code

```csharp
app.UseRouting();
```

---

### Responsibilities

* Reads URL
* Matches routes
* Finds endpoint
* Stores endpoint information

---

### Real Project Usage

Routing middleware directs requests to the correct API controller based on the request URL and HTTP method.

---

# 3. Authentication Middleware

### What is Authentication?

Authentication verifies **who the user is**.

It answers the question:

> "Who are you?"

---

Example

User logs in with

* Username
* Password
* JWT Token
* Cookie

Authentication validates the identity.

---

### Code

```csharp
app.UseAuthentication();
```

---

### Flow

```text
Client
   │
JWT Token
   │
Authentication
   │
Valid?
 /     \
Yes     No
 │       │
Continue 401 Unauthorized
```

---

### Real Project Usage

In secured APIs, authentication middleware validates JWT tokens before allowing requests to access protected resources.

---

# 4. Authorization Middleware

### What is Authorization?

Authorization determines **what the authenticated user is allowed to do**.

Question answered:

> "What can you access?"

---

Example

Authenticated User

↓

Role = Admin

↓

Access Admin Dashboard

---

Example

Authenticated User

↓

Role = Employee

↓

Cannot access Admin APIs

---

### Code

```csharp
app.UseAuthorization();
```

---

### Important Rule

Authentication must execute **before** Authorization.

Correct

```csharp
app.UseAuthentication();

app.UseAuthorization();
```

Wrong

```csharp
app.UseAuthorization();

app.UseAuthentication();
```

---

### Real Project Usage

Authorization middleware checks user roles or policies before allowing access to secured API endpoints.

---

# Authentication vs Authorization

| Authentication    | Authorization        |
| ----------------- | -------------------- |
| Verifies identity | Verifies permissions |
| "Who are you?"    | "What can you do?"   |
| Happens first     | Happens second       |
| Example: Login    | Example: Role check  |

---

# 5. Static Files Middleware

### What is it?

Static File Middleware serves files directly to the client without using a controller.

Examples

* Images
* CSS
* JavaScript
* HTML
* Fonts

---

### Code

```csharp
app.UseStaticFiles();
```

---

### Flow

```text
Request Image
      │
Static File Middleware
      │
File Exists?
 /       \
Yes       No
 │         │
Return    Continue
Image     Pipeline
```

---

### Example

Browser requests

```text
/images/logo.png
```

Static Files Middleware returns the image directly.

No controller executes.

---

### Real Project Usage

Used in MVC applications to serve logos, stylesheets, JavaScript files, and uploaded documents efficiently.

---

# 6. CORS Middleware

### What is CORS?

CORS stands for

**Cross-Origin Resource Sharing**

It controls whether another domain is allowed to access your API.

---

Example

Frontend

```text
http://localhost:3000
```

API

```text
https://localhost:5001
```

Different origins.

Without CORS

↓

Browser blocks request.

---

### Code

```csharp
app.UseCors();
```

---

### Flow

```text
Browser
    │
API Request
    │
CORS Middleware
    │
Allowed?
 /      \
Yes      No
 │        │
Continue 403 / Blocked
```

---

### Real Project Usage

React, Angular, and Vue applications commonly use CORS middleware to communicate securely with ASP.NET Core Web APIs hosted on another domain or port.

---

# 7. Exception Handling Middleware

### What is it?

This middleware catches unhandled exceptions and returns a proper error response instead of crashing the application.

---

### Code

```csharp
app.UseExceptionHandler("/error");
```

---

### Example

Without middleware

```text
Application Crash
```

With middleware

```text
500 Internal Server Error
```

A proper error response is returned.

---

### Real Project Usage

Used to log unexpected exceptions and return consistent error responses to API consumers without exposing sensitive application details.

---

# Typical Middleware Order

```csharp
app.UseHttpsRedirection();

app.UseRouting();

app.UseCors();

app.UseAuthentication();

app.UseAuthorization();

app.MapControllers();

app.Run();
```

---

# Why This Order?

| Middleware     | Reason                         |
| -------------- | ------------------------------ |
| HTTPS          | Secure requests first          |
| Routing        | Find endpoint                  |
| CORS           | Validate cross-origin requests |
| Authentication | Identify the user              |
| Authorization  | Check permissions              |
| Controllers    | Execute business logic         |

---

# 6. Code Example

```csharp
var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args);

builder.Services.AddControllers();

var app = builder.Build();

app.UseHttpsRedirection();

app.UseRouting();

app.UseCors();

app.UseAuthentication();

app.UseAuthorization();

app.MapControllers();

app.Run();
```

---

## Code Explanation

```csharp
app.UseHttpsRedirection();
```

Redirects HTTP requests to HTTPS.

---

```csharp
app.UseRouting();
```

Matches incoming requests to registered endpoints.

---

```csharp
app.UseCors();
```

Checks whether cross-origin requests are allowed.

---

```csharp
app.UseAuthentication();
```

Authenticates the user using the configured authentication scheme.

---

```csharp
app.UseAuthorization();
```

Verifies that the authenticated user has permission to access the requested endpoint.

---

```csharp
app.MapControllers();
```

Maps controller actions as endpoints.

---

```csharp
app.Run();
```

Starts the application and begins listening for incoming requests.

---

# 7. Real Project Usage ⭐

In my ASP.NET Core Web API projects, built-in middleware was configured in `Program.cs` to handle common request processing. HTTPS redirection secured API communication, routing matched incoming requests to controller actions, authentication validated JWT tokens, authorization enforced role-based access, and CORS allowed frontend applications to communicate with the APIs. This configuration kept controllers focused on business logic while middleware handled cross-cutting concerns. 

---

# 8. Best Practices

* Register middleware in the recommended order.
* Enable HTTPS in production.
* Always authenticate before authorizing users.
* Configure CORS with only trusted origins.
* Use built-in middleware instead of custom implementations when possible.
* Keep middleware responsibilities focused on a single task.
* Test middleware configuration after deployment.

---

# 9. Common Mistakes

* Registering `UseAuthorization()` before `UseAuthentication()`.
* Forgetting to enable HTTPS.
* Using overly permissive CORS settings (e.g., allowing all origins in production).
* Forgetting `MapControllers()`.
* Assuming Static File Middleware serves files outside the configured static file locations.
* Changing middleware order without understanding its impact.

---

# 10. Key Points to Remember

* Built-in middleware is provided by ASP.NET Core.
* Each middleware has one specific responsibility.
* Middleware executes in the order it is registered.
* Authentication identifies the user.
* Authorization checks permissions.
* Routing selects the endpoint.
* CORS controls cross-origin requests.
* Static File Middleware serves files directly.
* HTTPS middleware secures communication.
* Exception Handling Middleware provides centralized error handling.

---

# 11. Summary

Built-in middleware simplifies application development by providing ready-made components for common request-processing tasks. Middleware such as **HTTPS Redirection**, **Routing**, **Authentication**, **Authorization**, **Static Files**, **CORS**, and **Exception Handling** work together to process requests securely and efficiently. Configuring them in the correct order is essential for a secure, maintainable, and reliable ASP.NET Core application.

---

**Next:** **Part 2 – Interview Preparation** (6–8 interview questions with 10–12 line answers, project-based questions, rapid fire, follow-up questions, interview tips, revision notes, and keywords).

---
# Module 2 – The Host & Request Pipeline

# Topic 5 – Built-in Middleware (Routing, Authentication, Authorization, Static Files, HTTPS Redirection, CORS, etc.) – Part 2 (Interview Preparation)

---

# ⭐ Possible Interview Questions

## Q1. What is Built-in Middleware in ASP.NET Core?

### Answer

Built-in middleware are ready-made components provided by ASP.NET Core to handle common HTTP request and response processing tasks. Instead of writing code from scratch, developers can use middleware such as HTTPS Redirection, Routing, Authentication, Authorization, Static Files, CORS, and Exception Handling. These middleware are added to the request pipeline in `Program.cs`. Each middleware has a specific responsibility and processes the request before passing it to the next middleware. They help reduce code duplication, improve security, and make applications easier to maintain. Since they are provided by Microsoft, they are optimized and follow best practices. Most ASP.NET Core applications rely on built-in middleware for request processing.

---

## Q2. What is the purpose of `UseRouting()` middleware?

### Answer

`UseRouting()` enables the routing system in ASP.NET Core. When a request reaches the application, this middleware examines the request URL and HTTP method to find the matching endpoint. It does not execute the endpoint itself; instead, it identifies the correct controller action or Minimal API and stores that information for later middleware. If no matching endpoint is found, the request eventually returns a **404 Not Found** response. Routing middleware is an essential part of the request pipeline because it connects incoming requests to the appropriate endpoint. It is typically registered before authentication and authorization middleware. Without `UseRouting()`, ASP.NET Core cannot determine where the request should go.

---

## Q3. What is the difference between Authentication and Authorization?

### Answer

Authentication and Authorization serve different purposes in an ASP.NET Core application. Authentication verifies the identity of the user and answers the question, **"Who are you?"** It validates credentials such as JWT tokens, cookies, or login information. Authorization checks what the authenticated user is allowed to access and answers the question, **"What are you allowed to do?"** Authentication always occurs before Authorization. For example, a user must first be identified before the application can determine whether they have permission to access an admin API. Together, these middleware provide application security and protect sensitive resources.

---

## Q4. Why should `UseAuthentication()` come before `UseAuthorization()`?

### Answer

`UseAuthentication()` must execute before `UseAuthorization()` because the application must first identify the user before checking permissions. Authentication validates the user's identity and creates a `ClaimsPrincipal` containing user information and roles. Authorization then uses this information to determine whether the user has access to the requested resource. If authorization runs first, no authenticated user is available, and access may be denied incorrectly. This is why Microsoft's recommended middleware order always places Authentication before Authorization. Following the correct order ensures secure and predictable request processing.

---

## Q5. What is CORS and why is it required?

### Answer

CORS stands for **Cross-Origin Resource Sharing**. It is a security feature that controls whether a web application running on one origin can access resources hosted on another origin. For example, a React application running on `http://localhost:3000` may need to call an ASP.NET Core API running on `https://localhost:5001`. Without CORS, the browser blocks the request for security reasons. CORS middleware allows developers to specify which origins, HTTP methods, and headers are permitted. Proper CORS configuration enables secure communication between frontend applications and backend APIs. It is commonly used in modern SPA applications.

---

## Q6. What is Static File Middleware?

### Answer

Static File Middleware is responsible for serving static files directly to clients without involving controllers. Static files include images, CSS files, JavaScript files, fonts, and HTML pages. When a request is made for a static file, the middleware checks whether the file exists in the configured location, such as the `wwwroot` folder. If the file is found, it is returned immediately. If not, the request continues to the next middleware. Serving static files through middleware improves performance because controller execution is skipped. This middleware is enabled using `app.UseStaticFiles()`.

---

## Q7. Why do we use HTTPS Redirection Middleware?

### Answer

HTTPS Redirection Middleware automatically redirects HTTP requests to HTTPS. HTTPS encrypts data exchanged between the client and server, protecting sensitive information such as passwords, tokens, and personal data from interception. By forcing secure communication, the application reduces the risk of security attacks like man-in-the-middle attacks. The middleware is enabled using `app.UseHttpsRedirection()`. It is usually placed near the beginning of the request pipeline so that all subsequent communication occurs over a secure connection. Using HTTPS is considered a best practice for all production applications.

---

# ⭐ Project-Based Interview Questions

## Q1. Which built-in middleware did you use in your project?

### Answer

In my ASP.NET Core Web API projects, we configured built-in middleware in `Program.cs`. We used HTTPS Redirection to secure communication, Routing to match incoming requests, Authentication to validate JWT tokens, Authorization to enforce role-based access, and CORS to allow frontend applications to access the APIs. Controller endpoints were registered using `MapControllers()`. This configuration ensured that common request-processing tasks were handled before the request reached the business logic. 

---

## Q2. Why is middleware order important in real projects?

### Answer

Middleware executes in the same order in which it is registered. In our projects, we followed the recommended order so requests were processed correctly. For example, HTTPS Redirection executed first, Routing identified the endpoint, Authentication verified the user, Authorization checked permissions, and finally the controller handled the request. An incorrect order could cause authentication failures, authorization issues, or routing problems. Therefore, maintaining the correct middleware sequence was essential for application security and reliability. 

---

# ⭐ Rapid Fire Questions

1. What are Built-in Middleware? → Ready-made middleware provided by ASP.NET Core.
2. Which middleware enables routing? → `UseRouting()`
3. Which middleware authenticates users? → `UseAuthentication()`
4. Which middleware checks user permissions? → `UseAuthorization()`
5. Which middleware serves images and CSS files? → `UseStaticFiles()`
6. Which middleware redirects HTTP to HTTPS? → `UseHttpsRedirection()`
7. What does CORS stand for? → Cross-Origin Resource Sharing.
8. Which middleware handles unhandled exceptions? → `UseExceptionHandler()`
9. Should Authentication come before Authorization? → Yes.
10. Where is middleware configured? → `Program.cs`

---

# ⭐ Follow-up Questions

## Q1. Can we use only the middleware we need?

### Answer

Yes. ASP.NET Core allows developers to register only the middleware required by the application. For example, a Web API may use Routing, Authentication, Authorization, HTTPS Redirection, and CORS, while an MVC application may also use Static File Middleware. Registering only the required middleware keeps the request pipeline efficient and avoids unnecessary processing.

---

## Q2. Does every request pass through all middleware?

### Answer

Not always. A request passes through middleware in the order it is registered, but some middleware can stop the pipeline. For example, Authentication Middleware can return **401 Unauthorized**, or Static File Middleware can directly return an image file. This behavior is called **short-circuiting**, where the remaining middleware is skipped.

---

# ⭐ Interview Tips

* Remember the recommended middleware order:

  1. HTTPS Redirection
  2. Routing
  3. CORS
  4. Authentication
  5. Authorization
  6. MapControllers()
* Explain **Authentication = Identity** and **Authorization = Permission**.
* Mention that **Static File Middleware** returns files without calling controllers.
* Explain that **CORS** is mainly required when the frontend and backend run on different origins.
* State that built-in middleware is preferred over custom middleware whenever it satisfies the application's requirements.

---

# ⭐ Revision Notes

* Built-in middleware is provided by ASP.NET Core.
* Each middleware has a single responsibility.
* Middleware executes in registration order.
* `UseRouting()` matches requests to endpoints.
* `UseAuthentication()` verifies user identity.
* `UseAuthorization()` checks access permissions.
* `UseStaticFiles()` serves static content.
* `UseHttpsRedirection()` enforces secure communication.
* `UseCors()` enables controlled cross-origin access.
* `UseExceptionHandler()` provides centralized exception handling.

---

# ⭐ Keywords

**Built-in Middleware, UseRouting(), UseAuthentication(), UseAuthorization(), UseStaticFiles(), UseHttpsRedirection(), UseCors(), UseExceptionHandler(), Request Pipeline, JWT Authentication, Role-Based Authorization, CORS, HTTPS**

---
This topic is **small enough**, so I'll cover it in **one response**.

# Module 2 – The Host & Request Pipeline

# Topic 6 – Writing Custom Middleware

---

# 1. Introduction

ASP.NET Core provides many **built-in middleware** such as Routing, Authentication, Authorization, CORS, and HTTPS Redirection. However, sometimes an application needs functionality that is specific to its business requirements.

For example:

* Logging every request
* Validating a custom header
* Measuring request execution time
* Adding custom response headers
* Tracking API usage
* Auditing requests

In such cases, we create **Custom Middleware**.

Custom middleware allows developers to execute their own code for every HTTP request before or after the next middleware in the pipeline.

> **Interview Tip:** Creating custom middleware is a common coding and interview topic for ASP.NET Core developers with 2+ years of experience.

---

# 2. Definition

**Custom Middleware** is a user-defined middleware component that processes HTTP requests and responses as they pass through the ASP.NET Core request pipeline.

### One-Line Interview Definition

> Custom Middleware is middleware created by developers to perform application-specific request and response processing.

---

# 3. Why Do We Need Custom Middleware?

Built-in middleware handles common tasks, but every project has unique requirements.

Examples:

* Log API execution time
* Validate custom API keys
* Add security headers
* Audit user activity
* Modify request or response data
* Track request IDs
* Perform custom exception logging

Instead of writing the same code in every controller, we write it once as middleware.

### Benefits

* Code reusability
* Centralized logic
* Cleaner controllers
* Easy maintenance
* Better separation of concerns

---

# 4. How It Works

```text
Client
   │
HTTP Request
   │
Custom Middleware
   │
Built-in Middleware
   │
Controller
   │
Response
   │
Custom Middleware
   │
Client
```

The middleware can:

* Execute code before the next middleware.
* Pass the request to the next middleware.
* Execute code after the response returns.
* Stop the pipeline if required.

---

# 5. Key Concepts

---

## 1. Components of Custom Middleware

A custom middleware contains:

* A constructor
* A `RequestDelegate`
* An `InvokeAsync()` method

---

## 2. RequestDelegate

`RequestDelegate` represents the next middleware in the pipeline.

Example

```csharp
private readonly RequestDelegate _next;
```

Without it, the request cannot continue to the next middleware.

---

## 3. Constructor

The constructor receives the next middleware.

```csharp
public LoggingMiddleware(RequestDelegate next)
{
    _next = next;
}
```

---

## 4. InvokeAsync()

Every custom middleware must contain an `InvokeAsync()` method.

```csharp
public async Task InvokeAsync(HttpContext context)
{
    await _next(context);
}
```

ASP.NET Core automatically calls this method for every request.

---

## 5. HttpContext

`HttpContext` contains information about the current HTTP request and response.

Using `HttpContext`, we can access:

* Request URL
* HTTP Method
* Headers
* Query String
* User Information
* Response Status Code

Example

```csharp
var url = context.Request.Path;

var method = context.Request.Method;
```

---

## 6. Complete Custom Middleware Example

```csharp
public class LoggingMiddleware
{
    private readonly RequestDelegate _next;

    public LoggingMiddleware(RequestDelegate next)
    {
        _next = next;
    }

    public async Task InvokeAsync(HttpContext context)
    {
        Console.WriteLine("Request Started");

        await _next(context);

        Console.WriteLine("Request Finished");
    }
}
```

---

## 7. Registering Custom Middleware

After creating the middleware, register it in `Program.cs`.

```csharp
app.UseMiddleware<LoggingMiddleware>();
```

Pipeline

```text
Client
   │
Logging Middleware
   │
Authentication
   │
Authorization
   │
Controller
```

---

## 8. Execution Flow

```text
Request

↓

Logging Middleware
(Before)

↓

Next Middleware

↓

Controller

↓

Next Middleware Ends

↓

Logging Middleware
(After)

↓

Response
```

Notice that the middleware executes both before and after the controller.

---

## 9. Custom Middleware vs Built-in Middleware

| Custom Middleware                            | Built-in Middleware               |
| -------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------- |
| Created by developer                         | Provided by ASP.NET Core          |
| Project-specific                             | Common functionality              |
| Uses `UseMiddleware<T>()`                    | Uses methods like `UseRouting()`  |
| Can implement any business-independent logic | Handles routing, auth, CORS, etc. |

---

## 10. Common Real-World Uses

* Request logging
* Response logging
* Performance monitoring
* API key validation
* Correlation ID generation
* Request auditing
* Custom exception handling
* Security header injection

---

# 6. Code Example

### LoggingMiddleware.cs

```csharp
public class LoggingMiddleware
{
    private readonly RequestDelegate _next;

    public LoggingMiddleware(RequestDelegate next)
    {
        _next = next;
    }

    public async Task InvokeAsync(HttpContext context)
    {
        Console.WriteLine($"Request: {context.Request.Method} {context.Request.Path}");

        await _next(context);

        Console.WriteLine($"Response Status: {context.Response.StatusCode}");
    }
}
```

### Program.cs

```csharp
var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args);

builder.Services.AddControllers();

var app = builder.Build();

app.UseMiddleware<LoggingMiddleware>();

app.UseAuthentication();

app.UseAuthorization();

app.MapControllers();

app.Run();
```

---

# 7. Code Explanation

```csharp
private readonly RequestDelegate _next;
```

Stores the reference to the next middleware.

---

```csharp
public LoggingMiddleware(RequestDelegate next)
```

Receives the next middleware from ASP.NET Core.

---

```csharp
public async Task InvokeAsync(HttpContext context)
```

This method executes for every incoming request.

---

```csharp
await _next(context);
```

Passes the request to the next middleware.

---

```csharp
Console.WriteLine(...)
```

Logs request and response information.

---

```csharp
app.UseMiddleware<LoggingMiddleware>();
```

Registers the custom middleware in the request pipeline.

---

# 8. Real Project Usage ⭐

In enterprise ASP.NET Core applications, custom middleware is commonly used for request logging, audit logging, correlation IDs, custom exception handling, and adding security-related response headers. Keeping these concerns in middleware allows controllers to focus only on business logic while common processing happens once for every request. This approach improves maintainability and keeps the application architecture clean.

---

# 9. Best Practices

* Keep middleware focused on one responsibility.
* Always call `await _next(context)` unless intentionally stopping the pipeline.
* Use dependency injection instead of creating services manually.
* Handle exceptions carefully.
* Register middleware in the correct order.
* Avoid placing business logic inside middleware.
* Use `ILogger<T>` instead of `Console.WriteLine()` in production.

---

# 10. Common Mistakes

* Forgetting to call `await _next(context)`.
* Writing business logic inside middleware.
* Registering middleware in the wrong order.
* Using `Console.WriteLine()` in production.
* Accessing `HttpContext` after the response has completed.
* Creating one middleware that performs multiple unrelated tasks.

---

# 11. Key Points to Remember

* Custom middleware is created by developers.
* Every middleware requires `RequestDelegate`.
* `InvokeAsync()` is the execution method.
* `HttpContext` contains request and response data.
* Register custom middleware using `UseMiddleware<T>()`.
* Middleware can execute before and after the next middleware.
* Keep middleware reusable and focused on a single responsibility.

---

# 12. Summary

Custom Middleware enables developers to add application-specific functionality to the ASP.NET Core request pipeline. It is created using a class that receives a `RequestDelegate` and implements an `InvokeAsync()` method. By using `HttpContext`, middleware can inspect or modify requests and responses, perform logging, validation, or auditing, and then pass control to the next middleware. Properly designed custom middleware keeps controllers clean, promotes code reuse, and makes ASP.NET Core applications easier to maintain.

---

# 13. Practice Coding Questions

1. Create a custom middleware that logs every request URL.
2. Create middleware to measure API execution time.
3. Create middleware that checks for a custom request header.
4. Create middleware that adds a custom response header.
5. Create middleware that logs the HTTP method and status code.

---

# 14. Possible Interview Questions and Answers (2 Years Experience)

### Q1. What is Custom Middleware?

**Answer:** Custom Middleware is middleware created by developers to handle application-specific tasks during request and response processing. It executes for every request, can perform operations before and after the next middleware, and is registered using `UseMiddleware<T>()`.

---

### Q2. What are the main components of Custom Middleware?

**Answer:** A custom middleware consists of a constructor that accepts `RequestDelegate`, an `InvokeAsync()` method that receives `HttpContext`, and registration in `Program.cs` using `UseMiddleware<T>()`.

---

### Q3. What is `RequestDelegate`?

**Answer:** `RequestDelegate` represents the next middleware in the request pipeline. Calling `await _next(context)` forwards the request to the next middleware. Without it, the pipeline stops.

---

### Q4. What is `HttpContext`?

**Answer:** `HttpContext` represents the current HTTP request and response. It provides access to request details, response details, headers, user information, cookies, session, and more.

---

### Q5. How do you register Custom Middleware?

**Answer:** Custom middleware is registered in `Program.cs` using `app.UseMiddleware<MiddlewareName>();`. Once registered, it becomes part of the request pipeline and executes for incoming requests.

---

### Q6. Can Custom Middleware stop the pipeline?

**Answer:** Yes. If the middleware does not call `await _next(context)`, it can return a response directly to the client and stop the remaining middleware from executing. This behavior is known as **short-circuiting**.

---

### Q7. Where have you used Custom Middleware in real projects?

**Answer:** Common use cases include request logging, response logging, correlation IDs, API key validation, audit logging, performance monitoring, custom exception handling, and adding security headers. These tasks are centralized in middleware so controllers remain focused on business logic.

---

### Q8. Why should business logic not be written inside middleware?

**Answer:** Middleware should handle cross-cutting concerns that apply to many requests. Business logic belongs in controllers and services because it is specific to application functionality. Keeping responsibilities separate improves maintainability and testability.

---

# 15. Interview Tips

* Remember the three essentials: **RequestDelegate**, **HttpContext**, and **InvokeAsync()**.
* Mention that middleware executes **before and after** the next middleware.
* Explain why `await _next(context)` is important.
* Use examples like **logging** or **API key validation** when describing real-world usage.

---

# 16. Revision Notes

* Custom Middleware = Developer-created middleware.
* Uses `RequestDelegate` to continue the pipeline.
* `InvokeAsync()` is the execution method.
* `HttpContext` provides request/response information.
* Register with `UseMiddleware<T>()`.
* Used for logging, auditing, validation, monitoring, and custom processing.

---

# 17. Keywords

**Custom Middleware, RequestDelegate, HttpContext, InvokeAsync(), UseMiddleware<T>(), Logging Middleware, Request Pipeline, Dependency Injection, Cross-Cutting Concerns, ASP.NET Core**

---
# Module 2 – The Host & Request Pipeline

# Topic 7 – Short-Circuiting the Pipeline

---

# 1. Introduction

In previous topics, we learned that an HTTP request normally passes through all middleware until it reaches the controller. However, this does **not** always happen.

Sometimes, a middleware determines that there is **no need to continue processing the request**. Instead, it immediately generates a response and sends it back to the client.

This behavior is called **Short-Circuiting the Pipeline**.

Short-circuiting improves performance, enhances security, and avoids unnecessary processing.

> **Interview Tip:** "What is Short-Circuiting?" is a common follow-up question after middleware or request pipeline questions.

---

# 2. Definition

**Short-Circuiting** is the process where a middleware stops the request pipeline by returning a response directly to the client without calling the next middleware.

### One-Line Interview Definition

> Short-Circuiting is when middleware ends the request pipeline early by returning a response without executing the remaining middleware.

---

# 3. Why Do We Need Short-Circuiting?

Without short-circuiting, every request would continue through all middleware and eventually reach the controller, even when it is unnecessary.

Examples:

* Invalid JWT token
* Unauthorized user
* Static file found
* Health check request
* Invalid API key
* Maintenance mode

### Benefits

* Improves performance
* Reduces unnecessary processing
* Enhances security
* Reduces server load
* Faster response time

---

# 4. How It Works

### Normal Request Flow

```text
Client
   │
Middleware A
   │
Middleware B
   │
Middleware C
   │
Controller
   │
Response
```

---

### Short-Circuit Request Flow

```text
Client
   │
Middleware A
   │
Middleware B
   │
Response Returned
   │
Client
```

Notice that:

* Middleware C does **not** execute.
* Controller does **not** execute.

The request ends immediately.

---

# 5. Key Concepts

---

## 1. How Does Short-Circuiting Happen?

Normally, middleware calls

```csharp
await _next(context);
```

to continue the pipeline.

If this line is **not** executed, the request stops.

Example

```csharp
if (true)
{
    await context.Response.WriteAsync("Request Blocked");
    return;
}

await _next(context);
```

The `return` statement ends the request.

---

## 2. Authentication Example

Suppose a request contains an invalid JWT token.

Pipeline

```text
Client
   │
Authentication Middleware
   │
Invalid Token
   │
401 Unauthorized
   │
Client
```

The request never reaches:

* Authorization
* Controller
* Database

---

## 3. Static File Middleware Example

Request

```text
/images/logo.png
```

Pipeline

```text
Client
   │
Static File Middleware
   │
File Found
   │
Image Returned
   │
Client
```

No routing or controller execution is required.

---

## 4. Custom API Key Validation

Example middleware

```csharp
public async Task InvokeAsync(HttpContext context)
{
    if (!context.Request.Headers.ContainsKey("ApiKey"))
    {
        context.Response.StatusCode = 401;

        await context.Response.WriteAsync("API Key Missing");

        return;
    }

    await _next(context);
}
```

If the API key is missing:

* Response = 401
* Pipeline stops

---

## 5. Maintenance Mode Example

Sometimes applications are under maintenance.

Example

```csharp
public async Task InvokeAsync(HttpContext context)
{
    bool maintenanceMode = true;

    if (maintenanceMode)
    {
        await context.Response.WriteAsync("Website Under Maintenance");

        return;
    }

    await _next(context);
}
```

Users immediately receive the maintenance message.

---

## 6. Health Check Example

A request like

```text
GET /health
```

can return

```text
Application Running
```

without executing authentication, authorization, or controllers.

---

## 7. Difference Between Normal Pipeline and Short-Circuiting

| Normal Pipeline          | Short-Circuit Pipeline        |
| ------------------------ | ----------------------------- |
| Executes all middleware  | Stops early                   |
| Reaches controller       | May not reach controller      |
| Calls `await _next()`    | Does not call `await _next()` |
| More processing          | Less processing               |
| Used for normal requests | Used for special conditions   |

---

## 8. Built-in Middleware That Can Short-Circuit

| Middleware        | When It Stops          |
| ----------------- | ---------------------- |
| Authentication    | Invalid credentials    |
| Authorization     | Access denied          |
| Static Files      | File found             |
| Exception Handler | Exception occurs       |
| HTTPS Redirection | Redirect response sent |

---

## 9. When Should We Use Short-Circuiting?

Use it when:

* Authentication fails
* Authorization fails
* API key is invalid
* Maintenance mode is enabled
* Static file is found
* Invalid request detected
* Health check endpoint
* Request should be blocked immediately

---

# 6. Code Example

### Custom Middleware

```csharp
public class ApiKeyMiddleware
{
    private readonly RequestDelegate _next;

    public ApiKeyMiddleware(RequestDelegate next)
    {
        _next = next;
    }

    public async Task InvokeAsync(HttpContext context)
    {
        if (!context.Request.Headers.ContainsKey("ApiKey"))
        {
            context.Response.StatusCode = 401;

            await context.Response.WriteAsync("API Key Required");

            return;
        }

        await _next(context);
    }
}
```

### Register Middleware

```csharp
app.UseMiddleware<ApiKeyMiddleware>();

app.MapControllers();
```

---

# 7. Code Explanation

```csharp
if (!context.Request.Headers.ContainsKey("ApiKey"))
```

Checks whether the request contains the required API key.

---

```csharp
context.Response.StatusCode = 401;
```

Sets the response status to **401 Unauthorized**.

---

```csharp
await context.Response.WriteAsync("API Key Required");
```

Returns an error message to the client.

---

```csharp
return;
```

Stops the request pipeline immediately.

---

```csharp
await _next(context);
```

Executes only when the API key exists.

---

# 8. Real Project Usage ⭐

In enterprise ASP.NET Core applications, short-circuiting is commonly used to reject unauthorized requests before they reach the business logic. Middleware can validate authentication tokens, check custom headers, verify API keys, or block requests during maintenance mode. This approach improves security and reduces unnecessary processing because invalid requests are rejected early while valid requests continue through the normal pipeline.

---

# 9. Best Practices

* Short-circuit only when necessary.
* Return appropriate HTTP status codes (401, 403, 404, 503, etc.).
* Return meaningful error messages.
* Log blocked requests for troubleshooting.
* Keep short-circuit logic simple.
* Validate requests as early as possible.
* Avoid placing business logic inside middleware.

---

# 10. Common Mistakes

* Forgetting to call `await _next(context)` for valid requests.
* Returning incorrect status codes.
* Using short-circuiting for business logic.
* Not logging blocked requests.
* Placing validation middleware too late in the pipeline.
* Returning sensitive error information to clients.

---

# 11. Key Points to Remember

* Short-circuiting stops the request pipeline.
* Remaining middleware and controllers do not execute.
* It happens when middleware does **not** call `await _next(context)`.
* It improves performance and security.
* Built-in and custom middleware can short-circuit requests.
* Common examples include authentication failures, static files, and maintenance mode.

---

# 12. Summary

Short-Circuiting is an important feature of the ASP.NET Core request pipeline that allows middleware to stop request processing and return a response immediately. It is commonly used for authentication failures, authorization checks, static file serving, API key validation, and maintenance mode. By preventing unnecessary middleware and controller execution, short-circuiting improves application performance, enhances security, and keeps request processing efficient.

---

# 13. Practice Coding Questions

1. Create middleware that returns **401 Unauthorized** when an API key is missing.
2. Create middleware that blocks requests after office hours.
3. Create middleware that returns **503 Service Unavailable** when maintenance mode is enabled.
4. Create middleware that validates a custom request header.
5. Modify a logging middleware so it short-circuits requests to `/blocked`.

---

# 14. Possible Interview Questions and Answers (2 Years Experience)

### Q1. What is Short-Circuiting in ASP.NET Core?

**Answer:** Short-circuiting is the process where middleware stops the request pipeline and returns a response directly to the client without executing the remaining middleware or the controller. It occurs when the middleware does not call `await _next(context)`.

---

### Q2. Why do we use Short-Circuiting?

**Answer:** Short-circuiting is used to avoid unnecessary request processing. It improves performance and security by rejecting invalid requests early. Common examples include authentication failures, authorization failures, API key validation, maintenance mode, and serving static files.

---

### Q3. How does a middleware short-circuit the pipeline?

**Answer:** A middleware short-circuits the pipeline by generating a response and returning without calling `await _next(context)`. Since the next middleware is never invoked, the remaining pipeline is skipped.

---

### Q4. Can built-in middleware short-circuit the pipeline?

**Answer:** Yes. Built-in middleware such as Authentication, Authorization, Static File Middleware, HTTPS Redirection, and Exception Handling can short-circuit the request pipeline under specific conditions, such as invalid credentials or when a requested static file is found.

---

### Q5. What happens if `await _next(context)` is not called?

**Answer:** If `await _next(context)` is not called, the request does not continue to the next middleware. The pipeline ends at the current middleware, and any remaining middleware or controller actions are skipped.

---

### Q6. What are some real-world uses of Short-Circuiting?

**Answer:** Short-circuiting is used for JWT validation, API key validation, maintenance mode, serving static files, health check endpoints, blocking unauthorized users, and rejecting invalid requests before they reach business logic.

---

### Q7. What are the advantages of Short-Circuiting?

**Answer:** It improves performance by reducing unnecessary processing, enhances security by rejecting invalid requests early, lowers server workload, and provides faster responses for requests that do not need further processing.

---

### Q8. What is the relationship between `await _next(context)` and Short-Circuiting?

**Answer:** `await _next(context)` passes the request to the next middleware. If it is called, the pipeline continues. If it is omitted and the middleware returns a response directly, the request is short-circuited and the remaining pipeline does not execute.

---

# 15. Interview Tips

* Remember the key rule: **No `await _next(context)` = Pipeline Stops**.
* Use practical examples like **JWT validation**, **API key checks**, or **Static File Middleware**.
* Mention that short-circuiting is useful for both **performance** and **security**.
* Explain that both **built-in** and **custom** middleware can short-circuit the request.

---

# 16. Revision Notes

* Short-circuiting stops the request pipeline early.
* Remaining middleware and controllers are skipped.
* Occurs when `await _next(context)` is not called.
* Improves performance and security.
* Common in authentication, authorization, static files, and custom validation middleware.

---

# 17. Keywords

**Short-Circuiting, Request Pipeline, Middleware, RequestDelegate, HttpContext, `await _next(context)`, Authentication, Authorization, Static Files, API Key Validation, Maintenance Mode, ASP.NET Core**

---

# Module 3 – Routing

# Topic 1 – Routing Basics & Endpoint Routing

---

# 1. Introduction

When a client sends a request to an ASP.NET Core Web API, the framework needs to determine **which endpoint (Controller Action or Minimal API) should handle that request**.

This process is called **Routing**.

Routing is one of the most important features of ASP.NET Core because it connects the incoming request to the correct code.

ASP.NET Core uses **Endpoint Routing**, which matches the request URL and HTTP method (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, etc.) with the registered endpoints.

Without routing, the application would not know which controller or action should execute.

> **Interview Tip:** Routing is one of the most frequently asked ASP.NET Core interview topics. Interviewers often ask the difference between **Routing**, **Endpoint Routing**, **Attribute Routing**, and **Conventional Routing**.

---

# 2. Definition

**Routing** is the process of matching an incoming HTTP request to the appropriate endpoint based on the request URL and HTTP method.

**Endpoint Routing** is the routing system introduced in ASP.NET Core that selects the appropriate endpoint before it is executed.

### One-Line Interview Definition
