> Routing is the mechanism that maps incoming HTTP requests to the correct endpoint, and Endpoint Routing is the modern ASP.NET Core routing system that performs this matching.

---

# 3. Why Do We Need Routing?

Imagine an application with hundreds of APIs.

Example:

```text
GET    /api/employees

GET    /api/products

POST   /api/orders

PUT    /api/customers/5

DELETE /api/users/10
```

When a request arrives, ASP.NET Core must determine:

* Which controller should execute?
* Which action method should run?
* Which HTTP method matches?
* Which route parameters should be used?

Routing performs this matching automatically.

### Benefits

* Connects requests to the correct endpoint.
* Supports RESTful APIs.
* Makes URLs clean and meaningful.
* Supports route parameters.
* Works with Controllers and Minimal APIs.
* Improves maintainability.

---

# 4. How It Works

Suppose the client sends:

```http
GET /api/employees/5
```

Request Flow

```text
Client
   │
HTTP Request
   │
Routing Middleware
   │
Route Matching
   │
Endpoint Selected
   │
Controller Action Executes
   │
Response
   │
Client
```

If the route matches

```csharp
[HttpGet("{id}")]
public IActionResult GetEmployee(int id)
```

The action executes.

If no matching route exists

```http
404 Not Found
```

is returned.

---

# 5. Key Concepts

---

## 1. What is Routing?

Routing is the process of matching the incoming request with the correct endpoint.

Example

Request

```http
GET /api/products
```

Routing searches for

```text
ProductsController

↓

GetProducts()
```

If found

↓

Execute

Otherwise

↓

404 Not Found

---

## 2. What is an Endpoint?

An Endpoint is the final destination that processes an HTTP request.

Examples

* Controller Action
* Minimal API
* Razor Page
* SignalR Hub

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet]
public IActionResult GetEmployees()
{
    return Ok();
}
```

This action is an endpoint.

---

## 3. What is Endpoint Routing?

Endpoint Routing is the modern routing system introduced in ASP.NET Core.

Instead of directly executing a controller, Endpoint Routing first selects the endpoint.

Flow

```text
Client

↓

Routing

↓

Endpoint Selected

↓

Controller Executes

↓

Response
```

This separation makes routing faster and more flexible.

---

## 4. Route Template

A Route Template defines the URL pattern.

Example

```csharp
[Route("api/employees")]
```

Matches

```text
/api/employees
```

---

Another example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{id}")]
```

Matches

```text
/api/employees/10
```

where

```text
id = 10
```

---

## 5. HTTP Method Matching

Routing checks both

* URL
* HTTP Method

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet]
```

Matches only

```http
GET
```

---

```csharp
[HttpPost]
```

Matches only

```http
POST
```

---

```csharp
[HttpPut]
```

Matches only

```http
PUT
```

---

```csharp
[HttpDelete]
```

Matches only

```http
DELETE
```

Even if the URL is correct, the request fails if the HTTP method does not match.

---

## 6. Route Parameters

Route parameters capture values from the URL.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{id}")]
public IActionResult GetEmployee(int id)
{
    return Ok(id);
}
```

Request

```http
GET /api/employees/15
```

Result

```text
id = 15
```

ASP.NET Core automatically binds the URL value.

---

## 7. Multiple Endpoints

Example

```csharp
[ApiController]
[Route("api/[controller]")]
public class EmployeeController : ControllerBase
{
    [HttpGet]
    public IActionResult GetAll()
    {
        return Ok();
    }

    [HttpGet("{id}")]
    public IActionResult GetById(int id)
    {
        return Ok();
    }

    [HttpPost]
    public IActionResult AddEmployee()
    {
        return Ok();
    }
}
```

Matching

| Request             | Action        |
| ------------------- | ------------- |
| GET /api/employee   | GetAll()      |
| GET /api/employee/5 | GetById()     |
| POST /api/employee  | AddEmployee() |

---

## 8. Routing in Minimal APIs

Routing also works with Minimal APIs.

Example

```csharp
app.MapGet("/hello", () =>
{
    return "Hello World";
});
```

Request

```http
GET /hello
```

Output

```text
Hello World
```

---

## 9. What Happens if No Route Matches?

Example

Application

```text
/api/employees
```

Request

```http
GET /api/products
```

Result

```http
404 Not Found
```

The routing system cannot find a matching endpoint.

---

## 10. Request Lifecycle

```text
Client

↓

HTTP Request

↓

Routing

↓

Endpoint Selected

↓

Controller Action

↓

Business Logic

↓

Response

↓

Client
```

---

# 6. Code Example

```csharp
var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args);

builder.Services.AddControllers();

var app = builder.Build();

app.UseRouting();

app.UseAuthentication();

app.UseAuthorization();

app.MapControllers();

app.Run();
```

---

# 7. Code Explanation

```csharp
app.UseRouting();
```

Enables endpoint routing and matches incoming requests.

---

```csharp
app.UseAuthentication();
```

Authenticates the user after the endpoint is identified.

---

```csharp
app.UseAuthorization();
```

Checks whether the authenticated user can access the selected endpoint.

---

```csharp
app.MapControllers();
```

Registers all controller actions as endpoints.

---

```csharp
app.Run();
```

Starts the application and begins listening for requests.

---

# 8. Real Project Usage ⭐

In my ASP.NET Core Web API projects, routing was used to expose REST API endpoints for modules such as user management, authentication, and transaction processing. Controller actions were mapped using route attributes, allowing frontend applications and external clients to access APIs through clean and predictable URLs. Routing automatically selected the correct controller action based on the request URL and HTTP method, making the APIs easy to organize and maintain. 

---

# 9. Best Practices

* Use meaningful RESTful URLs.
* Keep route names simple and consistent.
* Use route parameters for resource identifiers.
* Avoid duplicate routes.
* Match the correct HTTP method.
* Register routing before authentication and authorization.
* Test routes using Postman or Swagger.

---

# 10. Common Mistakes

* Defining duplicate routes.
* Using incorrect HTTP methods.
* Forgetting to register controllers with `MapControllers()`.
* Creating confusing URL structures.
* Assuming routing checks only the URL (it also checks the HTTP method).
* Forgetting route parameters.

---

# 11. Key Points to Remember

* Routing maps requests to endpoints.
* Endpoint Routing is the modern routing system in ASP.NET Core.
* Routing checks both the URL and the HTTP method.
* An endpoint can be a Controller Action or a Minimal API.
* Route parameters are automatically bound.
* If no route matches, ASP.NET Core returns **404 Not Found**.

---

# 12. Summary

Routing is the mechanism that connects incoming HTTP requests to the correct endpoint in an ASP.NET Core application. Using Endpoint Routing, the framework matches the request URL and HTTP method with registered endpoints, then executes the appropriate controller action or Minimal API. Proper routing is essential for building organized, maintainable, and RESTful Web APIs.

---

# 13. Practice Coding Questions

1. Create an API with a `GET /api/employees` endpoint.
2. Create a `GET /api/employees/{id}` endpoint using a route parameter.
3. Create `POST`, `PUT`, and `DELETE` endpoints for a `ProductController`.
4. Create a Minimal API using `MapGet()`.
5. Test different routes using Postman and observe the responses.

---

# 14. Possible Interview Questions and Answers (2 Years Experience)

### Q1. What is Routing in ASP.NET Core?

**Answer:** Routing is the mechanism that matches an incoming HTTP request to the correct endpoint based on the request URL and HTTP method. It helps the framework determine which controller action or Minimal API should execute. Without routing, requests cannot reach the appropriate business logic.

---

### Q2. What is Endpoint Routing?

**Answer:** Endpoint Routing is the modern routing system introduced in ASP.NET Core. It first selects the matching endpoint based on the request and then allows the endpoint to execute. This approach improves flexibility, performance, and integration with middleware.

---

### Q3. What is an Endpoint?

**Answer:** An endpoint is the final component that handles an HTTP request and generates a response. Examples include Controller Actions, Minimal APIs, Razor Pages, and SignalR Hubs.

---

### Q4. What is a Route Template?

**Answer:** A Route Template defines the URL pattern that an endpoint matches. For example, `[Route("api/employees")]` matches `/api/employees`, while `[HttpGet("{id}")]` matches URLs such as `/api/employees/5`.

---

### Q5. Does Routing check only the URL?

**Answer:** No. Routing checks both the URL and the HTTP method. A request must match the correct route template and the correct HTTP verb (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) to reach the endpoint.

---

### Q6. What happens when no route matches?

**Answer:** If ASP.NET Core cannot find a matching endpoint for the request URL and HTTP method, it returns a **404 Not Found** response to the client.

---

### Q7. Where is routing configured?

**Answer:** Routing is enabled in `Program.cs` using `app.UseRouting()`, and controller endpoints are registered using `app.MapControllers()`. Together, these configure the application's routing system.

---

### Q8. Where have you used routing in your project?

**Answer:** In my projects, routing was used to expose REST APIs for different modules. Controller actions were mapped using route attributes, allowing frontend applications and external systems to access APIs through structured URLs. 

---

# 15. Interview Tips

* Don't confuse **Routing** with **Endpoint Routing**.
* Remember that routing checks **URL + HTTP Method**.
* Explain that **Controller Actions** and **Minimal APIs** are both endpoints.
* Mention that **404 Not Found** occurs when no route matches.
* Be ready for the next topic: **Attribute Routing vs Conventional Routing**.

---

# 16. Revision Notes

* Routing maps requests to endpoints.
* Endpoint Routing is the modern routing system.
* Endpoints include Controller Actions and Minimal APIs.
* Route Templates define URL patterns.
* Routing checks URL and HTTP method.
* Unmatched routes return **404 Not Found**.

---

# 17. Keywords

**Routing, Endpoint Routing, Endpoint, Route Template, Route Parameter, Controller Action, Minimal API, URL Matching, HTTP Method, `UseRouting()`, `MapControllers()`, 404 Not Found**

---
# Module 3 – Routing

# Topic 2 – Attribute Routing

---

# 1. Introduction

In the previous topic, we learned that **Routing** matches an incoming HTTP request to the correct endpoint.

But how does ASP.NET Core know which URL belongs to which controller or action?

One way is **Attribute Routing**.

In Attribute Routing, we define the route **directly on the Controller or Action Method** using attributes like:

* `[Route]`
* `[HttpGet]`
* `[HttpPost]`
* `[HttpPut]`
* `[HttpDelete]`

This is the **most commonly used routing approach in ASP.NET Core Web APIs** because it gives developers complete control over API URLs.

> **Interview Tip:** Almost every ASP.NET Core Web API project uses **Attribute Routing**, making it one of the most frequently asked interview topics.

---

# 2. Definition

**Attribute Routing** is a routing technique where routes are defined using attributes directly on controllers and action methods.

These attributes tell ASP.NET Core which URL and HTTP method should execute a particular action.

### One-Line Interview Definition

> Attribute Routing is a routing approach where routes are defined using attributes on controllers and action methods.

---

# 3. Why Do We Need Attribute Routing?

Without Attribute Routing, managing API URLs becomes difficult, especially in large applications.

Attribute Routing provides:

* Clear and readable routes
* Full control over URL structure
* Better support for REST APIs
* Easy maintenance
* Flexible route design

Example

```text
/api/employees

/api/employees/5

/api/employees/search

/api/orders/100/items
```

Each URL can be explicitly mapped to an action.

---

# 4. How It Works

Suppose the client sends:

```http
GET /api/employees/5
```

Pipeline

```text
Client
   │
HTTP Request
   │
Routing Middleware
   │
Attribute Routing
   │
EmployeeController
   │
GetEmployee(5)
   │
Response
```

The routing system reads the route attributes and finds the matching action.

---

# 5. Key Concepts

---

## 1. Route Attribute

The `[Route]` attribute defines the URL pattern.

Example

```csharp
[Route("api/employees")]
public class EmployeeController : ControllerBase
{
}
```

This controller responds to:

```text
/api/employees
```

---

## 2. HTTP Method Attributes

ASP.NET Core provides different attributes for different HTTP methods.

| Attribute      | HTTP Method |
| -------------- | ----------- |
| `[HttpGet]`    | GET         |
| `[HttpPost]`   | POST        |
| `[HttpPut]`    | PUT         |
| `[HttpDelete]` | DELETE      |
| `[HttpPatch]`  | PATCH       |

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet]
public IActionResult GetEmployees()
{
    return Ok();
}
```

Only GET requests match this action.

---

## 3. Controller-Level Route

A route can be defined at the controller level.

Example

```csharp
[ApiController]
[Route("api/employees")]
public class EmployeeController : ControllerBase
{
    [HttpGet]
    public IActionResult GetAll()
    {
        return Ok();
    }
}
```

Request

```http
GET /api/employees
```

Output

```text
GetAll()
```

---

## 4. Action-Level Route

Routes can also be defined on action methods.

Example

```csharp
[ApiController]
[Route("api/employees")]
public class EmployeeController : ControllerBase
{
    [HttpGet("{id}")]
    public IActionResult GetById(int id)
    {
        return Ok(id);
    }
}
```

Request

```http
GET /api/employees/10
```

Result

```text
id = 10
```

---

## 5. Using `[controller]` Token

Instead of writing the controller name manually, ASP.NET Core provides the `[controller]` token.

Example

```csharp
[ApiController]
[Route("api/[controller]")]
public class EmployeeController : ControllerBase
{
}
```

`[controller]`

↓

Automatically becomes

```text
Employee
```

Final Route

```text
/api/employee
```

If the controller name changes, the route updates automatically.

---

## 6. Route Parameters

Attribute Routing supports route parameters.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{id}")]
public IActionResult GetEmployee(int id)
{
    return Ok(id);
}
```

Request

```http
GET /api/employee/25
```

Result

```text
id = 25
```

ASP.NET Core automatically binds the URL value to the parameter.

---

## 7. Multiple Routes

One controller can contain multiple endpoints.

Example

```csharp
[ApiController]
[Route("api/[controller]")]
public class EmployeeController : ControllerBase
{
    [HttpGet]
    public IActionResult GetAll()
    {
        return Ok();
    }

    [HttpGet("{id}")]
    public IActionResult GetById(int id)
    {
        return Ok();
    }

    [HttpPost]
    public IActionResult Add()
    {
        return Ok();
    }

    [HttpPut("{id}")]
    public IActionResult Update(int id)
    {
        return Ok();
    }

    [HttpDelete("{id}")]
    public IActionResult Delete(int id)
    {
        return Ok();
    }
}
```

Matching

| Request                | Action    |
| ---------------------- | --------- |
| GET /api/employee      | GetAll()  |
| GET /api/employee/5    | GetById() |
| POST /api/employee     | Add()     |
| PUT /api/employee/5    | Update()  |
| DELETE /api/employee/5 | Delete()  |

---

## 8. Route Constraints

Route constraints restrict the type of values allowed.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{id:int}")]
public IActionResult Get(int id)
{
    return Ok();
}
```

Valid

```text
/api/employee/5
```

Invalid

```text
/api/employee/abc
```

Result

```http
404 Not Found
```

Common constraints

| Constraint | Example           |
| ---------- | ----------------- |
| `int`      | `{id:int}`        |
| `guid`     | `{id:guid}`       |
| `bool`     | `{status:bool}`   |
| `datetime` | `{date:datetime}` |

---

## 9. Combining Controller and Action Routes

Example

```csharp
[Route("api/employees")]
public class EmployeeController : ControllerBase
{
    [HttpGet("active")]
    public IActionResult GetActive()
    {
        return Ok();
    }
}
```

Final Route

```text
/api/employees/active
```

Controller route + Action route = Complete URL

---

## 10. Advantages of Attribute Routing

* Easy to understand
* Better REST API support
* Flexible URL design
* Controller-specific routes
* Supports route parameters
* Supports route constraints
* Easy maintenance

---

# 6. Code Example

```csharp
[ApiController]
[Route("api/[controller]")]
public class EmployeeController : ControllerBase
{
    [HttpGet]
    public IActionResult GetAll()
    {
        return Ok("All Employees");
    }

    [HttpGet("{id}")]
    public IActionResult GetById(int id)
    {
        return Ok($"Employee {id}");
    }

    [HttpPost]
    public IActionResult Add()
    {
        return Ok("Employee Added");
    }
}
```

---

# 7. Code Explanation

```csharp
[ApiController]
```

Marks the class as a Web API controller.

---

```csharp
[Route("api/[controller]")]
```

Defines the base route for the controller.

---

```csharp
[HttpGet]
```

Handles GET requests.

---

```csharp
[HttpGet("{id}")]
```

Handles GET requests with a route parameter.

---

```csharp
[HttpPost]
```

Handles POST requests.

---

# 8. Real Project Usage ⭐

In my ASP.NET Core Web API projects, Attribute Routing was used to define RESTful endpoints for different modules such as users, authentication, transactions, and reports. Each controller had a base route using `[Route("api/[controller]")]`, while action methods used attributes like `[HttpGet]`, `[HttpPost]`, `[HttpPut]`, and `[HttpDelete]`. This made the APIs easy to understand, maintain, and consume by frontend applications and external clients. 

---

# 9. Best Practices

* Use `[ApiController]` for Web APIs.
* Use `[Route("api/[controller]")]` instead of hardcoding controller names.
* Follow RESTful URL conventions.
* Keep routes short and meaningful.
* Use route parameters for resource IDs.
* Use route constraints where appropriate.
* Avoid duplicate routes.

---

# 10. Common Mistakes

* Forgetting to add route attributes.
* Creating duplicate routes.
* Using incorrect HTTP method attributes.
* Hardcoding controller names unnecessarily.
* Not using route constraints when needed.
* Mixing inconsistent URL naming conventions.

---

# 11. Key Points to Remember

* Attribute Routing defines routes using attributes.
* It is the preferred routing approach for ASP.NET Core Web APIs.
* Routes can be defined at both controller and action levels.
* `[controller]` automatically uses the controller name.
* Route parameters capture values from the URL.
* Route constraints validate parameter types.
* Supports all HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH).

---

# 12. Summary

Attribute Routing is the most widely used routing approach in ASP.NET Core Web APIs. It allows developers to define routes directly on controllers and action methods using attributes such as `[Route]`, `[HttpGet]`, and `[HttpPost]`. It provides complete control over API URLs, supports RESTful design, route parameters, and route constraints, making applications easier to develop and maintain.

---

# 13. Practice Coding Questions

1. Create an `EmployeeController` using `[Route("api/[controller]")]`.
2. Create a `GET /api/employee` endpoint.
3. Create a `GET /api/employee/{id}` endpoint using a route parameter.
4. Create `POST`, `PUT`, and `DELETE` endpoints using attribute routing.
5. Add an `int` route constraint to the `id` parameter.

---

# 14. Possible Interview Questions and Answers (2 Years Experience)

### Q1. What is Attribute Routing?

**Answer:** Attribute Routing is a routing technique where routes are defined directly on controllers and action methods using attributes like `[Route]`, `[HttpGet]`, `[HttpPost]`, and others. It provides better control over API URLs and is the preferred approach for ASP.NET Core Web APIs.

---

### Q2. Why is Attribute Routing preferred in Web APIs?

**Answer:** Attribute Routing gives developers complete control over URL structure, supports RESTful API design, allows route parameters and constraints, and makes APIs easier to understand and maintain. It is more flexible than conventional routing for Web API development.

---

### Q3. What is the purpose of `[Route]`?

**Answer:** The `[Route]` attribute defines the URL pattern that maps to a controller or action. It tells ASP.NET Core which requests should be handled by that endpoint.

---

### Q4. What is `[controller]` in routing?

**Answer:** `[controller]` is a route token that automatically uses the controller's name without the "Controller" suffix. For example, `EmployeeController` becomes `employee` in the route `api/[controller]`.

---

### Q5. What are Route Parameters?

**Answer:** Route parameters are placeholders in the route template that capture values from the URL and bind them to action method parameters. Example: `{id}` captures the value from `/api/employee/5`.

---

### Q6. What are Route Constraints?

**Answer:** Route constraints restrict the type of values that can match a route. For example, `{id:int}` allows only integer values. Invalid values do not match the route and usually return **404 Not Found**.

---

### Q7. Can we define routes at both the controller and action level?

**Answer:** Yes. A controller can define a base route using `[Route]`, and action methods can define additional routes using HTTP method attributes. ASP.NET Core combines them to create the final URL.

---

### Q8. Where have you used Attribute Routing in your project?

**Answer:** In my ASP.NET Core Web API projects, Attribute Routing was used to define RESTful endpoints for different modules. Each controller had a base route, and action methods used HTTP method attributes to expose APIs for CRUD operations. 

---

# 15. Interview Tips

* Remember: **Attribute Routing is the preferred routing method for Web APIs**.
* Explain the difference between **controller-level** and **action-level** routes.
* Mention the `[controller]` token and its benefit.
* Don't forget to explain **route parameters** and **route constraints**.
* Be prepared for the next topic: **Conventional Routing vs Attribute Routing**.

---

# 16. Revision Notes

* Attribute Routing uses attributes to define routes.
* `[Route]` defines the URL pattern.
* HTTP method attributes specify the request type.
* `[controller]` automatically uses the controller name.
* Route parameters capture URL values.
* Route constraints validate parameter types.
* Supports RESTful API design.

---

# 17. Keywords

**Attribute Routing, Route, Route Template, `[Route]`, `[HttpGet]`, `[HttpPost]`, `[HttpPut]`, `[HttpDelete]`, `[controller]`, Route Parameter, Route Constraint, REST API, ASP.NET Core**

---
# Module 3 – Routing

# Topic 3 – Route Templates and Parameters

---

# 1. Introduction

In the previous topic, we learned how **Attribute Routing** defines URLs for controller actions.

But many APIs need to work with **dynamic values** such as:

* Employee ID
* Product ID
* Order Number
* User ID

For example:

```text
/api/employees/5

/api/products/100

/api/orders/2001
```

The values **5**, **100**, and **2001** are different for every request.

Instead of creating separate routes for every value, ASP.NET Core uses **Route Templates** and **Route Parameters**.

A **Route Template** defines the URL pattern, while a **Route Parameter** captures the dynamic values from the URL.

> **Interview Tip:** Route Templates and Route Parameters are commonly used in CRUD APIs and are frequently asked in interviews.

---

# 2. Definition

A **Route Template** is a URL pattern that defines how an endpoint can be accessed.

A **Route Parameter** is a placeholder inside the route template that captures values from the URL.

### One-Line Interview Definition

> A Route Template defines the URL pattern, and Route Parameters capture dynamic values from that URL.

---

# 3. Why Do We Need Route Templates and Parameters?

Without route parameters, we would need a separate route for every resource.

Example (Not Practical)

```text
/api/employees/1

/api/employees/2

/api/employees/3

/api/employees/4
```

Instead, we create one template.

```text
/api/employees/{id}
```

Now the same route works for every employee.

### Benefits

* Dynamic URLs
* Less code
* Easy maintenance
* Supports REST APIs
* Easy parameter binding
* Cleaner URL structure

---

# 4. How It Works

Client Request

```http
GET /api/employees/15
```

Route Template

```text
/api/employees/{id}
```

Execution Flow

```text
Client
   │
GET /api/employees/15
   │
Routing
   │
Route Template
/api/employees/{id}
   │
id = 15
   │
Controller Action
   │
Response
```

ASP.NET Core automatically extracts **15** and assigns it to the `id` parameter.

---

# 5. Key Concepts

---

## 1. What is a Route Template?

A Route Template is the URL pattern used for matching requests.

Example

```csharp
[Route("api/employees")]
```

Matches

```text
/api/employees
```

---

Another example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{id}")]
```

Matches

```text
/api/employees/5
```

---

## 2. What is a Route Parameter?

A Route Parameter is a placeholder enclosed in curly braces `{}`.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{id}")]
public IActionResult GetEmployee(int id)
{
    return Ok(id);
}
```

Request

```http
GET /api/employees/10
```

Output

```text
10
```

ASP.NET Core automatically binds the URL value.

---

## 3. Multiple Route Parameters

Routes can contain multiple parameters.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{departmentId}/employees/{employeeId}")]
public IActionResult GetEmployee(int departmentId, int employeeId)
{
    return Ok();
}
```

Request

```http
GET /api/5/employees/20
```

Result

```text
departmentId = 5

employeeId = 20
```

---

## 4. Route Parameter Binding

ASP.NET Core automatically binds route values to method parameters.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{id}")]
public IActionResult Get(int id)
{
    return Ok($"Employee Id : {id}");
}
```

Request

```http
GET /api/employee/25
```

Result

```text
Employee Id : 25
```

No manual conversion is required.

---

## 5. Optional Route Parameters

A parameter can be optional using `?`.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{id?}")]
public IActionResult Get(int? id)
{
    if (id == null)
        return Ok("All Employees");

    return Ok($"Employee {id}");
}
```

Requests

```http
GET /api/employee
```

Output

```text
All Employees
```

---

```http
GET /api/employee/5
```

Output

```text
Employee 5
```

---

## 6. Route Constraints

Constraints restrict the type of values accepted.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{id:int}")]
public IActionResult Get(int id)
{
    return Ok(id);
}
```

Valid

```text
/api/employee/10
```

Invalid

```text
/api/employee/abc
```

Result

```http
404 Not Found
```

Some common constraints

| Constraint | Example           |
| ---------- | ----------------- |
| int        | `{id:int}`        |
| guid       | `{id:guid}`       |
| bool       | `{active:bool}`   |
| datetime   | `{date:datetime}` |

---

## 7. Literal Values with Parameters

Routes can combine fixed text and parameters.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("department/{id}")]
public IActionResult GetDepartment(int id)
{
    return Ok();
}
```

Matches

```text
/api/employee/department/10
```

---

## 8. Catch-All Parameters

A catch-all parameter captures the remaining URL.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{*path}")]
public IActionResult GetPath(string path)
{
    return Ok(path);
}
```

Request

```text
/files/images/logo.png
```

Result

```text
images/logo.png
```

---

## 9. Route Templates with Controller Token

Example

```csharp
[ApiController]
[Route("api/[controller]")]
public class ProductController : ControllerBase
{
    [HttpGet("{id}")]
    public IActionResult Get(int id)
    {
        return Ok();
    }
}
```

Final Route

```text
GET /api/product/5
```

---

## 10. Common REST API Route Templates

| Operation | Route                |
| --------- | -------------------- |
| Get All   | `/api/products`      |
| Get By Id | `/api/products/{id}` |
| Create    | `/api/products`      |
| Update    | `/api/products/{id}` |
| Delete    | `/api/products/{id}` |

---

# 6. Code Example

```csharp
[ApiController]
[Route("api/[controller]")]
public class EmployeeController : ControllerBase
{
    [HttpGet]
    public IActionResult GetAll()
    {
        return Ok("All Employees");
    }

    [HttpGet("{id}")]
    public IActionResult GetById(int id)
    {
        return Ok($"Employee Id : {id}");
    }

    [HttpGet("{departmentId}/employees/{employeeId}")]
    public IActionResult GetEmployee(int departmentId, int employeeId)
    {
        return Ok(new
        {
            Department = departmentId,
            Employee = employeeId
        });
    }
}
```

---

# 7. Code Explanation

```csharp
[Route("api/[controller]")]
```

Defines the base route.

---

```csharp
[HttpGet]
```

Handles requests for all employees.

---

```csharp
[HttpGet("{id}")]
```

Captures the employee ID from the URL.

---

```csharp
int id
```

Receives the route parameter automatically.

---

```csharp
[HttpGet("{departmentId}/employees/{employeeId}")]
```

Captures multiple route parameters.

---

# 8. Real Project Usage ⭐

In real ASP.NET Core Web API projects, route templates and parameters are used to access specific resources such as users, products, transactions, and reports. For example, endpoints like `/api/users/{id}` or `/api/transactions/{transactionId}` allow the application to retrieve or update a particular record. ASP.NET Core automatically binds the values from the URL to the action method parameters, making API development simple and maintainable. 

---

# 9. Best Practices

* Use meaningful route names.
* Use route parameters for resource IDs.
* Use route constraints to validate parameter types.
* Keep URLs RESTful and consistent.
* Avoid unnecessary nested routes.
* Use optional parameters only when required.
* Use lowercase URLs for consistency.

---

# 10. Common Mistakes

* Forgetting curly braces `{}` around parameters.
* Mismatching parameter names between the route and method.
* Not using constraints when needed.
* Creating overly complex route templates.
* Using query strings instead of route parameters for resource IDs.
* Defining duplicate routes.

---

# 11. Key Points to Remember

* Route Templates define URL patterns.
* Route Parameters capture dynamic URL values.
* Parameters are enclosed in `{}`.
* ASP.NET Core automatically binds route values.
* Multiple parameters are supported.
* Optional parameters use `?`.
* Constraints restrict parameter types.

---

# 12. Summary

Route Templates and Route Parameters are essential features of ASP.NET Core Routing. A route template defines the structure of a URL, while route parameters capture dynamic values such as IDs directly from the URL. ASP.NET Core automatically binds these values to action method parameters, enabling developers to build clean, flexible, and RESTful APIs with minimal code.

---

# 13. Practice Coding Questions

1. Create an endpoint using `/api/employees/{id}`.
2. Create an endpoint with two route parameters.
3. Create an endpoint using an optional route parameter.
4. Create an endpoint with an `int` route constraint.
5. Create an endpoint using a catch-all route parameter.

---

# 14. Possible Interview Questions and Answers (2 Years Experience)

### Q1. What is a Route Template?

**Answer:** A Route Template is a URL pattern that defines how a request is matched to an endpoint. It can contain fixed segments and dynamic route parameters. ASP.NET Core uses the template to identify the correct controller action.

---

### Q2. What is a Route Parameter?

**Answer:** A Route Parameter is a placeholder enclosed in curly braces `{}` that captures values from the URL. ASP.NET Core automatically binds these values to the corresponding action method parameters.

---

### Q3. How do Route Parameters work?

**Answer:** When a request matches a route template, ASP.NET Core extracts the parameter values from the URL and automatically passes them to the action method. For example, in `/api/employees/5`, the value `5` is bound to the `id` parameter.

---

### Q4. Can a route have multiple parameters?

**Answer:** Yes. A route can contain multiple parameters, such as `{departmentId}` and `{employeeId}`. ASP.NET Core binds each value to its corresponding method parameter.

---

### Q5. What are Optional Route Parameters?

**Answer:** Optional route parameters allow a URL to work with or without a specific value. They are defined using `?`, such as `{id?}`, enabling the same endpoint to handle multiple request patterns.

---

### Q6. What are Route Constraints?

**Answer:** Route Constraints limit the type of values that can match a route. For example, `{id:int}` allows only integer values. If the value does not satisfy the constraint, the request does not match the route.

---

### Q7. What is a Catch-All Parameter?

**Answer:** A Catch-All Parameter captures the remaining part of a URL. It is defined using `*`, such as `{*path}`, and is useful for handling file paths or nested URL segments.

---

### Q8. Where have you used Route Parameters in your project?

**Answer:** In my ASP.NET Core Web API projects, route parameters were used to perform CRUD operations on specific resources, such as retrieving, updating, or deleting records by their IDs. This kept the APIs RESTful and easy to use. 

---

# 15. Interview Tips

* Remember: **Route Template = URL Pattern**, **Route Parameter = Dynamic Value**.
* Explain that ASP.NET Core automatically binds route values to method parameters.
* Give a simple example like `/api/employees/{id}`.
* Mention optional parameters and constraints if asked follow-up questions.

---

# 16. Revision Notes

* Route Templates define URL patterns.
* Route Parameters capture values from URLs.
* Parameters are enclosed in `{}`.
* Multiple and optional parameters are supported.
* Constraints validate parameter types.
* Catch-all parameters capture the remaining URL path.

---

# 17. Keywords

**Route Template, Route Parameter, URL Pattern, Parameter Binding, Route Constraint, Optional Parameter, Catch-All Parameter, Attribute Routing, REST API, ASP.NET Core**

---
# Module 3 – Routing

# Topic 4 – Route Constraints (Part 1 – Learning)

---

# 1. Introduction

In the previous topic, we learned that **Route Parameters** allow us to capture dynamic values from the URL.

For example,

```text
GET /api/employees/10
```

Here, `10` is captured as the `id` parameter.

But what if someone sends:

```text
GET /api/employees/abc
```

The API expects an employee ID, which should be a number, but `"abc"` is not a valid ID.

How can ASP.NET Core prevent such invalid URLs from reaching our action method?

The answer is **Route Constraints**.

Route Constraints allow us to restrict the type or format of values that can match a route. They help ASP.NET Core validate route parameters before the request reaches the controller.

Instead of allowing every value, we can specify that a parameter must be:

* An integer
* A GUID
* A Boolean
* A DateTime
* A string of a certain length
* A value within a range
* A value matching a regular expression

If the incoming value does not satisfy the constraint, the route is **not matched**, and ASP.NET Core automatically returns **404 Not Found**.

> **Interview Tip:** Route Constraints are commonly used in REST APIs to ensure only valid URLs reach the application.

---

# 2. Definition

A **Route Constraint** is a rule applied to a route parameter that restricts the values allowed for that parameter.

If the incoming value satisfies the constraint, the route matches.

If it does not satisfy the constraint, ASP.NET Core ignores the route and returns **404 Not Found**.

### One-Line Interview Definition

> Route Constraints are rules that validate route parameter values before the request reaches the controller.

---

# 3. Why Do We Need Route Constraints?

Imagine an API:

```text
GET /api/products/{id}
```

Suppose someone sends

```text
GET /api/products/abc
```

The application expects an integer ID.

Without constraints:

* Invalid requests reach the controller.
* Extra validation code is required.
* Performance decreases slightly.
* APIs become less predictable.

With constraints:

```csharp
[HttpGet("{id:int}")]
```

Only integer values are accepted.

Example

Valid

```text
/api/products/25
```

Invalid

```text
/api/products/abc
```

Result

```http
404 Not Found
```

### Benefits

* Validates URLs before controller execution.
* Prevents invalid requests.
* Reduces unnecessary processing.
* Makes APIs more predictable.
* Improves readability.
* Supports RESTful API design.

---

# 4. How It Works

Suppose the client sends

```http
GET /api/employees/25
```

Route

```csharp
[HttpGet("{id:int}")]
```

Execution Flow

```text
Client
   │
HTTP Request
   │
Routing
   │
Route Constraint Check
   │
id = 25 ✔
   │
Controller Action
   │
Response
```

---

Invalid Request

```http
GET /api/employees/abc
```

Execution Flow

```text
Client
   │
Routing
   │
Constraint Check
   │
abc is NOT Integer
   │
Route Not Matched
   │
404 Not Found
```

Notice that the controller action is **never executed**.

---

# 5. Key Concepts

---

# 1. What is a Route Constraint?

A Route Constraint is added after the route parameter using a colon (`:`).

Syntax

```csharp
{parameter:constraint}
```

Example

```csharp
{id:int}
```

Here

* `id` → Route Parameter
* `int` → Route Constraint

---

# 2. Integer Constraint (`int`)

Allows only integer values.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{id:int}")]
public IActionResult GetEmployee(int id)
{
    return Ok(id);
}
```

Valid

```text
/api/employees/15
```

Controller executes.

Invalid

```text
/api/employees/abc
```

Result

```http
404 Not Found
```

---

# 3. GUID Constraint (`guid`)

Allows only GUID values.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{id:guid}")]
public IActionResult Get(Guid id)
{
    return Ok(id);
}
```

Valid

```text
/api/users/9e0e82f5-4dd2-4e7f-9f3d-1d5db4e1fd01
```

Invalid

```text
/api/users/12345
```

Result

```http
404 Not Found
```

GUIDs are commonly used as primary keys in enterprise applications.

---

# 4. Boolean Constraint (`bool`)

Accepts only

* true
* false

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{active:bool}")]
public IActionResult Get(bool active)
{
    return Ok(active);
}
```

Valid

```text
/api/users/true
```

```text
/api/users/false
```

Invalid

```text
/api/users/yes
```

Result

```http
404 Not Found
```

---

# 5. DateTime Constraint (`datetime`)

Accepts only valid date values.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{date:datetime}")]
public IActionResult Get(DateTime date)
{
    return Ok(date);
}
```

Example

```text
/api/report/2026-07-25
```

If the value is not a valid date, the route does not match.

---

# 6. Decimal Constraint (`decimal`)

Allows decimal values.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{price:decimal}")]
public IActionResult Get(decimal price)
{
    return Ok(price);
}
```

Valid

```text
/api/products/250.50
```

Invalid

```text
/api/products/ABC
```

---

# 7. Double Constraint (`double`)

Accepts floating-point values.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{value:double}")]
public IActionResult Get(double value)
{
    return Ok(value);
}
```

Example

```text
/temperature/36.7
```

---

# 8. Float Constraint (`float`)

Allows float values.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{weight:float}")]
public IActionResult Get(float weight)
{
    return Ok(weight);
}
```

Example

```text
/weight/65.5
```

---

# 9. Long Constraint (`long`)

Accepts large integer values.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{mobile:long}")]
public IActionResult Get(long mobile)
{
    return Ok(mobile);
}
```

Example

```text
/mobile/9876543210
```

Useful for large numeric values.

---

# 10. Alpha Constraint (`alpha`)

Accepts only alphabetic characters.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{name:alpha}")]
public IActionResult Get(string name)
{
    return Ok(name);
}
```

Valid

```text
/api/users/Vikas
```

Invalid

```text
/api/users/Vikas123
```

Result

```http
404 Not Found
```

---

# 11. Real Project Usage ⭐

In enterprise ASP.NET Core Web API projects, Route Constraints are used to validate route parameters before requests reach the business logic. For example, APIs often use `{id:int}` to ensure numeric IDs, `{id:guid}` for GUID-based identifiers, and `{date:datetime}` for report or transaction dates. This prevents invalid requests from reaching controllers, reduces unnecessary processing, and keeps APIs reliable and secure.

---

# 12. Best Practices

* Use constraints whenever a parameter has a fixed data type.
* Use `int` for numeric IDs.
* Use `guid` when identifiers are GUIDs.
* Keep route templates simple and readable.
* Combine constraints with RESTful URL design.
* Let routing reject invalid requests instead of writing manual validation in every controller.
* Use meaningful parameter names.

---

# 13. Common Mistakes

* Forgetting the colon (`:`) before the constraint.
* Using the wrong constraint type.
* Assuming constraints validate business rules (they only validate route values).
* Adding unnecessary constraints.
* Expecting invalid values to reach the controller.
* Confusing model validation with route constraints.

---

# 14. Key Points to Remember

* Route Constraints validate route parameters.
* Constraints are written after a colon (`:`).
* Invalid values do **not** execute the controller.
* ASP.NET Core returns **404 Not Found** when a constraint fails.
* Common constraints include `int`, `guid`, `bool`, `datetime`, `decimal`, `double`, `float`, `long`, and `alpha`.
* Constraints improve API reliability and performance.

---

# 15. Summary

Route Constraints are an important feature of ASP.NET Core Routing that restrict the values allowed for route parameters. They ensure that only valid URLs match a route, preventing invalid requests from reaching the controller. By supporting constraints such as `int`, `guid`, `bool`, `datetime`, and `alpha`, ASP.NET Core helps developers build secure, predictable, and maintainable REST APIs. In real-world projects, route constraints reduce unnecessary validation code and improve the overall reliability of the application.

---
# Module 3 – Routing

# Topic 4 – Route Constraints (Part 2 – Interview Preparation)

---

# ⭐ Possible Interview Questions

## Q1. What are Route Constraints in ASP.NET Core?

### Answer

Route Constraints are rules that restrict the values allowed for route parameters. They are added after a route parameter using a colon (`:`), such as `{id:int}`. Before the request reaches the controller, ASP.NET Core checks whether the route value satisfies the specified constraint. If the value is valid, the request continues to the controller action. If the value is invalid, the route does not match and ASP.NET Core returns **404 Not Found**. This prevents invalid requests from entering the application's business logic. Route Constraints improve API reliability, reduce unnecessary validation code, and make REST APIs more predictable. Common constraints include `int`, `guid`, `bool`, `datetime`, and `alpha`. They are widely used in enterprise applications to validate route values efficiently.

---

## Q2. Why do we use Route Constraints?

### Answer

Route Constraints are used to ensure that only valid URL values are accepted by an API. Without constraints, any value can match a route, which means invalid requests may reach the controller and require additional validation. By using constraints like `{id:int}`, ASP.NET Core validates the route before the controller executes. This improves application performance because invalid requests are rejected early. It also keeps controller code clean since developers do not need to manually check the parameter type in every action. Constraints make APIs more secure, predictable, and easier to maintain. They are especially useful in REST APIs where IDs, GUIDs, dates, and other values have fixed formats.

---

## Q3. What happens if a Route Constraint fails?

### Answer

When a Route Constraint fails, ASP.NET Core considers the route as **not matched**. The request never reaches the controller action, and the framework automatically returns a **404 Not Found** response. For example, if an action uses `{id:int}` and the request is `/api/employees/abc`, the value `abc` does not satisfy the integer constraint. Since the route does not match, ASP.NET Core skips controller execution completely. This behavior is different from model validation because the request is rejected during routing itself. Rejecting invalid URLs early improves performance and prevents unnecessary processing inside the application.

---

## Q4. Explain the `int` Route Constraint.

### Answer

The `int` Route Constraint allows only integer values for a route parameter. It is written as `{id:int}`. When a request is received, ASP.NET Core checks whether the value can be converted to an integer. If it can, the controller action executes normally. If not, the route is ignored and a **404 Not Found** response is returned. This constraint is commonly used for primary keys or numeric identifiers in CRUD APIs. For example, `/api/products/15` is valid, while `/api/products/abc` is invalid. Using the `int` constraint ensures that only numeric IDs reach the controller and simplifies application logic.

---

## Q5. What is the difference between Route Constraints and Model Validation?

### Answer

Route Constraints and Model Validation serve different purposes in ASP.NET Core. Route Constraints validate the values present in the URL before the controller action executes. If the constraint fails, the route does not match and ASP.NET Core returns **404 Not Found**. Model Validation, on the other hand, validates the request data after routing has already selected the controller action. It checks things such as required fields, string lengths, email formats, and custom validation rules. In simple terms, Route Constraints validate the URL, while Model Validation validates the request data sent to the action. Both are important but are used at different stages of request processing.

---

## Q6. What are some commonly used Route Constraints?

### Answer

ASP.NET Core provides several built-in Route Constraints for different data types and validation scenarios. Common constraints include `int` for integers, `guid` for GUID values, `bool` for true or false values, `datetime` for date values, `decimal` for decimal numbers, `double` for floating-point values, `float` for single-precision values, `long` for large integers, and `alpha` for alphabetic characters. There are also advanced constraints such as `min`, `max`, `length`, `minlength`, `maxlength`, and `regex`. Developers choose constraints based on the expected format of the route parameter.

---

## Q7. Can multiple Route Constraints be applied to the same parameter?

### Answer

Yes. ASP.NET Core allows multiple Route Constraints to be applied to the same route parameter. Multiple constraints are separated using a colon (`:`). For example:

```csharp
[HttpGet("{id:int:min(1)}")]
```

In this example, the value must first be an integer and then it must be greater than or equal to one. If either condition fails, the route does not match and the request returns **404 Not Found**. Combining constraints helps enforce more specific URL rules without writing additional validation code in the controller. It also makes the API behavior more predictable and easier to understand.

---

## Q8. Have you used Route Constraints in your project?

### Answer

Yes. In my ASP.NET Core Web API projects, Route Constraints were used to validate route parameters before requests reached the controller. For example, numeric IDs were defined using `{id:int}` to ensure that only valid integer values matched the endpoint. This prevented invalid URLs from entering the business logic and reduced the need for manual validation inside controller actions. Constraints made the APIs more reliable and easier to maintain. They also improved readability because the expected parameter type was visible directly in the route definition. 

---

# ⭐ Advanced Route Constraints

---

## 1. Minimum Value Constraint (`min`)

Ensures that the parameter is greater than or equal to a specified value.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{id:int:min(1)}")]
```

Valid

```text
/api/products/10
```

Invalid

```text
/api/products/0
```

---

## 2. Maximum Value Constraint (`max`)

Limits the maximum allowed value.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{age:int:max(60)}")]
```

Only values up to **60** are accepted.

---

## 3. Range Constraint

Restricts the value within a range.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{age:int:range(18,60)}")]
```

Only values between **18** and **60** are valid.

---

## 4. Length Constraint

Restricts the length of a string.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{code:length(5)}")]
```

Only strings with exactly **5** characters are accepted.

---

## 5. Minimum Length Constraint

```csharp
[HttpGet("{name:minlength(3)}")]
```

Accepts names with at least **3** characters.

---

## 6. Maximum Length Constraint

```csharp
[HttpGet("{name:maxlength(20)}")]
```

Accepts names up to **20** characters.

---

## 7. Regular Expression Constraint (`regex`)

Matches a value against a regular expression.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{code:regex(^[A-Z]{3}[0-9]{3}$)}")]
```

Valid

```text
ABC123
```

Invalid

```text
abc123
```

---

## 8. Combining Constraints

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{id:int:min(1):max(100)}")]
```

The value must:

* Be an integer
* Be at least 1
* Be at most 100

---

## 9. Custom Route Constraints

ASP.NET Core also allows developers to create **Custom Route Constraints** by implementing the `IRouteConstraint` interface.

This is useful when built-in constraints are not enough.

Example use cases:

* Employee Code validation
* PAN number validation
* GST number validation
* Company-specific URL formats

---

# ⭐ Project-Based Interview Questions

## Q1. Why did you use Route Constraints in your project?

### Answer

We used Route Constraints to validate URL parameters before requests reached the controller. For example, resource IDs were defined with `{id:int}` so that only numeric values could access CRUD endpoints. This reduced unnecessary validation code, improved API reliability, and ensured invalid URLs were rejected automatically during routing. 

---

## Q2. Have you ever created a Custom Route Constraint?

### Answer

In my project, I mainly used built-in Route Constraints such as `int` and `guid`. I have not implemented a custom route constraint in a production project yet, but I understand that it can be created by implementing the `IRouteConstraint` interface when business-specific URL validation is required.

---

## Q3. Why is returning 404 better than checking inside the controller?

### Answer

Returning **404 Not Found** during routing prevents invalid requests from entering the controller. This improves performance because the controller, services, and database are never executed. It also keeps controller code cleaner since validation is handled by the routing system.

---

# ⭐ Rapid Fire Questions

1. What is a Route Constraint? → A rule that validates a route parameter.
2. Which symbol is used before a constraint? → `:`
3. Which constraint allows only integers? → `int`
4. Which constraint allows only GUIDs? → `guid`
5. Which constraint allows only letters? → `alpha`
6. What happens if a constraint fails? → **404 Not Found**
7. Can multiple constraints be used together? → Yes.
8. Which interface is used for custom constraints? → `IRouteConstraint`
9. Do Route Constraints validate the request body? → No.
10. When are Route Constraints checked? → During routing, before the controller executes.

---

# ⭐ Follow-up Questions

### Q1. Can Route Constraints improve performance?

**Answer:** Yes. Invalid requests are rejected during routing, so the controller, services, and database are not executed. This reduces unnecessary processing and improves overall application performance.

---

### Q2. Do Route Constraints replace Model Validation?

**Answer:** No. Route Constraints validate URL values, while Model Validation checks request body data and business rules after the controller action has been selected.

---

# ⭐ Interview Tips

* Remember: **Route Constraints validate the URL, not the request body**.
* Mention that **constraint failures return 404 Not Found**, not 400 Bad Request.
* Explain the difference between **Route Constraints** and **Model Validation**.
* Give examples like `{id:int}` or `{id:guid}`.
* Mention **Custom Route Constraints** only if asked about advanced scenarios.

---

# ⭐ Revision Notes

* Route Constraints restrict route parameter values.
* Constraints are added after `:` in route templates.
* Failed constraints return **404 Not Found**.
* Common constraints: `int`, `guid`, `bool`, `datetime`, `alpha`.
* Advanced constraints: `min`, `max`, `range`, `length`, `regex`.
* Multiple constraints can be combined.
* Custom constraints implement `IRouteConstraint`.

---

# ⭐ Keywords

**Route Constraints, `int`, `guid`, `bool`, `datetime`, `alpha`, `min`, `max`, `range`, `length`, `regex`, `IRouteConstraint`, Route Parameter, 404 Not Found, ASP.NET Core Routing**

----
# Module 3 – Routing

# Topic 5 – Route Precedence and Ambiguity (Part 1 – Learning)

---

# 1. Introduction

In the previous topics, we learned how ASP.NET Core uses **Routing**, **Attribute Routing**, **Route Templates**, and **Route Constraints** to match incoming HTTP requests.

But what happens if **multiple routes seem to match the same request?**

For example, consider these two routes:

```csharp
[HttpGet("products/{id}")]
public IActionResult GetById(int id)
{
    return Ok();
}

[HttpGet("products/search")]
public IActionResult Search()
{
    return Ok();
}
```

Now suppose the client sends:

```http
GET /products/search
```

Should ASP.NET Core call:

* `GetById()` ?
* `Search()` ?

How does it decide?

The answer is **Route Precedence**.

ASP.NET Core follows a set of routing rules to determine **which route is more specific**. If it cannot decide because two or more routes have equal priority, it reports an **Ambiguous Route** error.

Understanding route precedence helps developers avoid routing conflicts and build predictable APIs.

> **Interview Tip:** Questions about ambiguous routes are common because they test whether you understand how ASP.NET Core selects endpoints.

---

# 2. Definition

**Route Precedence** is the priority system ASP.NET Core uses to determine which route should handle a request when multiple routes appear to match.

**Route Ambiguity** occurs when two or more routes have the same priority and ASP.NET Core cannot determine which endpoint should execute.

### One-Line Interview Definition

> Route Precedence decides the best matching route, while Route Ambiguity occurs when multiple routes match equally and ASP.NET Core cannot choose one.

---

# 3. Why Do We Need Route Precedence?

Imagine an API with hundreds of endpoints.

Different routes may look similar.

Example

```text
/api/products/search

/api/products/{id}

/api/products/latest

/api/products/{category}
```

If routing did not follow precedence rules:

* Wrong controller actions might execute.
* Different developers could create conflicting routes.
* APIs would behave unpredictably.

Route Precedence ensures:

* The most specific route wins.
* Requests always execute the correct endpoint.
* Routing remains fast and reliable.

### Benefits

* Predictable routing
* Fewer conflicts
* Easer debugging
* Better API design
* Cleaner route organization

---

# 4. How It Works

Suppose the application contains:

```csharp
[HttpGet("products/search")]
public IActionResult Search()
{
    return Ok();
}

[HttpGet("products/{id}")]
public IActionResult GetById(int id)
{
    return Ok();
}
```

Client Request

```http
GET /products/search
```

Execution Flow

```text
Client
   │
HTTP Request
   │
Routing
   │
Compare All Matching Routes
   │
Specific Route Wins
   │
Search()
   │
Response
```

ASP.NET Core chooses the **literal route** because it is more specific than a parameter route.

---

# 5. Key Concepts

---

# 1. What is Route Precedence?

Route Precedence is the order in which ASP.NET Core evaluates matching routes.

It always tries to select the **most specific** route.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("users/profile")]
```

and

```csharp
[HttpGet("users/{id}")]
```

Request

```http
GET /users/profile
```

Selected Route

```text
users/profile
```

because literal segments have higher priority than route parameters.

---

# 2. Literal Routes Have Higher Priority

Literal values are fixed values.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("products/latest")]
```

is more specific than

```csharp
[HttpGet("products/{id}")]
```

Request

```http
GET /products/latest
```

Result

```text
GetLatest()
```

The parameter route is ignored.

---

# 3. Route Parameters Have Lower Priority

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("products/{id}")]
```

Matches

```text
/products/1

/products/10

/products/25
```

But if a literal route also matches,

the literal route wins.

---

# 4. Route Constraints Increase Priority

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{id:int}")]
```

and

```csharp
[HttpGet("{name}")]
```

Request

```http
GET /25
```

ASP.NET Core selects

```text
{id:int}
```

because the constrained route is more specific.

---

Request

```http
GET /Vikas
```

Selected Route

```text
{name}
```

because `"Vikas"` is not an integer.

---

# 5. Longer Routes Are More Specific

Example

```csharp
products/{id}
```

and

```csharp
products/category/{id}
```

Request

```http
GET /products/category/5
```

Selected Route

```text
products/category/{id}
```

because it contains more literal segments.

---

# 6. Route Matching Example

Controller

```csharp
[ApiController]
[Route("api/products")]
public class ProductController : ControllerBase
{
    [HttpGet("latest")]
    public IActionResult Latest()
    {
        return Ok();
    }

    [HttpGet("{id:int}")]
    public IActionResult Get(int id)
    {
        return Ok();
    }
}
```

Requests

```http
GET /api/products/latest
```

↓

Latest()

---

```http
GET /api/products/15
```

↓

Get(15)

ASP.NET Core automatically selects the correct endpoint.

---

# 7. What is Route Ambiguity?

Ambiguity occurs when multiple routes have the **same priority**.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{id}")]
```

and

```csharp
[HttpGet("{name}")]
```

Request

```http
GET /10
```

Both routes match.

ASP.NET Core cannot decide.

Result

```text
AmbiguousMatchException
```

The application reports an ambiguous route error.

---

# 8. Another Ambiguous Example

Controller

```csharp
[HttpGet("{value}")]
public IActionResult First(string value)
{
    return Ok();
}

[HttpGet("{name}")]
public IActionResult Second(string name)
{
    return Ok();
}
```

Both routes represent the same pattern.

The parameter names are different,

but the route template is identical.

Therefore,

```http
GET /abc
```

matches both.

---

# 9. How to Avoid Ambiguous Routes

Instead of

```csharp
{id}
```

and

```csharp
{name}
```

use

```csharp
{id:int}
```

and

```csharp
{name:alpha}
```

Now

```text
/25
```

↓

First Action

---

```text
/Vikas
```

↓

Second Action

No ambiguity exists.

---

# 10. Route Selection Order

Generally, ASP.NET Core prefers routes in this order:

| Priority | Route Type             |
| -------- | ---------------------- |
| 1        | Literal Route          |
| 2        | Route with Constraints |
| 3        | Route Parameters       |
| 4        | Catch-All Routes       |

Example

```text
products/latest
```

↓

Highest Priority

---

```text
products/{id:int}
```

↓

Second Priority

---

```text
products/{id}
```

↓

Third Priority

---

```text
products/{*path}
```

↓

Lowest Priority

---

# 6. Code Example

```csharp
[ApiController]
[Route("api/products")]
public class ProductController : ControllerBase
{
    [HttpGet("latest")]
    public IActionResult GetLatest()
    {
        return Ok("Latest Product");
    }

    [HttpGet("{id:int}")]
    public IActionResult GetById(int id)
    {
        return Ok($"Product {id}");
    }

    [HttpGet("{name:alpha}")]
    public IActionResult GetByName(string name)
    {
        return Ok($"Product Name: {name}");
    }
}
```

---

# 7. Code Explanation

```csharp
[HttpGet("latest")]
```

Matches only:

```text
/api/products/latest
```

---

```csharp
[HttpGet("{id:int}")]
```

Matches only integer values.

Example

```text
/api/products/10
```

---

```csharp
[HttpGet("{name:alpha}")]
```

Matches alphabetic values only.

Example

```text
/api/products/Laptop
```

---

ASP.NET Core selects the correct action automatically based on route precedence.

---

# 8. Real Project Usage ⭐

In enterprise ASP.NET Core Web API projects, route precedence helps avoid conflicts between similar endpoints. For example, APIs may contain endpoints such as `/api/users/profile`, `/api/users/{id:int}`, and `/api/users/{username:alpha}`. ASP.NET Core uses literal routes and route constraints to determine the correct endpoint, ensuring that requests are routed accurately without ambiguity. This keeps APIs predictable and prevents runtime routing errors.

---

# 9. Best Practices

* Prefer literal routes whenever possible.
* Use route constraints to distinguish similar routes.
* Avoid duplicate route templates.
* Keep routes simple and RESTful.
* Test similar routes using Swagger or Postman.
* Use meaningful route names.
* Avoid unnecessary catch-all routes.

---

# 10. Common Mistakes

* Creating identical route templates.
* Depending only on parameter names to differentiate routes.
* Forgetting route constraints.
* Assuming ASP.NET Core checks method parameter names.
* Using catch-all routes unnecessarily.
* Ignoring ambiguous routing warnings.

---

# 11. Key Points to Remember

* Route Precedence decides which route executes.
* Literal routes have the highest priority.
* Route Constraints increase route specificity.
* Parameter names do **not** affect routing.
* Identical route templates create ambiguity.
* Ambiguous routes result in an `AmbiguousMatchException`.
* Catch-all routes have the lowest priority.

---

# 12. Summary

Route Precedence is the mechanism ASP.NET Core uses to select the most appropriate endpoint when multiple routes appear to match a request. Literal routes have the highest priority, followed by constrained routes, parameter routes, and finally catch-all routes. If two or more routes have the same priority and the framework cannot determine which one to execute, a Route Ambiguity occurs, resulting in an `AmbiguousMatchException`. By designing clear route templates and using route constraints, developers can build predictable, maintainable, and conflict-free Web APIs.

---

# Module 3 – Routing

# Topic 5 – Route Precedence and Ambiguity (Part 2 – Interview Preparation)

---

# ⭐ Possible Interview Questions

## Q1. What is Route Precedence in ASP.NET Core?

### Answer

Route Precedence is the mechanism used by ASP.NET Core to decide which endpoint should handle a request when multiple routes appear to match. The routing system compares all matching routes and selects the one that is considered the most specific. Literal routes have the highest priority, followed by routes with constraints, parameter routes, and finally catch-all routes. This ensures that requests are always directed to the most appropriate controller action. Route Precedence helps avoid incorrect endpoint selection and keeps API behavior predictable. Developers do not manually choose the route because ASP.NET Core performs this process automatically during routing. Understanding Route Precedence is important when designing APIs with similar route patterns.

---

## Q2. What is Route Ambiguity?

### Answer

Route Ambiguity occurs when two or more routes have the same priority and ASP.NET Core cannot determine which endpoint should process the request. This usually happens when multiple route templates are identical or equally specific. For example, two actions using `{id}` and `{name}` have different parameter names, but both represent the same route pattern. When a request matches both routes equally, the routing system cannot make a decision and throws an **AmbiguousMatchException**. Ambiguous routes should be avoided because they prevent the application from routing requests correctly. Using route constraints or changing the route templates resolves this problem.

---

## Q3. Which route has the highest priority?

### Answer

Literal routes have the highest priority in ASP.NET Core routing. A literal route contains fixed URL segments such as `/products/latest` or `/users/profile`. These routes are considered more specific because they match only one exact URL. If both a literal route and a parameter route match the same request, ASP.NET Core always selects the literal route. This behavior ensures that special endpoints are not mistakenly treated as route parameters. Understanding this priority helps developers avoid unexpected routing behavior when designing REST APIs.

---

## Q4. How do Route Constraints affect Route Precedence?

### Answer

Route Constraints increase the specificity of a route and therefore increase its priority. For example, `{id:int}` is considered more specific than `{value}` because it only accepts integer values. If a request contains an integer, ASP.NET Core prefers the constrained route over the general parameter route. Constraints help the routing engine distinguish between similar endpoints and reduce routing ambiguity. They also improve API reliability by rejecting invalid URLs before the controller executes. In real projects, constraints such as `int`, `guid`, and `alpha` are commonly used to make routing more predictable.

---

## Q5. Do parameter names affect Route Precedence?

### Answer

No. Parameter names do not affect Route Precedence or route matching. ASP.NET Core only considers the structure of the route template, not the variable names used inside it. For example, `{id}` and `{name}` are treated as identical route templates because both accept any single URL segment. If both routes exist for the same HTTP method, ASP.NET Core cannot distinguish between them and an **AmbiguousMatchException** occurs. To differentiate similar routes, developers should use route constraints or different literal segments instead of relying on parameter names.

---

## Q6. How can you resolve Route Ambiguity?

### Answer

Route Ambiguity can be resolved by making routes more specific. One common solution is to use route constraints such as `{id:int}` and `{name:alpha}` so that each route accepts different types of values. Another approach is to use different literal URL segments like `/users/profile` and `/users/{id}`. Developers should also avoid creating duplicate route templates with the same HTTP method. Proper route design makes the routing system more predictable and prevents runtime routing errors. Testing APIs in Swagger or Postman also helps identify ambiguous routes early.

---

## Q7. What happens when ASP.NET Core finds an ambiguous route?

### Answer

When ASP.NET Core finds two or more routes with the same priority, it cannot decide which controller action should execute. Instead of choosing one randomly, it throws an **AmbiguousMatchException**. This exception indicates that multiple endpoints match the same request and the routing system requires the developer to resolve the conflict. The application will not process the request until the ambiguity is removed. Developers should modify the route templates, add route constraints, or redesign the endpoints to eliminate the conflict. This behavior ensures consistent and reliable routing.

---

## Q8. Where have you seen Route Precedence in real projects?

### Answer

In ASP.NET Core Web API projects, Route Precedence is commonly seen when applications have both fixed and dynamic endpoints. For example, an API may contain routes such as `/api/users/profile`, `/api/users/{id:int}`, and `/api/users/{username:alpha}`. ASP.NET Core automatically selects the correct endpoint based on the request URL and route specificity. This prevents conflicts between fixed URLs and parameterized routes and ensures that requests always reach the intended controller action. Proper route design reduces maintenance effort and keeps APIs easy to understand. 

---

# ⭐ Project-Based Interview Questions

## Q1. Have you ever faced Route Ambiguity in a project?

### Answer

In my projects, I have mainly used well-structured Attribute Routing with route constraints, so I have not faced a major Route Ambiguity issue in production. However, I understand that ambiguity occurs when two routes have the same template and HTTP method. In such cases, I would resolve it by using route constraints like `{id:int}` or by changing the URL structure to make each endpoint unique.

---

## Q2. Why are Route Constraints useful in avoiding ambiguity?

### Answer

Route Constraints make routes more specific by restricting the values that a parameter can accept. For example, `{id:int}` accepts only integers, while `{name:alpha}` accepts only alphabetic values. This allows ASP.NET Core to clearly identify the correct endpoint and prevents ambiguous routing situations.

---

## Q3. What would you do if Swagger shows an AmbiguousMatchException?

### Answer

First, I would check the controller for duplicate route templates with the same HTTP method. Then I would verify whether route parameters are causing the conflict. If required, I would add route constraints, change the route pattern, or introduce literal segments to make each endpoint unique. After making the changes, I would test the endpoints again using Swagger or Postman.

---

# ⭐ Rapid Fire Questions

1. What is Route Precedence? → The priority system used to select the best matching route.
2. What is Route Ambiguity? → When multiple routes match equally and ASP.NET Core cannot choose one.
3. Which route has the highest priority? → Literal route.
4. Which has higher priority: `{id:int}` or `{id}`? → `{id:int}`.
5. Do parameter names affect routing? → No.
6. Which exception occurs for ambiguous routes? → `AmbiguousMatchException`.
7. Which route has the lowest priority? → Catch-all route.
8. Can route constraints prevent ambiguity? → Yes.
9. Does ASP.NET Core randomly choose between ambiguous routes? → No.
10. Should duplicate route templates be avoided? → Yes.

---

# ⭐ Follow-up Questions

## Q1. Can two actions have the same route template?

### Answer

Yes, but only if they are differentiated by another factor such as a different HTTP method. If two actions have the same route template and the same HTTP method, ASP.NET Core cannot determine which one should execute, resulting in an **AmbiguousMatchException**.

---

## Q2. Is Route Precedence configurable?

### Answer

No. ASP.NET Core has built-in routing rules that determine precedence automatically. Developers influence route selection by designing better route templates, adding literal segments, and using route constraints rather than changing the precedence rules themselves.

---

# ⭐ Interview Tips

* Remember the routing priority:

  1. Literal Routes
  2. Routes with Constraints
  3. Parameter Routes
  4. Catch-All Routes
* Explain that parameter names do **not** affect route matching.
* Mention **AmbiguousMatchException** when discussing ambiguous routes.
* Use route constraints to resolve similar route patterns.
* Give practical examples like `/products/latest` and `/products/{id:int}`.

---

# ⭐ Revision Notes

* Route Precedence selects the most specific route.
* Literal routes have the highest priority.
* Route Constraints increase route specificity.
* Parameter names are ignored during route matching.
* Duplicate route templates can cause ambiguity.
* Ambiguous routes result in **AmbiguousMatchException**.
* Catch-all routes have the lowest priority.

---

# ⭐ Keywords

**Route Precedence, Route Ambiguity, AmbiguousMatchException, Literal Route, Route Constraint, Route Parameter, Catch-All Route, Endpoint Selection, Attribute Routing, ASP.NET Core Routing**

---

# Module 3 – Routing

# Topic 6 – Route Groups (for Minimal APIs) (Part 1 – Learning)

---

# 1. Introduction

In traditional ASP.NET Core applications, APIs are usually organized using **Controllers**.

Example:

```text
/api/products
/api/products/1
/api/products/search
```

All these endpoints belong to the **ProductController**.

However, in **Minimal APIs**, there are no controllers. Every endpoint is created directly in `Program.cs` using methods like `MapGet()`, `MapPost()`, `MapPut()`, and `MapDelete()`.

As the application grows, hundreds of endpoints in a single `Program.cs` file become difficult to manage.

Example

```csharp
app.MapGet("/products", ...);
app.MapGet("/products/{id}", ...);
app.MapPost("/products", ...);
app.MapPut("/products/{id}", ...);
app.MapDelete("/products/{id}", ...);

app.MapGet("/customers", ...);
app.MapPost("/customers", ...);
```

This code quickly becomes difficult to read and maintain.

To solve this problem, ASP.NET Core introduced **Route Groups** in **.NET 7**.

Route Groups allow developers to group related endpoints under a common route prefix and apply common settings such as authorization, tags, filters, and middleware to the entire group.

> **Interview Tip:** Route Groups are available only for **Minimal APIs**. They are not used with MVC Controllers.

---

# 2. Definition

A **Route Group** is a feature in Minimal APIs that groups related endpoints under a common route prefix so they can share configuration like authorization, filters, tags, metadata, and middleware.

### One-Line Interview Definition

> Route Groups organize related Minimal API endpoints under a shared route prefix and common configuration.

---

# 3. Why Do We Need Route Groups?

Imagine a Product API.

Without Route Groups

```csharp
app.MapGet("/api/products", ...);
app.MapGet("/api/products/{id}", ...);
app.MapPost("/api/products", ...);
app.MapPut("/api/products/{id}", ...);
app.MapDelete("/api/products/{id}", ...);
```

Notice that the prefix

```text
/api/products
```

is repeated multiple times.

As the project grows,

* Code becomes repetitive.
* Route prefixes are duplicated.
* Configuration must be added to every endpoint.
* Maintenance becomes harder.

With Route Groups

```csharp
var products = app.MapGroup("/api/products");

products.MapGet("/", ...);
products.MapGet("/{id}", ...);
products.MapPost("/", ...);
products.MapPut("/{id}", ...);
products.MapDelete("/{id}", ...);
```

Now the prefix is written only once.

### Benefits

* Reduces duplicate route prefixes.
* Organizes related endpoints.
* Improves code readability.
* Makes maintenance easier.
* Allows shared configuration.
* Supports cleaner Minimal API design.

---

# 4. How It Works

Suppose we create a Route Group.

```csharp
var products = app.MapGroup("/api/products");
```

Then add endpoints.

```csharp
products.MapGet("/", ...);

products.MapPost("/", ...);

products.MapGet("/{id}", ...);
```

Execution Flow

```text
Client
   │
HTTP Request
   │
Route Group (/api/products)
   │
Matching Endpoint
   │
Execute Handler
   │
Response
```

The Route Group automatically adds the common prefix to every endpoint inside the group.

---

# 5. Key Concepts

---

# 1. What is `MapGroup()`?

`MapGroup()` creates a Route Group.

Syntax

```csharp
var group = app.MapGroup("/routePrefix");
```

Example

```csharp
var products = app.MapGroup("/api/products");
```

Now every endpoint inside the group starts with

```text
/api/products
```

---

# 2. Creating Endpoints Inside a Group

Example

```csharp
var products = app.MapGroup("/api/products");

products.MapGet("/", () =>
{
    return "All Products";
});

products.MapGet("/{id}", (int id) =>
{
    return $"Product {id}";
});

products.MapPost("/", () =>
{
    return "Product Created";
});
```

Generated Routes

```text
GET    /api/products

GET    /api/products/{id}

POST   /api/products
```

Notice that the prefix is added automatically.

---

# 3. Shared Route Prefix

Without Route Group

```csharp
app.MapGet("/api/orders", ...);

app.MapPost("/api/orders", ...);

app.MapPut("/api/orders/{id}", ...);
```

With Route Group

```csharp
var orders = app.MapGroup("/api/orders");

orders.MapGet("/", ...);

orders.MapPost("/", ...);

orders.MapPut("/{id}", ...);
```

The route prefix is declared only once.

---

# 4. Nested Route Groups

Route Groups can be nested.

Example

```csharp
var api = app.MapGroup("/api");

var products = api.MapGroup("/products");
```

Now the final route becomes

```text
/api/products
```

Example

```csharp
products.MapGet("/", () =>
{
    return "Products";
});
```

Final URL

```text
GET /api/products
```

Nested groups make large APIs easier to organize.

---

# 5. Shared Authorization

Instead of applying authorization to every endpoint,

```csharp
products.MapGet("/", ...)
        .RequireAuthorization();

products.MapPost("/", ...)
        .RequireAuthorization();
```

we can write

```csharp
var products = app.MapGroup("/api/products")
                  .RequireAuthorization();
```

Now every endpoint inside the group requires authorization automatically.

---

# 6. Shared Tags (Swagger)

Tags organize endpoints in Swagger UI.

Example

```csharp
var products = app.MapGroup("/api/products")
                  .WithTags("Products");
```

Now every endpoint appears under the **Products** section in Swagger.

---

# 7. Shared Endpoint Filters

Instead of adding a filter to every endpoint,

```csharp
products.MapGet("/")
        .AddEndpointFilter<MyFilter>();
```

Apply it once.

```csharp
var products = app.MapGroup("/api/products")
                  .AddEndpointFilter<MyFilter>();
```

Every endpoint in the group now uses the filter.

---

# 8. Shared Metadata

Metadata can also be applied to the whole group.

Example

```csharp
var products = app.MapGroup("/api/products")
                  .WithOpenApi();
```

All endpoints inherit the OpenAPI configuration.

---

# 9. Versioning Using Route Groups

Example

```csharp
var v1 = app.MapGroup("/api/v1");
```

Inside

```csharp
var products = v1.MapGroup("/products");
```

Final Route

```text
/api/v1/products
```

Similarly

```csharp
var v2 = app.MapGroup("/api/v2");
```

creates

```text
/api/v2/products
```

This keeps API versions organized.

---

# 6. Code Example

```csharp
var products = app.MapGroup("/api/products")
                  .RequireAuthorization()
                  .WithTags("Products");

products.MapGet("/", () =>
{
    return "All Products";
});

products.MapGet("/{id}", (int id) =>
{
    return $"Product {id}";
});

products.MapPost("/", () =>
{
    return "Product Created";
});
```

---

# 7. Code Explanation

```csharp
var products = app.MapGroup("/api/products");
```

Creates a Route Group with the prefix `/api/products`.

---

```csharp
.RequireAuthorization()
```

Applies authorization to all endpoints in the group.

---

```csharp
.WithTags("Products")
```

Groups all endpoints under the **Products** section in Swagger.

---

```csharp
products.MapGet("/");
```

Creates

```text
GET /api/products
```

---

```csharp
products.MapGet("/{id}");
```

Creates

```text
GET /api/products/{id}
```

---

```csharp
products.MapPost("/");
```

Creates

```text
POST /api/products
```
