An **HTTP Verb Attribute** specifies which HTTP request method (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, etc.) should execute that action.

### One-Line Interview Definition

> An Action Method processes an HTTP request, and an HTTP Verb Attribute specifies which HTTP method can invoke that action.

---

# 3. Why Do We Use Action Methods?

Suppose a client sends

```http
GET /api/products
```

Without an action method,

* No code executes.
* No response is generated.

With an action method,

```csharp
[HttpGet]
public IActionResult GetAll()
{
    return Ok(products);
}
```

ASP.NET Core knows

* Which request to handle
* Which method to execute
* What response to return

### Benefits

* Separates different API operations.
* Supports RESTful APIs.
* Makes code organized.
* Easy to maintain.
* Easy to test.
* Supports routing.

---

# 4. How It Works

Suppose a client sends

```http
GET /api/products
```

Execution Flow

```text
Client
   │
HTTP Request
   │
Routing
   │
ProductsController
   │
[HttpGet]
   │
GetAll()
   │
Business Logic
   │
Response
   │
Client
```

ASP.NET Core checks

* URL
* HTTP Method
* Route

Then executes the matching action.

---

# 5. Key Concepts

---

# 1. What is an Action Method?

An Action Method is a public method inside a controller.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet]
public IActionResult GetAll()
{
    return Ok();
}
```

Here,

```text
GetAll()
```

is the Action Method.

---

Another Example

```csharp
[HttpPost]
public IActionResult Create(Product product)
{
    return Ok();
}
```

Here,

```text
Create()
```

is another Action Method.

---

# 2. Action Method Naming Rules

Action methods should

* Be public
* Return `IActionResult` (recommended)
* Have meaningful names
* Handle one operation

Good Examples

```csharp
GetAll()

GetById()

Create()

Update()

Delete()
```

Bad Examples

```csharp
Method1()

ABC()

Test()
```

Meaningful names improve readability.

---

# 3. What are HTTP Verb Attributes?

HTTP Verb Attributes tell ASP.NET Core which HTTP request should execute an action.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet]
```

means

```http
GET
```

request.

Example

```csharp
[HttpPost]
```

means

```http
POST
```

request.

Without these attributes, ASP.NET Core cannot correctly identify which action should handle a request when using attribute routing.

---

# 4. `[HttpGet]`

Used for reading data.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet]
public IActionResult GetAll()
{
    return Ok();
}
```

Request

```http
GET /api/products
```

Use Cases

* Get all records
* Get one record
* Search
* Filtering

GET should not modify data.

---

# 5. `[HttpPost]`

Used for creating new resources.

Example

```csharp
[HttpPost]
public IActionResult Create(Product product)
{
    return Created("", product);
}
```

Request

```http
POST /api/products
```

Use Cases

* Add Product
* Register User
* Create Order

POST creates new data.

---

# 6. `[HttpPut]`

Used for updating an entire resource.

Example

```csharp
[HttpPut("{id}")]
public IActionResult Update(int id, Product product)
{
    return NoContent();
}
```

Request

```http
PUT /api/products/5
```

PUT replaces the entire resource.

---

# 7. `[HttpPatch]`

Used for partially updating a resource.

Example

```csharp
[HttpPatch("{id}")]
public IActionResult UpdatePrice(int id)
{
    return NoContent();
}
```

Request

```http
PATCH /api/products/5
```

Only selected fields are updated.

---

# 8. `[HttpDelete]`

Used for deleting data.

Example

```csharp
[HttpDelete("{id}")]
public IActionResult Delete(int id)
{
    return NoContent();
}
```

Request

```http
DELETE /api/products/5
```

Deletes the specified resource.

---

# 9. `[HttpHead]`

Used for returning only HTTP headers.

Example

```csharp
[HttpHead("{id}")]
public IActionResult Head(int id)
{
    return Ok();
}
```

Response

* Headers returned
* No response body

Used to check whether a resource exists or to inspect metadata.

---

# 10. `[HttpOptions]`

Returns supported HTTP methods.

Example

```csharp
[HttpOptions]
public IActionResult Options()
{
    Response.Headers.Add("Allow", "GET,POST,PUT,DELETE");
    return Ok();
}
```

Response

```text
Allow:
GET, POST, PUT, DELETE
```

Useful for API discovery and CORS scenarios.

---

# 11. `[AcceptVerbs]`

Allows multiple HTTP methods.

Example

```csharp
[AcceptVerbs("GET", "POST")]
public IActionResult Process()
{
    return Ok();
}
```

This action accepts

* GET
* POST

requests.

---

# 12. Route Templates with HTTP Verb Attributes

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{id}")]
public IActionResult Get(int id)
```

Generated Route

```text
GET /api/products/5
```

---

Example

```csharp
[HttpDelete("{id}")]
```

Generated Route

```text
DELETE /api/products/5
```

Route templates can be combined with HTTP Verb Attributes to create clean RESTful endpoints.

---

# 13. Multiple HTTP Verb Attributes

An action can respond to more than one HTTP method.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet]
[HttpPost]
public IActionResult Test()
{
    return Ok();
}
```

or

```csharp
[AcceptVerbs("GET", "POST")]
```

Both allow GET and POST requests to execute the same action.

---

# 14. Action Selection Process

Suppose the client sends

```http
PUT /api/products/10
```

ASP.NET Core checks

```text
1. URL
        ↓
2. Controller
        ↓
3. Route
        ↓
4. HTTP Method
        ↓
5. Matching Action Method
        ↓
Execute Action
```

If no matching action exists,

ASP.NET Core returns

```http
405 Method Not Allowed
```

or

```http
404 Not Found
```

depending on the routing result.

---

# 6. Complete Code Example

```csharp
[ApiController]
[Route("api/[controller]")]
public class ProductsController : ControllerBase
{
    [HttpGet]
    public IActionResult GetAll()
    {
        return Ok("All Products");
    }

    [HttpGet("{id}")]
    public IActionResult GetById(int id)
    {
        return Ok($"Product {id}");
    }

    [HttpPost]
    public IActionResult Create(Product product)
    {
        return Created("", product);
    }

    [HttpPut("{id}")]
    public IActionResult Update(int id, Product product)
    {
        return NoContent();
    }

    [HttpDelete("{id}")]
    public IActionResult Delete(int id)
    {
        return NoContent();
    }
}
```

---

# 7. Code Explanation

```csharp
[HttpGet]
```

Handles GET requests to retrieve all products.

---

```csharp
[HttpGet("{id}")]
```

Handles GET requests for a specific product using its ID.

---

```csharp
[HttpPost]
```

Handles POST requests to create a new product.

---

```csharp
[HttpPut("{id}")]
```

Handles PUT requests to replace an existing product.

---

```csharp
[HttpDelete("{id}")]
```

Handles DELETE requests to remove a product.

Each HTTP Verb Attribute maps the corresponding HTTP request to the correct action method.

---

# 8. Real Project Usage ⭐

In enterprise ASP.NET Core Web API projects, action methods are organized based on REST operations. For example, a `ProductsController` typically contains `GetAll()`, `GetById()`, `Create()`, `Update()`, and `Delete()` actions decorated with `[HttpGet]`, `[HttpPost]`, `[HttpPut]`, and `[HttpDelete]`. Controllers receive client requests, call service classes to perform business logic, and return appropriate HTTP status codes such as **200 OK**, **201 Created**, **204 No Content**, or **404 Not Found**.

---

# 9. Best Practices

* Use the correct HTTP Verb Attribute for each operation.
* Keep one responsibility per action method.
* Return appropriate HTTP status codes.
* Use meaningful action names.
* Keep business logic inside service classes.
* Follow RESTful API conventions.

---

# 10. Common Mistakes

* Using `POST` for data retrieval.
* Using `GET` to modify data.
* Using incorrect HTTP Verb Attributes.
* Writing too much business logic inside action methods.
* Returning incorrect HTTP status codes.
* Creating multiple actions with the same route and HTTP method, causing ambiguity.

---

# 11. Key Points to Remember

* Action Methods handle incoming requests.
* HTTP Verb Attributes map HTTP methods to actions.
* `GET` → Read data.
* `POST` → Create data.
* `PUT` → Replace an entire resource.
* `PATCH` → Update part of a resource.
* `DELETE` → Remove a resource.
* `HEAD` returns only headers.
* `OPTIONS` returns supported HTTP methods.
* `AcceptVerbs` supports multiple HTTP methods.

---

# 12. Summary

Action Methods are the core methods inside ASP.NET Core controllers that process incoming HTTP requests and return responses. HTTP Verb Attributes such as `[HttpGet]`, `[HttpPost]`, `[HttpPut]`, `[HttpPatch]`, and `[HttpDelete]` define which HTTP methods can invoke each action. Together, they enable ASP.NET Core to build clean, RESTful, and maintainable Web APIs by correctly mapping client requests to the appropriate action methods.

----
# Module 4 – Controllers vs Minimal APIs

# Topic 2 – Action Methods and HTTP Verb Attributes (Part 2 – Interview Preparation)

---

# ⭐ Possible Interview Questions

## Q1. What is an Action Method in ASP.NET Core?

### Answer

An Action Method is a public method inside a controller that handles an incoming HTTP request and returns a response to the client. Each action method represents a specific operation, such as retrieving, creating, updating, or deleting data. ASP.NET Core selects the correct action based on the route and HTTP method. Action methods usually return `IActionResult` because it allows different types of HTTP responses like `Ok()`, `BadRequest()`, and `NotFound()`. In a well-designed application, action methods should only handle request processing and delegate business logic to service classes. This keeps controllers clean and maintainable.

---

## Q2. What are HTTP Verb Attributes?

### Answer

HTTP Verb Attributes are attributes that specify which HTTP request method can execute an action method. Common HTTP Verb Attributes include `[HttpGet]`, `[HttpPost]`, `[HttpPut]`, `[HttpPatch]`, `[HttpDelete]`, `[HttpHead]`, and `[HttpOptions]`. When a client sends a request, ASP.NET Core checks both the route and the HTTP method before selecting the action. These attributes help build RESTful APIs by clearly defining the purpose of each endpoint. They also improve readability and prevent incorrect request handling. Using the correct HTTP Verb Attribute is considered a best practice in API development.

---

## Q3. What is the difference between GET and POST?

### Answer

The **GET** method is used to retrieve data from the server without modifying it. It is considered a safe and read-only operation. The **POST** method is used to create new resources or submit data to the server. Unlike GET, POST changes the application's data. GET requests usually send parameters in the URL, while POST requests commonly send data in the request body. GET responses can often be cached, whereas POST responses are generally not cached. In REST APIs, GET is used for reading data, and POST is used for creating new records.

---

## Q4. What is the difference between PUT and PATCH?

### Answer

Both PUT and PATCH are used to update existing resources, but they work differently. **PUT** replaces the entire resource with the new data provided by the client. If some fields are omitted, they may be overwritten depending on the implementation. **PATCH** updates only the specified fields while leaving the remaining fields unchanged. PUT is suitable when the complete resource is available, whereas PATCH is better for partial updates. Using the correct method improves API design and follows REST principles. Many enterprise APIs use PATCH for small updates such as changing a user's email or status.

---

## Q5. What is the purpose of `[HttpDelete]`?

### Answer

`[HttpDelete]` is used to handle HTTP DELETE requests. It is typically used to remove an existing resource from the database. The client usually provides the resource ID in the route, such as `/api/products/5`. After successfully deleting the resource, the API commonly returns **204 No Content** or **200 OK**. If the resource does not exist, the API should return **404 Not Found**. Using `[HttpDelete]` makes the API more RESTful by clearly indicating that the endpoint is responsible for deletion.

---

## Q6. What is `[AcceptVerbs]`?

### Answer

`[AcceptVerbs]` is an attribute that allows an action method to handle multiple HTTP methods. Instead of applying multiple HTTP Verb Attributes like `[HttpGet]` and `[HttpPost]`, developers can write `[AcceptVerbs("GET", "POST")]`. ASP.NET Core allows both request types to execute the same action. This attribute is useful in specific scenarios where one action should support multiple HTTP methods. However, in RESTful APIs, it is generally recommended to keep separate action methods for different operations to improve readability and maintainability.

---

## Q7. How does ASP.NET Core select an Action Method?

### Answer

When an HTTP request reaches ASP.NET Core, it first passes through the middleware pipeline. The routing system then checks the request URL and finds the matching controller. After identifying the controller, ASP.NET Core checks the HTTP method such as GET, POST, PUT, or DELETE. It then looks for an action method with the matching HTTP Verb Attribute and route template. If a matching action is found, it is executed. If no suitable action exists, ASP.NET Core returns **404 Not Found** or **405 Method Not Allowed**, depending on the situation.

---

## Q8. Why should Action Methods be small?

### Answer

Action methods should focus only on receiving the request, validating input if necessary, calling the appropriate service, and returning the response. Writing business logic directly inside action methods makes controllers difficult to maintain and test. Keeping action methods small improves readability and follows the Single Responsibility Principle. It also makes unit testing easier because the business logic is separated into service classes. Thin controllers are considered a best practice in enterprise ASP.NET Core applications.

---

## Q9. Where have you used HTTP Verb Attributes in your project?

### Answer

In my ASP.NET Core Web API project, I used HTTP Verb Attributes to expose REST API endpoints. I used `[HttpGet]` to retrieve data, `[HttpPost]` to create new records, `[HttpPut]` to update existing records, and `[HttpDelete]` to remove records. Each controller contained action methods decorated with the appropriate HTTP Verb Attribute. The controllers called service classes to execute business logic and returned proper HTTP status codes like **200 OK**, **201 Created**, **204 No Content**, and **404 Not Found**. This made the API easy to understand and maintain.

---

# ⭐ Project-Based Interview Questions

## Q1. Why shouldn't business logic be written inside Action Methods?

### Answer

Action methods should only handle request processing and response generation. Business logic should be placed in service classes because it improves code organization, reusability, maintainability, and testability. This also follows the Single Responsibility Principle.

---

## Q2. Which HTTP method did you mostly use in your project?

### Answer

I mainly used **GET**, **POST**, **PUT**, and **DELETE** because they covered most CRUD operations. GET was used to retrieve data, POST to create records, PUT to update records, and DELETE to remove records. These methods followed RESTful API standards.

---

## Q3. What happens if a client sends a POST request to an action decorated with `[HttpGet]`?

### Answer

The request will not match the action because the HTTP methods are different. ASP.NET Core checks both the route and the HTTP method before executing an action. If no matching POST action exists for that route, the framework usually returns **405 Method Not Allowed**.

---

# ⭐ Rapid Fire Questions

1. What is an Action Method? → A public method that handles HTTP requests.
2. Which attribute handles GET requests? → `[HttpGet]`
3. Which attribute handles POST requests? → `[HttpPost]`
4. Which attribute handles PUT requests? → `[HttpPut]`
5. Which attribute handles PATCH requests? → `[HttpPatch]`
6. Which attribute handles DELETE requests? → `[HttpDelete]`
7. Which attribute supports multiple HTTP methods? → `[AcceptVerbs]`
8. Which HTTP method is used to retrieve data? → GET
9. Which HTTP method is used to create data? → POST
10. Which HTTP method is used to delete data? → DELETE

---

# ⭐ Follow-up Questions

## Q1. Can an Action Method have multiple HTTP Verb Attributes?

### Answer

Yes. An action method can have multiple HTTP Verb Attributes such as `[HttpGet]` and `[HttpPost]`, or it can use `[AcceptVerbs]` to support multiple HTTP methods. However, in RESTful APIs, separate action methods are generally preferred for better clarity and maintainability.

---

## Q2. What should an Action Method return?

### Answer

An Action Method should generally return `IActionResult` because it allows different types of HTTP responses such as `Ok()`, `BadRequest()`, `NotFound()`, `Created()`, and `NoContent()`. This provides flexibility and makes API responses more consistent.

---

# ⭐ Interview Tips

* Remember that an **Action Method** is a **public method inside a controller**.
* HTTP Verb Attributes determine **which HTTP request can execute the action**.
* Explain the difference between **PUT** and **PATCH** clearly.
* Mention that **GET should never modify data**.
* Say that **business logic belongs in service classes**, not in controllers.

---

# ⭐ Revision Notes

* Action Methods process HTTP requests.
* They are public methods inside controllers.
* HTTP Verb Attributes map requests to action methods.
* GET → Read data.
* POST → Create data.
* PUT → Replace an entire resource.
* PATCH → Update part of a resource.
* DELETE → Remove a resource.
* `AcceptVerbs` supports multiple HTTP methods.
* Use `IActionResult` for flexible HTTP responses.

---

# ⭐ Keywords

**Action Method, HTTP Verb Attributes, `[HttpGet]`, `[HttpPost]`, `[HttpPut]`, `[HttpPatch]`, `[HttpDelete]`, `[HttpHead]`, `[HttpOptions]`, `[AcceptVerbs]`, `IActionResult`, REST API, CRUD Operations, ASP.NET Core**

---
# Module 4 – Controllers vs Minimal APIs

# Topic 3 – Minimal APIs (`MapGet()`, `MapPost()`, etc.) (Part 1 – Learning)

---

# 1. Introduction

In previous topics, we learned that ASP.NET Core Web APIs are usually built using **Controllers**.

Example

```csharp
[ApiController]
[Route("api/[controller]")]
public class ProductsController : ControllerBase
{
    [HttpGet]
    public IActionResult GetAll()
    {
        return Ok();
    }
}
```

This approach is well-structured and suitable for medium and large applications.

However, in .NET 6 and later, Microsoft introduced **Minimal APIs**.

Minimal APIs allow developers to create API endpoints **without creating controllers**.

Instead of writing a controller class, endpoints are defined directly in **Program.cs**.

Example

```csharp
app.MapGet("/products", () =>
{
    return Results.Ok("All Products");
});
```

This approach requires much less code and is ideal for small APIs, prototypes, and microservices.

> **Interview Tip:** Minimal APIs remove the need for Controllers and ControllerBase. Endpoints are created directly in `Program.cs` using methods like `MapGet()` and `MapPost()`.

---

# 2. Definition

A **Minimal API** is a lightweight way to build HTTP APIs in ASP.NET Core without using controllers. Endpoints are defined directly in `Program.cs` using methods like `MapGet()`, `MapPost()`, `MapPut()`, and `MapDelete()`.

### One-Line Interview Definition

> Minimal APIs are a lightweight approach to creating ASP.NET Core APIs directly in `Program.cs` without using controllers.

---

# 3. Why Do We Use Minimal APIs?

Suppose we want to create one simple endpoint.

Using Controllers

```text
Create Controller
        │
Add Route
        │
Add Action Method
        │
Run API
```

Using Minimal API

```csharp
app.MapGet("/hello", () =>
{
    return "Hello World";
});
```

Only one statement is required.

### Benefits

* Less code
* Faster development
* Easy to learn
* Better for small APIs
* Better for prototypes
* Good for microservices

---

# 4. How It Works

Client Request

```http
GET /products
```

Execution Flow

```text
Client
   │
HTTP Request
   │
Routing
   │
MapGet()
   │
Lambda Function
   │
Return Response
   │
Client
```

ASP.NET Core matches the request directly with the endpoint defined in `Program.cs`.

---

# 5. Key Concepts

---

# 1. What is a Minimal API?

Minimal APIs allow developers to build APIs without controllers.

Example

```csharp
var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args);

var app = builder.Build();

app.MapGet("/", () => "Hello World");

app.Run();
```

No controller is required.

---

# 2. Where are Minimal APIs Written?

They are usually written inside

```text
Program.cs
```

Example

```csharp
var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args);

var app = builder.Build();

app.MapGet("/products", () =>
{
    return Results.Ok();
});

app.Run();
```

Everything is configured in one file.

---

# 3. `MapGet()`

Used for reading data.

Example

```csharp
app.MapGet("/products", () =>
{
    return Results.Ok("All Products");
});
```

Request

```http
GET /products
```

Response

```http
200 OK
```

---

# 4. `MapPost()`

Used for creating data.

Example

```csharp
app.MapPost("/products", (Product product) =>
{
    return Results.Created("/products/1", product);
});
```

Request

```http
POST /products
```

Creates a new resource.

---

# 5. `MapPut()`

Used for updating an entire resource.

Example

```csharp
app.MapPut("/products/{id}", (int id, Product product) =>
{
    return Results.NoContent();
});
```

Request

```http
PUT /products/5
```

Updates the complete resource.

---

# 6. `MapPatch()`

Used for partially updating data.

Example

```csharp
app.MapPatch("/products/{id}", (int id) =>
{
    return Results.NoContent();
});
```

Request

```http
PATCH /products/5
```

Updates only selected fields.

---

# 7. `MapDelete()`

Used for deleting data.

Example

```csharp
app.MapDelete("/products/{id}", (int id) =>
{
    return Results.NoContent();
});
```

Request

```http
DELETE /products/5
```

Deletes the resource.

---

# 8. Route Parameters

Minimal APIs support route parameters.

Example

```csharp
app.MapGet("/products/{id}", (int id) =>
{
    return Results.Ok(id);
});
```

Request

```http
GET /products/10
```

Output

```text
10
```

ASP.NET Core automatically binds the route value to the parameter.

---

# 9. Query Parameters

Example

```csharp
app.MapGet("/search", (string name) =>
{
    return Results.Ok(name);
});
```

Request

```http
GET /search?name=Laptop
```

Output

```text
Laptop
```

The query parameter is automatically bound.

---

# 10. Dependency Injection

Minimal APIs support Dependency Injection.

Example

```csharp
app.MapGet("/products", (IProductService service) =>
{
    return service.GetProducts();
});
```

ASP.NET Core injects the service automatically.

---

# 11. Results Class

Instead of `ControllerBase` methods like

```csharp
Ok()
```

Minimal APIs use

```csharp
Results.Ok()
```

Common methods

```text
Results.Ok()

Results.Created()

Results.BadRequest()

Results.NotFound()

Results.NoContent()

Results.Unauthorized()
```

These return standard HTTP responses.

---

# 12. Controllers vs Minimal APIs

| Controllers                                | Minimal APIs                   |
| ------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------ |
| Class-based                                | Function-based                 |
| Uses ControllerBase                        | No Controller                  |
| Uses Action Methods                        | Uses Lambda Expressions        |
| More code                                  | Less code                      |
| Better for large projects                  | Better for small projects      |
| Highly organized                           | Simple and lightweight         |
| Supports controller filters and attributes | Simpler endpoint configuration |

---

# 13. When Should You Use Minimal APIs?

Use Minimal APIs when:

* Building small applications
* Creating prototypes
* Building internal APIs
* Building microservices
* Creating simple CRUD APIs
* Rapid development is important

Avoid using Minimal APIs when:

* The application is very large.
* Many controllers and endpoints are required.
* Complex architecture and organization are needed.

---

# 6. Complete Code Example

```csharp
var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args);

var app = builder.Build();

app.MapGet("/products", () =>
{
    return Results.Ok(new[]
    {
        "Laptop",
        "Mouse",
        "Keyboard"
    });
});

app.MapGet("/products/{id}", (int id) =>
{
    return Results.Ok($"Product {id}");
});

app.MapPost("/products", (Product product) =>
{
    return Results.Created("/products/1", product);
});

app.MapPut("/products/{id}", (int id, Product product) =>
{
    return Results.NoContent();
});

app.MapDelete("/products/{id}", (int id) =>
{
    return Results.NoContent();
});

app.Run();
```

---

# 7. Code Explanation

```csharp
app.MapGet("/products", ...)
```

Creates a GET endpoint for retrieving products.

---

```csharp
app.MapPost("/products", ...)
```

Creates a POST endpoint for adding a new product.

---

```csharp
app.MapPut("/products/{id}", ...)
```

Updates an existing product.

---

```csharp
app.MapDelete("/products/{id}", ...)
```

Deletes a product.

---

```csharp
Results.Ok()
```

Returns **200 OK**.

---

```csharp
Results.Created()
```

Returns **201 Created**.

---

```csharp
Results.NoContent()
```

Returns **204 No Content**.

---

# 8. Real Project Usage ⭐

In enterprise development, Minimal APIs are commonly used for **small internal services**, **microservices**, **health check endpoints**, **utility APIs**, and **proof-of-concept (POC)** applications where a full controller structure is unnecessary. Larger business applications with many modules usually prefer Controllers because they provide better organization, scalability, and support for advanced features.

---

# 9. Best Practices

* Use Minimal APIs for small and simple applications.
* Keep endpoint logic short and readable.
* Move business logic to service classes.
* Use Dependency Injection instead of creating objects manually.
* Return proper HTTP status codes using the `Results` class.
* Use meaningful route names.

---

# 10. Common Mistakes

* Writing large amounts of business logic inside `MapGet()` or `MapPost()`.
* Using Minimal APIs for very large enterprise applications.
* Forgetting to register required services before using Dependency Injection.
* Returning incorrect HTTP status codes.
* Creating too many endpoints in a single `Program.cs`, making it difficult to maintain.

---

# 11. Key Points to Remember

* Minimal APIs were introduced in **.NET 6**.
* They do not require Controllers or `ControllerBase`.
* Endpoints are created in `Program.cs`.
* `MapGet()` handles GET requests.
* `MapPost()` handles POST requests.
* `MapPut()` handles PUT requests.
* `MapPatch()` handles PATCH requests.
* `MapDelete()` handles DELETE requests.
* The `Results` class is used to return HTTP responses.
* Minimal APIs are best suited for small applications and microservices.

---

# 12. Summary

Minimal APIs provide a lightweight and modern way to build HTTP APIs in ASP.NET Core without using controllers. They define endpoints directly in `Program.cs` using methods such as `MapGet()`, `MapPost()`, `MapPut()`, `MapPatch()`, and `MapDelete()`. They require less code, support Dependency Injection, route binding, and standard HTTP responses through the `Results` class. While they are excellent for small applications, prototypes, and microservices, Controllers remain the preferred choice for large enterprise applications due to their better organization and scalability.

---
# Module 4 – Controllers vs Minimal APIs

# Topic 3 – Minimal APIs (`MapGet()`, `MapPost()`, etc.) (Part 2 – Interview Preparation)

---

# ⭐ Possible Interview Questions

## Q1. What are Minimal APIs in ASP.NET Core?

### Answer

Minimal APIs are a lightweight way to build HTTP APIs in ASP.NET Core without creating controllers. They were introduced in **.NET 6** to simplify API development. Instead of defining controller classes and action methods, endpoints are created directly in `Program.cs` using methods like `MapGet()`, `MapPost()`, `MapPut()`, and `MapDelete()`. This approach reduces boilerplate code and speeds up development. Minimal APIs support routing, model binding, dependency injection, and standard HTTP responses. They are best suited for small applications, prototypes, internal APIs, and microservices.

---

## Q2. Why were Minimal APIs introduced?

### Answer

Minimal APIs were introduced to simplify API development in ASP.NET Core. Earlier, even simple APIs required creating controllers, action methods, and multiple files. With Minimal APIs, developers can define endpoints directly in `Program.cs` using only a few lines of code. This reduces development time and makes the code easier to understand for small projects. They are also useful for rapid prototyping and microservices where a full controller structure is unnecessary. Although they reduce code, they still support important ASP.NET Core features like routing and dependency injection.

---

## Q3. What is `MapGet()`?

### Answer

`MapGet()` is a method used in Minimal APIs to create an endpoint that handles HTTP GET requests. It maps a URL to a function or lambda expression. When a client sends a GET request to the specified route, ASP.NET Core executes the associated code and returns the response. `MapGet()` is commonly used to retrieve data from the application. It replaces the combination of `[HttpGet]` and an action method used in controllers. It is simple to write and works well for lightweight APIs.

---

## Q4. What is the difference between Controllers and Minimal APIs?

### Answer

Controllers are class-based and use action methods to handle requests, while Minimal APIs are function-based and define endpoints directly in `Program.cs`. Controllers provide better organization, filters, attributes, and scalability, making them suitable for large enterprise applications. Minimal APIs require less code and are easier to create, making them ideal for small projects, prototypes, and microservices. Controllers use `ControllerBase` and methods like `Ok()`, whereas Minimal APIs use the `Results` class. Both approaches support dependency injection and routing, but Controllers offer better structure for complex applications.

---

## Q5. What is the `Results` class in Minimal APIs?

### Answer

The `Results` class is used to return HTTP responses in Minimal APIs. Since Minimal APIs do not inherit from `ControllerBase`, helper methods like `Ok()` and `BadRequest()` are not available directly. Instead, developers use methods such as `Results.Ok()`, `Results.Created()`, `Results.BadRequest()`, `Results.NotFound()`, and `Results.NoContent()`. These methods return standard HTTP status codes along with optional response data. Using the `Results` class keeps responses consistent and follows REST API standards.

---

## Q6. Do Minimal APIs support Dependency Injection?

### Answer

Yes. Minimal APIs fully support Dependency Injection. Services registered in the dependency injection container can be injected directly into endpoint parameters. ASP.NET Core automatically resolves the required service and passes it to the endpoint. This allows developers to separate business logic into service classes instead of writing it inside the endpoint. Dependency Injection in Minimal APIs works similarly to controllers and is one of the reasons they are suitable for real-world applications.

---

## Q7. When should you use Minimal APIs?

### Answer

Minimal APIs should be used when building small applications, internal APIs, prototypes, proof-of-concept projects, or microservices. They are useful when the application has only a few endpoints and does not require the full structure of controllers. Because they require less code, development is faster and simpler. However, for large enterprise applications with many modules and complex business logic, Controllers are generally preferred because they provide better organization and scalability. Choosing between Controllers and Minimal APIs depends on the project size and requirements.

---

## Q8. Can Minimal APIs perform CRUD operations?

### Answer

Yes. Minimal APIs can perform all CRUD operations. Developers use `MapGet()` to retrieve data, `MapPost()` to create new records, `MapPut()` to update existing records, `MapPatch()` to partially update records, and `MapDelete()` to remove records. Each method corresponds to an HTTP request method and behaves similarly to controller action methods. This makes Minimal APIs suitable for creating complete RESTful APIs, especially for smaller applications.

---

## Q9. Where have you used or where would you use Minimal APIs?

### Answer

I would use Minimal APIs for small internal tools, microservices, health check endpoints, utility APIs, or proof-of-concept applications where only a few endpoints are required. For large enterprise projects with multiple modules, authentication, and complex business logic, I would prefer Controllers because they provide better structure and maintainability. Choosing the right approach depends on the project's complexity and future scalability.

---

# ⭐ Project-Based Interview Questions

## Q1. Why would you choose Controllers over Minimal APIs?

### Answer

I would choose Controllers for large applications because they provide better organization, support attributes and filters, and make it easier to separate responsibilities. Controllers are also easier to maintain when there are many endpoints and developers working on the same project.

---

## Q2. Why would you choose Minimal APIs?

### Answer

I would choose Minimal APIs for small applications, internal services, prototypes, or microservices because they require less code and can be developed quickly. They are simple to understand and still support routing, dependency injection, and standard HTTP responses.

---

## Q3. Can you move business logic into a Minimal API endpoint?

### Answer

Technically yes, but it is not recommended. Just like controllers, Minimal API endpoints should remain thin and delegate business logic to service classes. This keeps the application clean, reusable, and easy to maintain.

---

# ⭐ Rapid Fire Questions

1. In which .NET version were Minimal APIs introduced? → **.NET 6**
2. Where are Minimal APIs usually written? → **Program.cs**
3. Which method handles GET requests? → `MapGet()`
4. Which method handles POST requests? → `MapPost()`
5. Which method handles PUT requests? → `MapPut()`
6. Which method handles PATCH requests? → `MapPatch()`
7. Which method handles DELETE requests? → `MapDelete()`
8. Which class returns HTTP responses? → `Results`
9. Do Minimal APIs support Dependency Injection? → **Yes**
10. Are Controllers required for Minimal APIs? → **No**

---

# ⭐ Follow-up Questions

## Q1. Can Controllers and Minimal APIs exist in the same project?

### Answer

Yes. ASP.NET Core allows Controllers and Minimal APIs to be used together in the same application. Developers often use Controllers for major business modules and Minimal APIs for simple endpoints such as health checks or internal utility APIs.

---

## Q2. Are Minimal APIs suitable for enterprise applications?

### Answer

They can be used in enterprise applications for small independent services or utility endpoints. However, for large business modules with many endpoints, Controllers are generally preferred because they offer better organization, scalability, and maintainability.

---

# ⭐ Interview Tips

* Mention that **Minimal APIs were introduced in .NET 6**.
* Remember that they **do not require Controllers or `ControllerBase`**.
* Explain that **`MapGet()` is equivalent to `[HttpGet]` in Controllers**.
* Mention the **`Results` class** for returning HTTP responses.
* Say that **Controllers are generally preferred for large enterprise applications**, while Minimal APIs are better for small projects and microservices.

---

# ⭐ Revision Notes

* Minimal APIs are a lightweight API development approach.
* Introduced in **.NET 6**.
* Endpoints are created in `Program.cs`.
* `MapGet()` handles GET requests.
* `MapPost()` handles POST requests.
* `MapPut()` handles PUT requests.
* `MapPatch()` handles PATCH requests.
* `MapDelete()` handles DELETE requests.
* `Results` returns HTTP responses.
* Dependency Injection is fully supported.
* Best for small APIs, prototypes, and microservices.

---

# ⭐ Keywords

**Minimal APIs, `MapGet()`, `MapPost()`, `MapPut()`, `MapPatch()`, `MapDelete()`, `Program.cs`, `Results`, Dependency Injection, Routing, REST API, .NET 6, Controllers vs Minimal APIs**

---
# Module 4 – Controllers vs Minimal APIs

# Topic 4 – When to use Controllers vs Minimal APIs (Part 1 – Learning)

---

# 1. Introduction

ASP.NET Core provides **two ways** to build Web APIs:

* Controllers
* Minimal APIs

Both can create RESTful APIs and support features like routing, dependency injection, model binding, and returning HTTP responses.

However, they are designed for different scenarios.

For example, a small application with only a few endpoints can use **Minimal APIs**.

```csharp
app.MapGet("/products", () =>
{
    return Results.Ok("All Products");
});
```

A large enterprise application usually uses **Controllers**.

```csharp
[ApiController]
[Route("api/[controller]")]
public class ProductsController : ControllerBase
{
    [HttpGet]
    public IActionResult GetAll()
    {
        return Ok("All Products");
    }
}
```

Choosing the correct approach depends on the project size, complexity, and future maintenance.

> **Interview Tip:** Minimal APIs are best for **small applications**, while Controllers are generally preferred for **large enterprise applications**.

---

# 2. Definition

**Controllers** are class-based components that organize API endpoints using action methods.

**Minimal APIs** are function-based endpoints created directly in `Program.cs` without controllers.

### One-Line Interview Definition

> Controllers provide a structured approach for large applications, while Minimal APIs provide a lightweight approach for small applications.

---

# 3. Why Do We Need Both?

Not every application has the same requirements.

Example 1

A Health Check API

```text
Only 2 Endpoints
```

Using Controllers may add unnecessary files.

Minimal APIs are simpler.

---

Example 2

An E-Commerce System

```text
Products
Orders
Customers
Payments
Authentication
Reports
```

Hundreds of endpoints.

Controllers provide better organization.

### Benefits of Choosing the Right Approach

* Better maintainability
* Cleaner code
* Easier scalability
* Faster development
* Better project organization

---

# 4. How It Works

Small Project

```text
Client
    │
Minimal API
    │
Program.cs
    │
Response
```

---

Large Project

```text
Client
    │
Routing
    │
Controller
    │
Action Method
    │
Service
    │
Database
    │
Response
```

Both produce the same HTTP response but use different application structures.

---

# 5. Key Concepts

---

# 1. When to Use Controllers

Controllers are recommended when:

* Large applications
* Enterprise projects
* Multiple modules
* Many developers
* Complex business logic
* Better code organization
* Long-term maintenance
* Large REST APIs

Example

```text
Banking System

Products

Orders

Payments

Customers

Employees

Reports
```

Controllers organize all modules properly.

---

# 2. When to Use Minimal APIs

Minimal APIs are recommended when:

* Small applications
* Prototype applications
* Internal APIs
* Utility APIs
* Microservices
* Health Check APIs
* Learning ASP.NET Core
* Rapid development

Example

```text
Health Check API

2 Endpoints
```

Minimal APIs are enough.

---

# 3. Project Size Comparison

| Small Project      | Large Project         |
| ------------------ | --------------------- |
| Minimal APIs       | Controllers           |
| Less code          | More organized        |
| Few endpoints      | Hundreds of endpoints |
| Faster development | Easier maintenance    |
| Good for learning  | Good for enterprise   |

---

# 4. Code Comparison

### Controller

```csharp
[ApiController]
[Route("api/[controller]")]
public class ProductsController : ControllerBase
{
    [HttpGet]
    public IActionResult GetAll()
    {
        return Ok("Products");
    }
}
```

---

### Minimal API

```csharp
app.MapGet("/products", () =>
{
    return Results.Ok("Products");
});
```

Both return

```http
200 OK
```

The difference is the application structure.

---

# 5. Folder Structure Comparison

### Controllers

```text
Controllers
    ProductsController.cs
    OrdersController.cs
    CustomersController.cs

Services

Repositories

Models
```

Highly organized.

---

### Minimal APIs

```text
Program.cs
```

Everything starts in one place.

For larger applications, developers often separate endpoints into extension methods or endpoint classes to keep the project maintainable.

---

# 6. Readability

Small API

```text
10 Endpoints
```

Minimal APIs are easy to read.

---

Large API

```text
250 Endpoints
```

Controllers are much easier to manage.

---

# 7. Maintainability

Controllers

* Easier to maintain
* Easier to debug
* Easier to extend
* Better separation of concerns

Minimal APIs

* Easy for small projects
* Can become difficult to maintain if too many endpoints are placed in `Program.cs`

---

# 8. Performance

Many developers ask:

Which is faster?

In practice,

* Both are very fast.
* For most business applications, the performance difference is negligible.
* The choice should be based on project complexity and maintainability rather than speed.

---

# 9. Can Both Be Used Together?

Yes.

ASP.NET Core allows both approaches in the same application.

Example

```text
Controllers

Products

Orders

Customers
```

---

Minimal APIs

```text
Health Check

Ping API

Version API
```

This is common in real projects.

---

# 10. Microsoft Recommendation

Microsoft supports both Controllers and Minimal APIs.

General guidance:

* Use Controllers for medium and large applications.
* Use Minimal APIs for simple applications, prototypes, internal tools, and microservices.

The choice depends on your application's requirements.

---

# 11. Decision Table

| Scenario               | Recommended Approach |
| ---------------------- | -------------------- |
| Learning ASP.NET Core  | Minimal APIs         |
| Prototype              | Minimal APIs         |
| Internal Tool          | Minimal APIs         |
| Health Check API       | Minimal APIs         |
| Microservice           | Minimal APIs         |
| Small CRUD API         | Minimal APIs         |
| Enterprise Application | Controllers          |
| Banking Application    | Controllers          |
| ERP System             | Controllers          |
| Large REST API         | Controllers          |

---

# 6. Complete Code Example

## Controller Example

```csharp
[ApiController]
[Route("api/[controller]")]
public class ProductsController : ControllerBase
{
    [HttpGet]
    public IActionResult GetProducts()
    {
        return Ok("Products");
    }
}
```

---

## Minimal API Example

```csharp
var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args);

var app = builder.Build();

app.MapGet("/products", () =>
{
    return Results.Ok("Products");
});

app.Run();
```

---

# 7. Code Explanation

### Controller Example

```csharp
public class ProductsController : ControllerBase
```

Creates a controller class.

---

```csharp
[HttpGet]
```

Handles GET requests.

---

```csharp
return Ok("Products");
```

Returns **200 OK**.

---

### Minimal API Example

```csharp
app.MapGet("/products", ...)
```

Creates a GET endpoint directly in `Program.cs`.

---

```csharp
Results.Ok("Products")
```

Returns **200 OK**.

Both implementations return the same response but use different development styles.

---

# 8. Real Project Usage ⭐

In enterprise ASP.NET Core applications, **Controllers** are commonly used for core business modules such as **Products**, **Customers**, **Orders**, and **Payments** because they provide better organization and scalability. **Minimal APIs** are often used alongside Controllers for simple endpoints like **health checks**, **application version**, **status**, or lightweight internal utility APIs. Using both approaches together helps keep the application clean while choosing the right tool for each scenario.

---

# 9. Best Practices

* Use Controllers for medium and large applications.
* Use Minimal APIs for small applications and prototypes.
* Keep business logic in service classes.
* Organize controllers by feature or module.
* Avoid putting many endpoints directly in `Program.cs`.
* Choose the approach based on project requirements, not just the amount of code.

---

# 10. Common Mistakes

* Using Minimal APIs for very large enterprise applications without proper organization.
* Creating hundreds of endpoints in `Program.cs`.
* Choosing Minimal APIs only because they require less code.
* Writing business logic directly inside Controllers or Minimal API endpoints.
* Assuming Minimal APIs always perform better than Controllers.

---

# 11. Key Points to Remember

* ASP.NET Core supports both Controllers and Minimal APIs.
* Controllers are class-based.
* Minimal APIs are function-based.
* Controllers are better for large applications.
* Minimal APIs are better for small applications.
* Both support routing and Dependency Injection.
* Both can exist in the same project.
* Choose based on maintainability and project complexity.

---

# 12. Summary

Controllers and Minimal APIs are two supported approaches for building ASP.NET Core Web APIs. Controllers provide a structured, class-based architecture that is well suited for medium and large enterprise applications with many modules and complex business logic. Minimal APIs offer a lightweight, function-based approach that reduces boilerplate code and is ideal for small applications, prototypes, internal tools, and microservices. Both approaches support modern ASP.NET Core features such as routing, dependency injection, and standard HTTP responses, and they can even be used together within the same application.

---
# Module 4 – Controllers vs Minimal APIs

# Topic 4 – When to Use Controllers vs Minimal APIs (Part 2 – Interview Preparation)

---

# ⭐ Possible Interview Questions

## Q1. What is the difference between Controllers and Minimal APIs?

### Answer

Controllers are class-based components that organize API endpoints using action methods, while Minimal APIs define endpoints directly in `Program.cs` without creating controllers. Controllers provide better structure, attributes, filters, and organization, making them suitable for large enterprise applications. Minimal APIs require less code and are easier to develop, making them ideal for small applications and prototypes. Both support routing, dependency injection, and model binding. Controllers usually inherit from `ControllerBase`, whereas Minimal APIs use methods like `MapGet()` and `MapPost()`. The choice depends on the size and complexity of the project.

---

## Q2. When should you use Controllers?

### Answer

Controllers should be used when building medium or large ASP.NET Core applications. They are suitable for projects with multiple modules such as Products, Orders, Customers, and Payments. Controllers provide better code organization by grouping related endpoints together. They also support attributes, filters, model validation, and better separation of concerns. Since business logic is usually placed in service classes, controllers remain clean and maintainable. They are easier to extend, debug, and test. Most enterprise applications prefer Controllers because they scale well as the project grows.

---

## Q3. When should you use Minimal APIs?

### Answer

Minimal APIs should be used for small applications, prototypes, internal tools, utility APIs, health check endpoints, and microservices. They allow developers to create API endpoints with very little code. Since endpoints are defined directly in `Program.cs`, development is faster and simpler. Minimal APIs also support routing, dependency injection, and model binding. They are a good choice when only a few endpoints are required. However, if the project grows significantly, maintaining all endpoints in one place can become difficult. Therefore, they are best suited for lightweight applications.

---

## Q4. Which approach is better for enterprise applications?

### Answer

For enterprise applications, Controllers are generally the better choice. Enterprise projects usually contain many modules, complex business logic, authentication, authorization, logging, and validation. Controllers help organize these features into separate classes, making the code easier to maintain and extend. They also support filters and attributes, which are commonly used in large projects. Although Minimal APIs can be used in enterprise applications for simple endpoints, Controllers are preferred for the main business modules because they provide better scalability and maintainability.

---

## Q5. Can Controllers and Minimal APIs exist in the same project?

### Answer

Yes. ASP.NET Core allows Controllers and Minimal APIs to be used together in the same project. Many applications use Controllers for core business modules such as Products, Orders, and Customers. At the same time, they use Minimal APIs for simple endpoints like health checks, version information, or internal utility APIs. This approach allows developers to choose the most suitable option for each scenario. Using both together is fully supported by ASP.NET Core and is common in real-world applications.

---

## Q6. Are Minimal APIs faster than Controllers?

### Answer

Minimal APIs have slightly less framework overhead because they avoid controller creation and some MVC components. However, in most business applications, the performance difference is very small and usually not noticeable. Database operations, external API calls, and business logic generally have a much greater impact on performance than the choice between Controllers and Minimal APIs. Therefore, developers should choose the approach based on maintainability and project requirements rather than performance alone.

---

## Q7. What factors do you consider before choosing Controllers or Minimal APIs?

### Answer

Before choosing an approach, I consider the project size, complexity, number of endpoints, future scalability, and maintenance requirements. If the application is small with only a few endpoints, I prefer Minimal APIs because they require less code. If the application is large with multiple modules and long-term maintenance, I prefer Controllers because they provide better organization. I also consider the development team size and whether advanced features like filters or structured architecture are required. The final decision depends on the project's needs.

---

## Q8. What would you choose for a Health Check API?

### Answer

For a Health Check API, I would choose Minimal APIs because they are simple and require very little code. Health check endpoints usually perform only one task, such as returning the application status. Creating a complete controller for one or two endpoints would add unnecessary complexity. Using `MapGet()` makes the implementation cleaner and easier to maintain. This is a common approach in modern ASP.NET Core applications.

---

## Q9. Which approach would you use in your project?

### Answer

For a large ASP.NET Core Web API project, I would use Controllers because they provide better organization and maintainability. Controllers make it easier to separate modules such as Products, Customers, Orders, and Payments into different classes. If the project also required simple endpoints such as health checks or application version APIs, I would use Minimal APIs for those endpoints. Using both approaches together provides a clean and practical solution.

---

# ⭐ Project-Based Interview Questions

## Q1. Why would you choose Controllers for your project?

### Answer

I would choose Controllers because my project contains multiple modules and business operations. Controllers help organize endpoints into separate classes, making the code easier to understand, maintain, and test. They also work well with dependency injection, filters, and validation.

---

## Q2. Would you use Minimal APIs for every project?

### Answer

No. I would use Minimal APIs only when the application is small or has a limited number of endpoints. For large enterprise applications with many modules, Controllers are a better choice because they provide better structure and long-term maintainability.

---

## Q3. Can Minimal APIs replace Controllers completely?

### Answer

No. Minimal APIs can handle many scenarios, but Controllers are still the preferred choice for large enterprise applications because they offer better organization, scalability, and support for advanced MVC features. Both approaches are valuable, and the choice depends on the project requirements.

---

# ⭐ Rapid Fire Questions

1. Which approach is class-based? → **Controllers**
2. Which approach is function-based? → **Minimal APIs**
3. Where are Minimal APIs usually written? → **Program.cs**
4. Which approach is better for enterprise applications? → **Controllers**
5. Which approach is better for prototypes? → **Minimal APIs**
6. Which approach is better for health check APIs? → **Minimal APIs**
7. Can Controllers and Minimal APIs be used together? → **Yes**
8. Which approach requires less code? → **Minimal APIs**
9. Which approach provides better organization? → **Controllers**
10. Which approach is generally better for long-term maintenance? → **Controllers**

---

# ⭐ Follow-up Questions

## Q1. Can you convert a Minimal API into a Controller later?

### Answer

Yes. Since both approaches use the same ASP.NET Core framework, a Minimal API endpoint can later be moved into a controller if the application grows. This allows developers to start with a simple implementation and reorganize the code as needed.

---

## Q2. Does choosing Minimal APIs mean you cannot use Dependency Injection?

### Answer

No. Minimal APIs fully support Dependency Injection. Services registered in the dependency injection container can be injected directly into endpoint parameters, just like they are injected into controllers.

---

# ⭐ Interview Tips

* Remember that **there is no universally "better" approach**.
* Say **Controllers are preferred for medium and large enterprise applications**.
* Say **Minimal APIs are ideal for small applications, prototypes, internal tools, and microservices**.
* Mention that **both support routing, model binding, and Dependency Injection**.
* Explain that **both approaches can exist together in the same project**.

---

# ⭐ Revision Notes

* Controllers are class-based.
* Minimal APIs are function-based.
* Controllers use `ControllerBase`.
* Minimal APIs use `MapGet()`, `MapPost()`, etc.
* Controllers are best for large applications.
* Minimal APIs are best for small applications.
* Both support Dependency Injection.
* Both support routing and model binding.
* Both can be used together in one project.
* Choose based on maintainability and project requirements.

---

# ⭐ Keywords

**Controllers, Minimal APIs, `ControllerBase`, `Program.cs`, `MapGet()`, `MapPost()`, Enterprise Applications, Microservices, Health Check API, Dependency Injection, Routing, Scalability, Maintainability**

----
# Module 4 – Controllers vs Minimal APIs

# Topic 5 – Organizing Endpoints at Scale (Part 1 – Learning)

---

# 1. Introduction

When we first start building an ASP.NET Core Web API, we usually have only a few endpoints.

Example

```csharp
app.MapGet("/products", () => Results.Ok());

app.MapPost("/products", () => Results.Ok());
```

This is simple and easy to understand.

However, as the application grows, we may have hundreds of endpoints.

For example:

* Products
* Customers
* Orders
* Payments
* Authentication
* Reports
* Notifications

If all endpoints are written in **Program.cs**, the file becomes very large and difficult to maintain.

To solve this problem, ASP.NET Core provides different ways to organize endpoints.

> **Interview Tip:** As the application grows, endpoints should be organized into separate files or feature-based modules instead of keeping everything in `Program.cs`.

---

# 2. Definition

**Organizing endpoints at scale** means structuring API endpoints into separate files, groups, or modules so that large applications remain clean, maintainable, and easy to understand.

### One-Line Interview Definition

> Organizing endpoints at scale means separating API endpoints into logical modules instead of placing all endpoints in one file.

---

# 3. Why Do We Organize Endpoints?

Imagine an API with only two endpoints.

```text
Program.cs

MapGet()

MapPost()
```

Easy to manage.

Now imagine a real project.

```text
Products

20 Endpoints

Customers

15 Endpoints

Orders

25 Endpoints

Payments

18 Endpoints

Reports

12 Endpoints

Authentication

15 Endpoints
```

If every endpoint is inside one file,

* Finding code becomes difficult.
* Debugging takes longer.
* Multiple developers may edit the same file, causing merge conflicts.
* Maintenance becomes harder.

Organizing endpoints solves these problems.

### Benefits

* Cleaner code
* Better readability
* Easier debugging
* Better teamwork
* Easier maintenance
* Better scalability

---

# 4. How It Works

Without Organization

```text
Program.cs

MapGet()

MapPost()

MapPut()

MapDelete()

...

300+ Endpoints
```

↓

Very difficult to manage.

---

With Organization

```text
Program.cs

↓

Products Endpoints

↓

Customer Endpoints

↓

Order Endpoints

↓

Payment Endpoints
```

Each module manages only its own endpoints.

---

# 5. Key Concepts

---

# 1. Small Project Organization

Small APIs may keep everything inside `Program.cs`.

Example

```csharp
app.MapGet("/products", () => Results.Ok());

app.MapPost("/products", () => Results.Ok());
```

This is acceptable when there are only a few endpoints.

---

# 2. Large Project Organization

Large applications should separate endpoints.

Example

```text
Products

Customers

Orders

Payments

Reports
```

Each feature should have its own endpoint definitions.

---

# 3. Feature-Based Organization

Instead of organizing by HTTP method,

Organize by business feature.

Example

```text
Features

    Products

    Customers

    Orders

    Payments
```

Each feature contains its own endpoints, services, models, and business logic.

This approach is common in enterprise applications.

---

# 4. Endpoint Extension Methods

A common approach is to move endpoint registration into extension methods.

Example

**ProductEndpoints.cs**

```csharp
public static class ProductEndpoints
{
    public static void MapProductEndpoints(this WebApplication app)
    {
        app.MapGet("/products", () =>
        {
            return Results.Ok("Products");
        });

        app.MapPost("/products", () =>
        {
            return Results.Ok();
        });
    }
}
```

**Program.cs**

```csharp
var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args);

var app = builder.Build();

app.MapProductEndpoints();

app.Run();
```

`Program.cs` remains clean while endpoint definitions are stored separately.

---

# 5. Route Groups

Route Groups help organize related endpoints.

Example

```csharp
var products = app.MapGroup("/products");

products.MapGet("/", () =>
{
    return Results.Ok();
});

products.MapPost("/", () =>
{
    return Results.Ok();
});
```

All product endpoints are grouped together.

---

# 6. Separate Files

Instead of writing everything inside one file,

Use

```text
ProductEndpoints.cs

CustomerEndpoints.cs

OrderEndpoints.cs

PaymentEndpoints.cs
```

Each file manages one feature.

---

# 7. Folder Structure

Example

```text
Project

│

├── Endpoints

│      ProductEndpoints.cs

│      CustomerEndpoints.cs

│      OrderEndpoints.cs

│      PaymentEndpoints.cs

│

├── Services

├── Models

├── Repositories

├── Program.cs
```

This structure is much easier to maintain.

---

# 8. Why Enterprise Teams Organize Endpoints

Suppose ten developers work on one API.

Without organization,

everyone edits

```text
Program.cs
```

This causes merge conflicts.

With organized endpoints,

Developer 1

```text
Products
```

Developer 2

```text
Orders
```

Developer 3

```text
Payments
```

Each developer works independently.

---

# 9. Dependency Injection Still Works

Even if endpoints are moved into separate files,

Dependency Injection works normally.

Example

```csharp
public static class ProductEndpoints
{
    public static void MapProductEndpoints(this WebApplication app)
    {
        app.MapGet("/products", (IProductService service) =>
        {
            return service.GetProducts();
        });
    }
}
```

ASP.NET Core automatically injects the service.

---

# 10. Best Organization Strategy

For enterprise applications:

* Keep `Program.cs` small.
* Move endpoints into separate files.
* Group endpoints by feature.
* Keep business logic in service classes.
* Keep database access in repository classes.
* Use Route Groups for related endpoints.

---

# 6. Complete Code Example

## ProductEndpoints.cs

```csharp
public static class ProductEndpoints
{
    public static void MapProductEndpoints(this WebApplication app)
    {
        var products = app.MapGroup("/products");

        products.MapGet("/", () =>
        {
            return Results.Ok("All Products");
        });

        products.MapPost("/", () =>
        {
            return Results.Ok("Product Created");
        });
    }
}
```

---

## Program.cs

```csharp
var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args);

var app = builder.Build();

app.MapProductEndpoints();

app.Run();
```

---

# 7. Code Explanation

```csharp
public static class ProductEndpoints
```

Creates a class to hold all product endpoints.

---

```csharp
MapProductEndpoints()
```

Registers all product-related endpoints.

---

```csharp
app.MapGroup("/products")
```

Creates a route group for all product endpoints.

---

```csharp
products.MapGet("/")
```

Handles GET requests for products.

---

```csharp
products.MapPost("/")
```

Handles POST requests for creating products.

---

```csharp
app.MapProductEndpoints();
```

Registers the endpoint group during application startup.

---

# 8. Real Project Usage ⭐

In enterprise ASP.NET Core applications, endpoints are rarely kept entirely in `Program.cs`. Instead, they are organized by feature into separate files such as **ProductEndpoints**, **OrderEndpoints**, and **CustomerEndpoints**. Related endpoints are grouped together, while business logic remains in service classes and database operations in repository classes. This structure makes the application easier to maintain, improves team collaboration, and reduces merge conflicts when multiple developers work on the same project.

---

# 9. Best Practices

* Keep `Program.cs` short and clean.
* Organize endpoints by feature.
* Use separate endpoint files.
* Use Route Groups for related endpoints.
* Keep business logic in service classes.
* Use meaningful route names.
* Follow a consistent folder structure.

---

# 10. Common Mistakes

* Placing hundreds of endpoints in `Program.cs`.
* Mixing business logic with endpoint definitions.
* Organizing endpoints randomly instead of by feature.
* Creating very large endpoint files.
* Duplicating routes across multiple files.

---

# 11. Key Points to Remember

* Organize endpoints as the application grows.
* Keep `Program.cs` minimal.
* Use feature-based organization.
* Use extension methods for endpoint registration.
* Use Route Groups to organize related routes.
* Keep business logic outside endpoints.
* Separate endpoints into different files.
* Better organization improves scalability and maintainability.

---

# 12. Summary

As an ASP.NET Core application grows, keeping all endpoints in `Program.cs` becomes difficult to manage. Organizing endpoints into separate files, grouping related routes, and following a feature-based structure makes the code cleaner, easier to maintain, and more scalable. A common enterprise approach is to register endpoints through extension methods, group related routes with `MapGroup()`, and keep business logic in service classes while using `Program.cs` only for application configuration and endpoint registration.

---
# Module 4 – Controllers vs Minimal APIs

# Topic 5 – Organizing Endpoints at Scale (Part 2 – Interview Preparation)

---

# ⭐ Possible Interview Questions

## Q1. What does "Organizing Endpoints at Scale" mean?

### Answer

Organizing endpoints at scale means structuring API endpoints into separate files or modules as the application grows. Instead of keeping all endpoints inside `Program.cs`, related endpoints are grouped together based on features like Products, Customers, or Orders. This makes the code easier to read, maintain, and debug. It also improves team collaboration because multiple developers can work on different modules without modifying the same file. Organizing endpoints is a common practice in enterprise ASP.NET Core applications. It helps the project remain clean and scalable.

---

## Q2. Why shouldn't all endpoints be placed in `Program.cs`?

### Answer

Keeping all endpoints in `Program.cs` is acceptable for very small applications, but it becomes difficult to manage as the project grows. A large application may contain hundreds of endpoints, making `Program.cs` very long and hard to navigate. It also increases the chances of merge conflicts when multiple developers work on the same file. Separating endpoints into feature-based files improves readability and maintenance. It also makes debugging easier because related endpoints are located together. Therefore, `Program.cs` should mainly contain application configuration and endpoint registration.

---

## Q3. How do you organize endpoints in a large ASP.NET Core application?

### Answer

In a large ASP.NET Core application, I organize endpoints by feature. For example, I create separate files such as `ProductEndpoints.cs`, `CustomerEndpoints.cs`, and `OrderEndpoints.cs`. Each file contains only the endpoints related to that feature. These endpoint files are registered from `Program.cs` using extension methods. Business logic is kept inside service classes, while repositories handle database operations. This approach keeps the application modular, maintainable, and easier to scale.

---

## Q4. What are Endpoint Extension Methods?

### Answer

Endpoint Extension Methods are custom extension methods used to register related endpoints from separate files. Instead of writing endpoint definitions directly in `Program.cs`, they are placed inside classes like `ProductEndpoints.cs`. The extension method, such as `MapProductEndpoints()`, is called from `Program.cs` during application startup. This keeps the startup file clean while allowing each feature to manage its own endpoints. Extension methods improve code organization and are commonly used in large Minimal API projects.

---

## Q5. What are Route Groups?

### Answer

Route Groups allow developers to group related endpoints under a common route prefix. For example, all product-related endpoints can be grouped under `/products` using `MapGroup()`. This reduces duplicate route definitions and keeps related endpoints together. Route Groups improve readability and make endpoint management easier. They are especially useful in Minimal APIs with multiple endpoints for the same feature. Using Route Groups also helps maintain a consistent API structure.

---

## Q6. Why is feature-based organization recommended?

### Answer

Feature-based organization groups all related code for a business feature together. For example, Product endpoints, services, models, and repositories belong to the Product feature. This makes the project easier to understand because developers can find everything related to a feature in one place. It also simplifies maintenance and reduces the impact of changes. Enterprise applications commonly follow this approach because it improves scalability and supports teamwork effectively.

---

## Q7. Does organizing endpoints affect Dependency Injection?

### Answer

No. Organizing endpoints into separate files does not affect Dependency Injection. Services are still registered in the dependency injection container and can be injected into endpoint methods normally. ASP.NET Core automatically resolves the required services regardless of where the endpoint is defined. This means developers can organize the project without changing how Dependency Injection works. It keeps the application clean while preserving all framework features.

---

## Q8. What are the benefits of organizing endpoints?

### Answer

Organizing endpoints improves code readability, maintainability, and scalability. It keeps `Program.cs` small and easy to understand. Developers can quickly locate feature-specific endpoints without searching through one large file. It also reduces merge conflicts when multiple developers work on different features. Organized endpoints make testing and debugging easier because related code is grouped together. This structure is considered a best practice for large ASP.NET Core applications.

---

## Q9. How would you organize endpoints in a real project?

### Answer

In a real project, I would organize endpoints by business features such as Products, Customers, Orders, Payments, and Authentication. Each feature would have its own endpoint file, service, and repository. I would use Route Groups to group related endpoints and extension methods to register them from `Program.cs`. Business logic would remain in service classes, and database operations would be handled by repositories. This approach keeps the project clean, scalable, and easy for multiple developers to maintain.

---

# ⭐ Project-Based Interview Questions

## Q1. Why is feature-based organization useful in a team environment?

### Answer

Feature-based organization allows each developer to work on a separate module such as Products or Orders. This reduces merge conflicts because developers are not editing the same file. It also makes the project easier to understand and maintain as the team grows.

---

## Q2. Where should business logic be placed when using organized endpoints?

### Answer

Business logic should be placed in service classes, not inside endpoint methods. Endpoints should only receive the request, call the appropriate service, and return the response. This keeps the application clean and follows the Single Responsibility Principle.

---

## Q3. What should remain inside `Program.cs`?

### Answer

`Program.cs` should mainly contain application configuration, service registration, middleware configuration, and calls to register endpoint groups. The actual endpoint definitions should be placed in separate files to keep the startup code clean and maintainable.

---

# ⭐ Rapid Fire Questions

1. Where should endpoint registration usually happen? → **Program.cs**
2. Where should endpoint definitions be placed? → **Separate endpoint files**
3. Which method groups related endpoints? → **`MapGroup()`**
4. Which approach is recommended for large projects? → **Feature-based organization**
5. Should business logic be inside endpoints? → **No**
6. Do organized endpoints support Dependency Injection? → **Yes**
7. Which file should remain clean and small? → **`Program.cs`**
8. What is a common endpoint file name? → **`ProductEndpoints.cs`**
9. What is the main goal of organizing endpoints? → **Maintainability**
10. Does organizing endpoints improve team collaboration? → **Yes**

---

# ⭐ Follow-up Questions

## Q1. Can Route Groups and Endpoint Extension Methods be used together?

### Answer

Yes. Route Groups can be created inside endpoint extension methods. This allows related endpoints to be grouped under a common route while keeping the registration code organized in separate files. This combination is commonly used in large Minimal API projects.

---

## Q2. Can Controllers and organized Minimal API endpoints exist in the same project?

### Answer

Yes. ASP.NET Core allows both Controllers and organized Minimal API endpoints to exist together. Developers often use Controllers for major business modules and Minimal APIs for utility endpoints such as health checks or internal services.

---

# ⭐ Interview Tips

* Explain that organizing endpoints becomes important as the application grows.
* Mention that **`Program.cs` should remain clean**.
* Say that **feature-based organization** is commonly used in enterprise projects.
* Remember that **`MapGroup()`** is used to group related endpoints.
* Mention that business logic belongs in **service classes**, not in endpoint methods.

---

# ⭐ Revision Notes

* Organize endpoints by feature.
* Keep `Program.cs` small.
* Use separate endpoint files.
* Register endpoints using extension methods.
* Use `MapGroup()` for related routes.
* Store business logic in services.
* Store database logic in repositories.
* Organizing endpoints improves readability.
* Organizing endpoints improves scalability.
* Common practice in enterprise ASP.NET Core applications.

---

# ⭐ Keywords

**Organizing Endpoints, Feature-Based Organization, Endpoint Extension Methods, `MapGroup()`, `Program.cs`, `ProductEndpoints.cs`, Minimal APIs, Dependency Injection, Maintainability, Scalability, Team Collaboration, ASP.NET Core**

---
# Module 5 – Handling Requests & Responses

# Topic 1 – Model Binding (Route, Query, Body, Header, Form) (Part 1 – Learning)

---

# 1. Introduction

Whenever a client sends a request to an ASP.NET Core Web API, it often includes data.

For example:

* Route parameter
* Query string
* Request body
* Request header
* Form data

The API needs a way to automatically read this data and assign it to C# variables or objects.

This process is called **Model Binding**.

Without Model Binding, developers would have to manually read every value from the HTTP request, which would make the code longer and more difficult to maintain.

> **Interview Tip:** Model Binding automatically converts HTTP request data into C# parameters or objects.

---

# 2. Definition

**Model Binding** is an ASP.NET Core feature that automatically reads data from an HTTP request and binds it to action method parameters or model objects.

### One-Line Interview Definition

> Model Binding automatically maps request data to C# parameters or model objects.

---

# 3. Why Do We Use Model Binding?

Suppose a client sends the following request:

```http
GET /api/products/10
```

Without Model Binding

```csharp
// Manually read request values (not recommended)
```

With Model Binding

```csharp
[HttpGet("{id}")]
public IActionResult GetProduct(int id)
{
    return Ok(id);
}
```

ASP.NET Core automatically assigns:

```text
id = 10
```

No manual code is required.

### Benefits

* Less code
* Automatic data conversion
* Cleaner controllers
* Easy to use
* Reduces errors
* Supports multiple data sources

---

# 4. How It Works

Client Request

```http
POST /api/products
```

↓

Request contains data

↓

ASP.NET Core receives the request

↓

Model Binder reads the request

↓

Maps values to C# parameters or objects

↓

Controller Action executes

↓

Returns Response

---

# 5. Key Concepts

---

# 1. Route Binding (`[FromRoute]`)

Route Binding reads values from the URL path.

Example

```http
GET /api/products/5
```

Controller

```csharp
[HttpGet("{id}")]
public IActionResult GetProduct([FromRoute] int id)
{
    return Ok(id);
}
```

Output

```text
5
```

Use Route Binding when the value is part of the URL.

---

# 2. Query String Binding (`[FromQuery]`)

Reads values from the query string.

Example

```http
GET /api/products?category=Laptop&page=2
```

Controller

```csharp
[HttpGet]
public IActionResult GetProducts(
    [FromQuery] string category,
    [FromQuery] int page)
{
    return Ok(new
    {
        category,
        page
    });
}
```

Output

```json
{
  "category": "Laptop",
  "page": 2
}
```

Use Query Binding for filtering, searching, sorting, and pagination.

---

# 3. Body Binding (`[FromBody]`)

Reads JSON data from the request body.

Request

```http
POST /api/products
```

Body

```json
{
  "name": "Laptop",
  "price": 50000
}
```

Model

```csharp
public class Product
{
    public string Name { get; set; }

    public decimal Price { get; set; }
}
```

Controller

```csharp
[HttpPost]
public IActionResult CreateProduct([FromBody] Product product)
{
    return Ok(product);
}
```

ASP.NET Core automatically converts the JSON into a `Product` object.

---

# 4. Header Binding (`[FromHeader]`)

Reads values from HTTP request headers.

Example Request

```http
GET /api/products

X-App-Version: 1.0
```

Controller

```csharp
[HttpGet]
public IActionResult GetVersion(
    [FromHeader(Name = "X-App-Version")] string version)
{
    return Ok(version);
}
```

Output

```text
1.0
```

Useful for API versioning, custom headers, and tracking information.

---

# 5. Form Binding (`[FromForm]`)

Reads data sent as form-data.

Example

```http
POST /api/upload
```

Form Data

```text
Name = Laptop

Price = 50000
```

Controller

```csharp
[HttpPost]
public IActionResult Upload(
    [FromForm] Product product)
{
    return Ok(product);
}
```

Commonly used when submitting HTML forms or uploading files.

---

# 6. Default Model Binding

In many cases, ASP.NET Core automatically determines where the data comes from.

Example

```csharp
[HttpGet("{id}")]
public IActionResult GetProduct(int id)
{
    return Ok(id);
}
```

`id` is automatically taken from the route.

Another example

```csharp
[HttpPost]
public IActionResult Create(Product product)
{
    return Ok(product);
}
```

With the `[ApiController]` attribute, ASP.NET Core automatically binds complex objects from the request body.

Explicit attributes like `[FromRoute]` and `[FromBody]` improve readability.

---

# 7. Simple Types vs Complex Types

### Simple Types

* int
* string
* bool
* decimal
* DateTime
* Guid

Usually bound from:

* Route
* Query
* Header

Example

```csharp
public IActionResult Get(int id)
```

---

### Complex Types

Examples

```csharp
Product

Customer

Employee
```

Usually bound from the request body in POST and PUT requests.

Example

```csharp
public IActionResult Create(Product product)
```

---

# 8. Can We Use Multiple Binding Sources?

Yes.

Example

```http
PUT /api/products/5?page=2
```

Request Header

```text
X-User = Admin
```

Request Body

```json
{
  "name": "Laptop",
  "price": 60000
}
```

Controller

```csharp
[HttpPut("{id}")]
public IActionResult UpdateProduct(
    [FromRoute] int id,
    [FromQuery] int page,
    [FromHeader(Name = "X-User")] string user,
    [FromBody] Product product)
{
    return Ok(new
    {
        id,
        page,
        user,
        product
    });
}
```

ASP.NET Core binds data from all specified sources automatically.

---

# 9. Common Binding Sources

| Attribute      | Reads From   | Example                 |
| -------------- | ------------ | ----------------------- |
| `[FromRoute]`  | URL Route    | `/products/5`           |
| `[FromQuery]`  | Query String | `?page=2`               |
| `[FromBody]`   | JSON Body    | POST Request            |
| `[FromHeader]` | HTTP Header  | `Authorization`         |
