# Module 6 – DTOs, Mapping & Validation

# Topic 5 – FluentValidation (Introduction) (Part 1 – Learning)

---

# 1. Introduction

Data Annotations are useful for simple validation such as:

* Required fields
* Email validation
* String length
* Numeric range

However, in real-world applications, validation rules can become more complex.

Examples:

* Age must be greater than 18 only if the user is an employee.
* Password must contain uppercase, lowercase, number, and special character.
* End Date must be after Start Date.
* Username must be unique in the database.

Writing these validations using Data Annotations becomes difficult.

For such scenarios, ASP.NET Core commonly uses **FluentValidation**.

> **Interview Tip:** FluentValidation is a popular validation library that allows developers to write clean, reusable, and complex validation rules outside the DTO.

---

# 2. Definition

**FluentValidation** is a third-party .NET library used to create validation rules using a fluent, readable syntax instead of Data Annotation attributes.

### One-Line Interview Definition

> FluentValidation is a library used to create clean and reusable validation rules for models and DTOs.

---

# 3. Why Do We Use FluentValidation?

Suppose we have a registration form.

Requirements:

* Name is required
* Email must be valid
* Password must contain:

  * Uppercase letter
  * Lowercase letter
  * Number
  * Special character
* Age must be between 18 and 60

These validations become difficult to manage with Data Annotations.

FluentValidation keeps validation logic separate from DTOs.

### Benefits

* Cleaner code
* Reusable validation rules
* Easier maintenance
* Better readability
* Supports complex business validation
* Easy to test

---

# 4. How It Works

Client Sends Request

↓

Model Binding

↓

FluentValidation Executes

↓

Validation Passes

↓

Controller Executes

↓

Validation Fails

↓

400 Bad Request Returned

---

# 5. Key Concepts

---

# 1. Install FluentValidation

Install the NuGet package.

```text
FluentValidation.AspNetCore
```

---

# 2. DTO Example

```csharp
public class EmployeeDto
{
    public string Name { get; set; }

    public string Email { get; set; }

    public int Age { get; set; }
}
```

Notice that there are **no validation attributes** on the DTO.

---

# 3. Create a Validator

```csharp
using FluentValidation;

public class EmployeeValidator
    : AbstractValidator<EmployeeDto>
{
    public EmployeeValidator()
    {
        RuleFor(x => x.Name)
            .NotEmpty()
            .MaximumLength(50);

        RuleFor(x => x.Email)
            .NotEmpty()
            .EmailAddress();

        RuleFor(x => x.Age)
            .InclusiveBetween(18, 60);
    }
}
```

Each validation rule is written using `RuleFor()`.

---

# 4. Register FluentValidation

Program.cs

```csharp
builder.Services.AddValidatorsFromAssemblyContaining<EmployeeValidator>();
```

This registers the validator with Dependency Injection.

---

# 5. Controller

```csharp
[ApiController]
[Route("api/[controller]")]
public class EmployeesController : ControllerBase
{
    [HttpPost]
    public IActionResult Create(EmployeeDto dto)
    {
        return Ok(dto);
    }
}
```

No manual validation code is required.

---

# 6. Validation Rules

### Required Field

```csharp
RuleFor(x => x.Name)
    .NotEmpty();
```

---

### Maximum Length

```csharp
RuleFor(x => x.Name)
    .MaximumLength(50);
```

---

### Minimum Length

```csharp
RuleFor(x => x.Password)
    .MinimumLength(8);
```

---

### Email Validation

```csharp
RuleFor(x => x.Email)
    .EmailAddress();
```

---

### Range Validation

```csharp
RuleFor(x => x.Age)
    .InclusiveBetween(18,60);
```

---

### Custom Message

```csharp
RuleFor(x => x.Name)
    .NotEmpty()
    .WithMessage("Name is required.");
```

---

### Regular Expression

```csharp
RuleFor(x => x.Password)
    .Matches(@"^(?=.*[A-Z])(?=.*\d).+$");
```

---

# 7. Complex Validation

Example

```csharp
RuleFor(x => x.EndDate)
    .GreaterThan(x => x.StartDate);
```

This is much easier than using Data Annotations.

---

# 8. Data Annotations vs FluentValidation

| Data Annotations           | FluentValidation             |
| -------------------------- | ---------------------------- |
| Built into .NET            | Third-party library          |
| Validation inside DTO      | Validation in separate class |
| Best for simple validation | Best for complex validation  |
| Limited flexibility        | Highly flexible              |
| Harder to reuse            | Easy to reuse                |

---

# 9. Complete Code Example

## DTO

```csharp
public class EmployeeDto
{
    public string Name { get; set; }

    public string Email { get; set; }

    public int Age { get; set; }
}
```

---

## Validator

```csharp
using FluentValidation;

public class EmployeeValidator
    : AbstractValidator<EmployeeDto>
{
    public EmployeeValidator()
    {
        RuleFor(x => x.Name)
            .NotEmpty()
            .MaximumLength(50);

        RuleFor(x => x.Email)
            .EmailAddress();

        RuleFor(x => x.Age)
            .InclusiveBetween(18,60);
    }
}
```

---

## Program.cs

```csharp
builder.Services.AddValidatorsFromAssemblyContaining<EmployeeValidator>();
```

---

## Controller

```csharp
[ApiController]
[Route("api/[controller]")]
public class EmployeesController : ControllerBase
{
    [HttpPost]
    public IActionResult Create(EmployeeDto dto)
    {
        return Ok(dto);
    }
}
```

---

# 10. Code Explanation

```csharp
AbstractValidator<EmployeeDto>
```

Creates a validator for the `EmployeeDto` class.

---

```csharp
RuleFor()
```

Specifies which property should be validated.

---

```csharp
NotEmpty()
```

Ensures the property is not empty.

---

```csharp
EmailAddress()
```

Validates the email format.

---

```csharp
InclusiveBetween()
```

Checks whether a numeric value falls within the specified range.

---

```csharp
WithMessage()
```

Provides a custom validation error message.

---

# 11. Real Project Usage ⭐

In enterprise ASP.NET Core Web API projects, FluentValidation is commonly used for validating Request DTOs that require more than basic validation. It keeps validation rules in separate validator classes, making the code easier to maintain and test. While Data Annotations are suitable for simple rules, FluentValidation is often preferred for complex business validations such as conditional rules, cross-property validation, and reusable validation logic.

---

# 12. Best Practices

* Use FluentValidation for complex validation rules.
* Keep validation logic in separate validator classes.
* Use meaningful custom error messages.
* Reuse validators where possible.
* Continue using DTOs for data transfer and validators for validation.

---

# 13. Common Mistakes

* Mixing complex validation rules with Data Annotations.
* Writing business logic inside validators.
* Forgetting to register validators in `Program.cs`.
* Duplicating validation rules across multiple validators.
* Using FluentValidation for very simple validation when Data Annotations are sufficient.

---

# 14. Key Points to Remember

* FluentValidation is a third-party library.
* Validation rules are written in separate validator classes.
* `AbstractValidator<T>` is the base class.
* `RuleFor()` defines validation rules.
* It is ideal for complex validation.
* Validation logic remains separate from DTOs.
* It improves readability and maintainability.

---

# 15. Summary

FluentValidation is a powerful validation library for ASP.NET Core that provides a clean and fluent way to define validation rules outside DTO classes. It is especially useful for complex business validation that is difficult to implement using Data Annotations. By separating validation logic into dedicated validator classes, applications become easier to maintain, test, and extend.

---
# Module 6 – DTOs, Mapping & Validation

# Topic 5 – FluentValidation (Introduction) (Part 2 – Interview Preparation)

---

# ⭐ Possible Interview Questions

## Q1. What is FluentValidation?

### Answer

FluentValidation is a third-party .NET library used to validate models and DTOs in ASP.NET Core applications. It allows developers to write validation rules using a fluent and readable syntax. Unlike Data Annotations, validation rules are written in separate validator classes. This keeps DTOs clean and makes validation easier to maintain. It is commonly used for complex validation scenarios. FluentValidation improves code readability and reusability.

---

## Q2. Why do we use FluentValidation?

### Answer

FluentValidation is used when validation rules become too complex for Data Annotations. It allows developers to write reusable and maintainable validation logic in separate classes. It supports conditional validation, cross-property validation, and custom validation rules. This keeps the DTO simple and separates validation from business logic. It also makes testing validation rules easier. It is widely used in enterprise ASP.NET Core applications.

---

## Q3. What is the difference between Data Annotations and FluentValidation?

### Answer

Data Annotations are built into .NET and are suitable for simple validation such as required fields, email validation, and string length. FluentValidation is a third-party library that provides a fluent syntax for creating complex validation rules. Data Annotations are written inside the DTO, while FluentValidation keeps validation in separate validator classes. FluentValidation is more flexible and reusable. It is preferred for advanced validation scenarios.

---

## Q4. What is `AbstractValidator<T>`?

### Answer

`AbstractValidator<T>` is the base class used to create a validator in FluentValidation. The generic type `T` represents the model or DTO being validated. All validation rules are written inside this class using the `RuleFor()` method. Each DTO usually has its own validator class. This keeps validation logic organized and easy to maintain. It is the starting point for creating FluentValidation rules.

Example:

```csharp id="3zgbtq"
public class EmployeeValidator
    : AbstractValidator<EmployeeDto>
{
}
```

---

## Q5. What is `RuleFor()`?

### Answer

`RuleFor()` is the main method used to define validation rules in FluentValidation. It specifies which property should be validated and what validation rules should be applied. Multiple validation methods can be chained together for the same property. This makes the validation code clean and readable. It is used in every FluentValidation validator class.

Example:

```csharp id="t8br6f"
RuleFor(x => x.Name)
    .NotEmpty()
    .MaximumLength(50);
```

---

## Q6. Where are FluentValidation rules written?

### Answer

FluentValidation rules are written in separate validator classes that inherit from `AbstractValidator<T>`. Unlike Data Annotations, validation attributes are not placed inside the DTO. This separates validation logic from the data model and makes the code easier to maintain. Each DTO usually has a corresponding validator class. This approach follows the Single Responsibility Principle.

---

## Q7. How do you register FluentValidation?

### Answer

After creating validator classes, they must be registered with the Dependency Injection container. This is usually done in `Program.cs` using `AddValidatorsFromAssemblyContaining<T>()`. Once registered, ASP.NET Core automatically uses the validator during request validation. This eliminates the need for manual validation code. Proper registration is required for FluentValidation to work.

Example:

```csharp id="rjjlwm"
builder.Services.AddValidatorsFromAssemblyContaining<EmployeeValidator>();
```

---

## Q8. When should you use FluentValidation instead of Data Annotations?

### Answer

FluentValidation should be used when validation rules are complex or need to be reused. Examples include conditional validation, comparing multiple properties, or creating custom validation logic. Data Annotations are sufficient for simple validations like required fields and email format. FluentValidation provides more flexibility and better code organization. Many enterprise applications use both approaches depending on the requirement.

---

## Q9. Where have you used FluentValidation in your project?

### Answer

In my ASP.NET Core Web API project, I mainly used Data Annotations for basic validation. I understand that FluentValidation is used when validation rules become complex or need to be reused. It keeps validation logic separate from DTOs and improves maintainability. Although I have learned FluentValidation, my project mostly required simple validation rules. I can implement FluentValidation when complex business validation is needed.

---

# ⭐ Project-Based Interview Questions

## Q1. Why would you choose FluentValidation for a large project?

### Answer

Because large projects often have complex validation rules. FluentValidation keeps validation logic separate from DTOs, makes the code easier to maintain, and allows validation rules to be reused across the application.

---

## Q2. Can Data Annotations and FluentValidation be used together?

### Answer

Yes. Many projects use Data Annotations for simple validation and FluentValidation for complex business rules. Both can work together depending on the application's requirements.

---

## Q3. Give one real-world example where FluentValidation is useful.

### Answer

A user registration system where the password must contain uppercase letters, lowercase letters, numbers, and special characters, and the confirm password must match the password. These rules are easier to implement with FluentValidation than with Data Annotations.

---

# ⭐ Rapid Fire Questions

1. Is FluentValidation built into .NET? → **No**
2. What type of library is FluentValidation? → **Third-party validation library**
3. Which base class is used to create validators? → **`AbstractValidator<T>`**
4. Which method defines validation rules? → **`RuleFor()`**
5. Which method checks for an empty value? → **`NotEmpty()`**
6. Which method validates email format? → **`EmailAddress()`**
7. Which method validates a numeric range? → **`InclusiveBetween()`**
8. Which method adds a custom error message? → **`WithMessage()`**
9. Where are validation rules written? → **In separate validator classes**
10. Is FluentValidation suitable for complex validation? → **Yes**

---

# ⭐ Follow-up Questions

## Q1. Does FluentValidation replace Data Annotations?

### Answer

No. FluentValidation does not replace Data Annotations. Data Annotations are still useful for simple validation. FluentValidation is mainly used for complex or reusable validation rules. Many projects use both together.

---

## Q2. Does FluentValidation improve code maintainability?

### Answer

Yes. Since validation logic is stored in separate validator classes, DTOs remain clean and the validation rules are easier to update, test, and reuse. This improves code maintainability, especially in large applications.

---

# ⭐ Interview Tips

* Remember that **FluentValidation is a third-party library**.
* Explain that it **keeps validation logic separate from DTOs**.
* Mention that **`AbstractValidator<T>`** is the base class.
* State that **`RuleFor()`** is used to define validation rules.
* Explain that **Data Annotations are suitable for simple validation**, while **FluentValidation is better for complex business rules**.
* If asked about your project, be honest about your experience and explain where FluentValidation would be appropriate.

---

# ⭐ Revision Notes

* FluentValidation is a third-party validation library.
* Validation rules are written in separate classes.
* `AbstractValidator<T>` is the base class.
* `RuleFor()` defines validation rules.
* Supports reusable and complex validation.
* DTOs remain clean because validation is separated.
* Register validators in `Program.cs`.
* Better suited for enterprise applications with complex business rules.
* Can be used together with Data Annotations.
* Improves readability and maintainability.

---

# ⭐ Keywords

**FluentValidation, `AbstractValidator<T>`, `RuleFor()`, `NotEmpty()`, `EmailAddress()`, `InclusiveBetween()`, `WithMessage()`, Validator Class, Request DTO, Data Annotations, ASP.NET Core Web API**

---
# Module 6 – DTOs, Mapping & Validation

# Topic 6 – ProblemDetails and ValidationProblemDetails (Part 1 – Learning)

---

# 1. Introduction

When an error occurs in an ASP.NET Core Web API, the API should return a **clear and consistent error response**.

Instead of returning random error messages, ASP.NET Core provides two standard error response formats:

* **ProblemDetails**
* **ValidationProblemDetails**

These classes follow the **RFC 7807 (Problem Details for HTTP APIs)** standard and help clients understand what went wrong.

> **Interview Tip:** `ProblemDetails` is used for general API errors, while `ValidationProblemDetails` is used for model validation errors.

---

# 2. Definition

### ProblemDetails

`ProblemDetails` is a standard class used to return information about general API errors.

### ValidationProblemDetails

`ValidationProblemDetails` is a specialized version of `ProblemDetails` that returns model validation errors.

### One-Line Interview Definition

> `ProblemDetails` provides standardized API error responses, while `ValidationProblemDetails` is specifically used for validation errors.

---

# 3. Why Do We Use ProblemDetails?

Without a standard format, different APIs may return different error structures.

Example

API 1

```json id="uw9ucv"
{
    "error":"Something went wrong"
}
```

API 2

```json id="qczjfx"
{
    "message":"Invalid request"
}
```

API 3

```json id="wc4nkw"
{
    "status":400
}
```

This inconsistency makes client applications difficult to build.

Using `ProblemDetails` provides one consistent error format.

### Benefits

* Standard error format
* Easy for clients to understand
* Follows RFC 7807
* Better debugging
* Consistent API responses

---

# 4. How It Works

Client Sends Request

↓

API Detects Error

↓

Creates ProblemDetails

↓

Returns Error Response

↓

Client Understands Error

---

# 5. Key Concepts

---

# 1. ProblemDetails

Example

```csharp id="8dpyq5"
return Problem(
    title: "Employee Not Found",
    detail: "Employee with ID 10 does not exist.",
    statusCode: 404);
```

Response

```json id="mkl77m"
{
  "type": "https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc9110#section-15.5.5",
  "title": "Employee Not Found",
  "status": 404,
  "detail": "Employee with ID 10 does not exist."
}
```

---

# 2. Properties of ProblemDetails

| Property | Description                                         |
| -------- | --------------------------------------------------- |
| type     | Link describing the error type                      |
| title    | Short summary of the error                          |
| status   | HTTP status code                                    |
| detail   | Detailed error description                          |
| instance | URI of the request that caused the error (optional) |

---

# 3. ValidationProblemDetails

When model validation fails, ASP.NET Core automatically returns a `ValidationProblemDetails` object.

Example DTO

```csharp id="y7kxv0"
public class EmployeeDto
{
    [Required]
    public string Name { get; set; }

    [EmailAddress]
    public string Email { get; set; }
}
```

---

Invalid Request

```json id="t08m0j"
{
    "name": "",
    "email": "abc"
}
```

Automatic Response

```json id="oovrzm"
{
  "type": "https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc9110#section-15.5.1",
  "title": "One or more validation errors occurred.",
  "status": 400,
  "errors": {
    "Name": [
      "The Name field is required."
    ],
    "Email": [
      "The Email field is not a valid e-mail address."
    ]
  }
}
```

This response is a `ValidationProblemDetails` object.

---

# 4. Returning ProblemDetails Manually

Controller

```csharp id="4k1c91"
[HttpGet("{id}")]
public IActionResult GetEmployee(int id)
{
    if (id != 1)
    {
        return Problem(
            title: "Employee Not Found",
            detail: $"Employee {id} does not exist.",
            statusCode: 404);
    }

    return Ok();
}
```

---

# 5. Returning ValidationProblem Manually

Example

```csharp id="2sgww8"
if (!ModelState.IsValid)
{
    return ValidationProblem(ModelState);
}
```

This creates a `ValidationProblemDetails` response.

---

# 6. Problem() Helper Method

ASP.NET Core provides the `Problem()` helper.

Example

```csharp id="4e1jy5"
return Problem(
    statusCode: 500,
    title: "Internal Server Error",
    detail: "Unexpected error occurred.");
```

---

# 7. ValidationProblem() Helper Method

ASP.NET Core also provides the `ValidationProblem()` helper.

Example

```csharp id="5ygzbl"
return ValidationProblem(ModelState);
```

This returns validation errors in a standard format.

---

# 8. ProblemDetails vs ValidationProblemDetails

| ProblemDetails                 | ValidationProblemDetails       |
| ------------------------------ | ------------------------------ |
| General API errors             | Validation errors              |
| Any HTTP error                 | Model validation only          |
| Uses `Problem()`               | Uses `ValidationProblem()`     |
| Contains title, detail, status | Contains title, status, errors |
| Example: 404, 500              | Example: 400 Bad Request       |

---

# 9. Complete Code Example

## DTO

```csharp id="e0ifvt"
public class EmployeeDto
{
    [Required]
    public string Name { get; set; }

    [EmailAddress]
    public string Email { get; set; }
}
```

---

## Controller

```csharp id="0a1wva"
[ApiController]
[Route("api/[controller]")]
public class EmployeesController : ControllerBase
{
    [HttpGet("{id}")]
    public IActionResult Get(int id)
    {
        if (id != 1)
        {
            return Problem(
                title: "Employee Not Found",
                detail: $"Employee {id} not found.",
                statusCode: 404);
        }

        return Ok();
    }

    [HttpPost]
    public IActionResult Create(EmployeeDto dto)
    {
        return Ok(dto);
    }
}
```

---

# 10. Code Explanation

```csharp id="qoqw5u"
Problem()
```

Returns a `ProblemDetails` response.

---

```csharp id="dgkocd"
ValidationProblem()
```

Returns a `ValidationProblemDetails` response.

---

```csharp id="mz6g5o"
title
```

Provides a short summary of the error.

---

```csharp id="5if44z"
detail
```

Provides detailed information about the error.

---

```csharp id="e5nv6k"
errors
```

Contains validation errors for invalid models.

---

# 11. Real Project Usage ⭐

In enterprise ASP.NET Core Web API projects, `ProblemDetails` is commonly used to return standardized error responses for situations such as **404 Not Found**, **500 Internal Server Error**, and **403 Forbidden**. `ValidationProblemDetails` is automatically returned when Request DTO validation fails using the `[ApiController]` attribute. Using these standard response formats makes it easier for frontend and mobile applications to display meaningful error messages consistently.

---

# 12. Best Practices

* Use `ProblemDetails` for general API errors.
* Use `ValidationProblemDetails` for validation errors.
* Return meaningful titles and details.
* Avoid exposing sensitive internal exception details.
* Follow the standard error response format across the application.

---

# 13. Common Mistakes

* Returning plain text error messages.
* Exposing stack traces or internal exception details.
* Using different error formats in different controllers.
* Returning validation errors as normal strings.
* Ignoring the built-in `Problem()` and `ValidationProblem()` helpers.

---

# 14. Key Points to Remember

* `ProblemDetails` is the standard API error format.
* `ValidationProblemDetails` is used for validation errors.
* `Problem()` returns a `ProblemDetails` response.
* `ValidationProblem()` returns a `ValidationProblemDetails` response.
* `[ApiController]` automatically returns `ValidationProblemDetails` for invalid models.
* Both provide consistent and client-friendly error responses.

---

# 15. Summary

`ProblemDetails` and `ValidationProblemDetails` provide standardized error responses in ASP.NET Core Web APIs. `ProblemDetails` is used for general HTTP errors such as **404** and **500**, while `ValidationProblemDetails` is used for model validation failures and is automatically returned by controllers using the `[ApiController]` attribute. These classes help create consistent, readable, and standards-based error responses that improve API usability and maintainability.

---
# Module 6 – DTOs, Mapping & Validation

# Topic 6 – ProblemDetails and ValidationProblemDetails (Part 2 – Interview Preparation)

---

# ⭐ Possible Interview Questions

## Q1. What is `ProblemDetails` in ASP.NET Core?

### Answer

`ProblemDetails` is a built-in class in ASP.NET Core used to return standardized error responses from a Web API. It follows the **RFC 7807** standard for HTTP API error responses. It provides information such as the error title, status code, and detailed message. This helps clients understand the error in a consistent format. It is commonly used for errors like **404 Not Found** and **500 Internal Server Error**. It improves API consistency and readability.

---

## Q2. Why do we use `ProblemDetails`?

### Answer

`ProblemDetails` is used to return a consistent and structured error response instead of plain text or custom error objects. It makes it easier for frontend and mobile applications to handle errors because every API follows the same response format. It also improves debugging and API documentation. Using a standard format keeps the API professional and easier to maintain. It is recommended for RESTful APIs.

---

## Q3. What is `ValidationProblemDetails`?

### Answer

`ValidationProblemDetails` is a specialized version of `ProblemDetails` used specifically for model validation errors. It is automatically returned when model validation fails in controllers that use the `[ApiController]` attribute. In addition to the standard error properties, it contains an **errors** collection with validation messages for each invalid property. This helps clients identify exactly which fields are invalid. It is commonly returned with a **400 Bad Request** status code.

---

## Q4. What is the difference between `ProblemDetails` and `ValidationProblemDetails`?

### Answer

`ProblemDetails` is used for general API errors such as **404 Not Found**, **403 Forbidden**, and **500 Internal Server Error**. `ValidationProblemDetails` is used only for model validation errors. `ProblemDetails` contains properties such as **title**, **detail**, and **status**, while `ValidationProblemDetails` also contains an **errors** collection with field-level validation messages. Both follow the same standard error format. The choice depends on the type of error.

---

## Q5. What is the `Problem()` method?

### Answer

`Problem()` is a helper method provided by `ControllerBase` that creates and returns a `ProblemDetails` response. Developers can specify the title, detail, and status code. It is commonly used when an API needs to return standardized error information. This method reduces the need to manually create error objects. It helps maintain consistent error responses across the application.

Example:

```csharp id="yfmkts"
return Problem(
    title: "Employee Not Found",
    detail: "Employee does not exist.",
    statusCode: 404);
```

---

## Q6. What is the `ValidationProblem()` method?

### Answer

`ValidationProblem()` is a helper method used to return a `ValidationProblemDetails` response. It is mainly used when developers manually validate `ModelState`. It returns validation errors in a standard format with a **400 Bad Request** status code. Although `[ApiController]` usually handles validation automatically, this method is useful for custom validation scenarios. It keeps validation responses consistent.

Example:

```csharp id="kqjlwm"
return ValidationProblem(ModelState);
```

---

## Q7. What information does `ProblemDetails` contain?

### Answer

`ProblemDetails` typically contains the **type**, **title**, **status**, **detail**, and optionally the **instance** property. The **type** provides a reference to the error type, **title** gives a short summary, **status** contains the HTTP status code, **detail** provides more information, and **instance** identifies the request that caused the error. These properties help clients understand the error clearly.

---

## Q8. What happens when model validation fails with `[ApiController]`?

### Answer

When a controller uses the `[ApiController]` attribute, ASP.NET Core automatically validates the incoming model. If validation fails, the controller action is not executed. Instead, ASP.NET Core returns a **400 Bad Request** response containing a `ValidationProblemDetails` object. This object includes validation error messages for each invalid property. This automatic behavior reduces manual validation code and keeps API responses consistent.

---

## Q9. Where have you used `ProblemDetails` in your project?

### Answer

In my ASP.NET Core Web API project, validation errors were automatically returned as `ValidationProblemDetails` because the controllers used the `[ApiController]` attribute. For general errors such as **404 Not Found**, we returned appropriate HTTP status codes. I understand that `ProblemDetails` is the recommended way to return standardized error responses and can use it when custom error responses are required.

---

# ⭐ Project-Based Interview Questions

## Q1. Did you manually create `ProblemDetails` objects in your project?

### Answer

Most validation errors were handled automatically by ASP.NET Core using `ValidationProblemDetails`. For general API errors, I used appropriate HTTP status codes. If custom standardized error responses are needed, `Problem()` can be used to create `ProblemDetails`.

---

## Q2. Why is `ValidationProblemDetails` useful?

### Answer

It provides detailed validation errors for each invalid field in a standard format. This makes it easy for frontend applications to display validation messages and helps clients quickly identify input mistakes.

---

## Q3. Why should APIs return standardized error responses?

### Answer

Standardized error responses make APIs easier to consume because every error follows the same format. This simplifies frontend development, improves debugging, and makes the API more maintainable.

---

# ⭐ Rapid Fire Questions

1. What is `ProblemDetails`? → **Standard API error response**
2. What is `ValidationProblemDetails`? → **Standard validation error response**
3. Which helper returns `ProblemDetails`? → **`Problem()`**
4. Which helper returns `ValidationProblemDetails`? → **`ValidationProblem()`**
5. Which HTTP status code is commonly used for validation errors? → **400 Bad Request**
6. Which attribute automatically returns `ValidationProblemDetails`? → **`[ApiController]`**
7. Does `ProblemDetails` follow a standard? → **Yes, RFC 7807**
8. Which property contains validation errors? → **`errors`**
9. Is `ValidationProblemDetails` derived from `ProblemDetails`? → **Yes**
10. Is `ProblemDetails` used only for validation errors? → **No**

---

# ⭐ Follow-up Questions

## Q1. Can we customize the contents of a `ProblemDetails` response?

### Answer

Yes. Developers can customize properties such as **title**, **detail**, **status**, and **type** when using the `Problem()` helper method. Applications can also implement custom error handling to return additional information when needed.

---

## Q2. Does `ValidationProblemDetails` replace `ModelState`?

### Answer

No. `ModelState` stores the validation results, while `ValidationProblemDetails` is the response object sent to the client. When validation fails, ASP.NET Core converts the `ModelState` errors into a `ValidationProblemDetails` response.

---

# ⭐ Interview Tips

* Remember that **`ProblemDetails` is used for general API errors**.
* Explain that **`ValidationProblemDetails` is specifically for model validation errors**.
* Mention that **`Problem()` returns `ProblemDetails`**.
* State that **`ValidationProblem()` returns `ValidationProblemDetails`**.
* Explain that **`[ApiController]` automatically returns `ValidationProblemDetails`** when model validation fails.
* Remember that both follow the **RFC 7807** standard.

---

# ⭐ Revision Notes

* `ProblemDetails` = Standard API error response.
* `ValidationProblemDetails` = Standard validation error response.
* `Problem()` returns `ProblemDetails`.
* `ValidationProblem()` returns `ValidationProblemDetails`.
* `[ApiController]` automatically returns validation errors.
* Validation errors return **400 Bad Request**.
* `ProblemDetails` contains `title`, `detail`, `status`, `type`, and optional `instance`.
* `ValidationProblemDetails` also contains an `errors` collection.
* Both improve API consistency.
* Both follow **RFC 7807**.

---

# ⭐ Keywords

**ProblemDetails, ValidationProblemDetails, `Problem()`, `ValidationProblem()`, RFC 7807, Error Response, Validation Errors, `[ApiController]`, ModelState, 400 Bad Request, ASP.NET Core Web API**

---
# Module 7 – Dependency Injection

# Topic 1 – What Dependency Injection (DI) is and Why ASP.NET Core is Built on It (Part 1 – Learning)

---

# 1. Introduction

As applications grow, classes need to work with other classes.

For example:

* `EmployeeController` needs `EmployeeService`
* `EmployeeService` needs `EmployeeRepository`
* `EmployeeRepository` needs `ApplicationDbContext`

If every class creates its own objects using the `new` keyword, the code becomes tightly coupled and difficult to maintain.

ASP.NET Core solves this problem using **Dependency Injection (DI)**.

Dependency Injection is one of the most important features of ASP.NET Core and is used throughout the framework.

> **Interview Tip:** Dependency Injection is a design pattern that provides required objects (dependencies) to a class instead of letting the class create them itself.

---

# 2. Definition

**Dependency Injection (DI)** is a design pattern in which the required objects (dependencies) are provided to a class from the outside instead of the class creating them itself.

ASP.NET Core has a built-in Dependency Injection container that automatically creates and manages objects.

### One-Line Interview Definition

> Dependency Injection is a design pattern that provides required objects to a class instead of allowing the class to create them using the `new` keyword.

---

# 3. Why Do We Use Dependency Injection?

Imagine an Employee Controller.

Without DI:

```csharp
public class EmployeeController : ControllerBase
{
    private EmployeeService _service =
        new EmployeeService();
}
```

Problems:

* Controller creates the object itself.
* Tight coupling.
* Difficult to replace implementations.
* Difficult to test.
* Hard to maintain.

With DI:

```csharp
public class EmployeeController : ControllerBase
{
    private readonly IEmployeeService _service;

    public EmployeeController(IEmployeeService service)
    {
        _service = service;
    }
}
```

Now ASP.NET Core provides the object automatically.

### Benefits

* Loose coupling
* Easy testing
* Better maintainability
* Better scalability
* Easier to replace implementations
* Cleaner code
* Follows SOLID principles

---

# 4. How It Works

Application Starts

↓

Services Registered in DI Container

↓

Client Sends Request

↓

ASP.NET Core Creates Controller

↓

DI Container Creates Required Objects

↓

Dependencies Injected into Constructor

↓

Controller Uses the Service

↓

Response Returned

---

# 5. Key Concepts

---

# 1. What is a Dependency?

A dependency is an object that another class needs to perform its work.

Example

```csharp
public class EmployeeController
{
    private readonly EmployeeService _service;
}
```

Here,

`EmployeeService` is the dependency.

---

# 2. What is Injection?

Injection means providing the dependency from outside the class.

Instead of

```csharp
private EmployeeService _service =
    new EmployeeService();
```

ASP.NET Core provides it automatically.

---

# 3. Constructor Injection

The most common type of Dependency Injection.

```csharp
public class EmployeeController : ControllerBase
{
    private readonly IEmployeeService _service;

    public EmployeeController(IEmployeeService service)
    {
        _service = service;
    }
}
```

The DI container automatically passes the object to the constructor.

---

# 4. Built-in DI Container

ASP.NET Core includes a built-in Dependency Injection container.

It is responsible for:

* Creating objects
* Managing object lifetime
* Injecting dependencies
* Disposing objects when required

No external library is required for most applications.

---

# 5. Registering a Service

Program.cs

```csharp
builder.Services.AddScoped<IEmployeeService, EmployeeService>();
```

Meaning:

* Interface → `IEmployeeService`
* Implementation → `EmployeeService`

The DI container now knows which class to create.

---

# 6. Resolving the Service

Controller

```csharp
public class EmployeeController : ControllerBase
{
    private readonly IEmployeeService _service;

    public EmployeeController(IEmployeeService service)
    {
        _service = service;
    }
}
```

ASP.NET Core automatically injects the registered implementation.

---

# 7. Without DI vs With DI

## Without DI

```csharp
public class EmployeeController : ControllerBase
{
    private EmployeeService _service =
        new EmployeeService();
}
```

Problems

* Tight coupling
* Hard to test
* Hard to replace service
* Difficult to maintain

---

## With DI

```csharp
public class EmployeeController : ControllerBase
{
    private readonly IEmployeeService _service;

    public EmployeeController(IEmployeeService service)
    {
        _service = service;
    }
}
```

Advantages

* Loose coupling
* Easy testing
* Better maintainability
* Easy to change implementations

---

# 8. Why ASP.NET Core is Built on Dependency Injection

Almost every component in ASP.NET Core uses DI.

Examples include:

* Controllers
* Services
* Repositories
* Entity Framework Core `DbContext`
* Logging (`ILogger<T>`)
* Configuration (`IConfiguration`)
* Options (`IOptions<T>`)
* HTTP Clients (`HttpClient`)
* Authentication and Authorization services

This allows the framework to create and manage objects consistently and efficiently.

---

# 9. Complete Code Example

## Interface

```csharp
public interface IEmployeeService
{
    string GetMessage();
}
```

---

## Service

```csharp
public class EmployeeService : IEmployeeService
{
    public string GetMessage()
    {
        return "Employee Service Called";
    }
}
```

---

## Program.cs

```csharp
builder.Services.AddScoped<IEmployeeService, EmployeeService>();
```

---

## Controller

```csharp
[ApiController]
[Route("api/[controller]")]
public class EmployeeController : ControllerBase
{
    private readonly IEmployeeService _service;

    public EmployeeController(IEmployeeService service)
    {
        _service = service;
    }

    [HttpGet]
    public IActionResult Get()
    {
        return Ok(_service.GetMessage());
    }
}
```

---

# 10. Code Explanation

```csharp
IEmployeeService
```

Defines the contract that the service must implement.

---

```csharp
EmployeeService
```

Implements the business logic defined in the interface.

---

```csharp
builder.Services.AddScoped<IEmployeeService, EmployeeService>();
```

Registers the service with the Dependency Injection container.

---

```csharp
EmployeeController(IEmployeeService service)
```

Uses constructor injection to receive the service.

---

```csharp
_service.GetMessage()
```

Calls the injected service instead of creating a new object.

---

# 11. Real Project Usage ⭐

In my ASP.NET Core Web API project, I used Dependency Injection to inject services into controllers instead of creating objects with the `new` keyword. Controllers received service interfaces through constructor injection, and the services contained the business logic. Repository classes and Entity Framework Core `DbContext` were also resolved through the DI container. This made the code modular, easier to maintain, and simpler to test by replacing implementations when needed.

---

# 12. Best Practices

* Prefer constructor injection over property or method injection.
* Register services using interfaces whenever possible.
* Choose the correct service lifetime (`Singleton`, `Scoped`, or `Transient`).
* Keep services focused on a single responsibility.
* Avoid creating dependencies with the `new` keyword inside controllers.

---

# 13. Common Mistakes

* Creating services manually using `new`.
* Registering services with the wrong lifetime.
* Injecting too many dependencies into one class.
* Forgetting to register a service in `Program.cs`.
* Depending directly on concrete classes instead of interfaces.

---

# 14. Key Points to Remember

* Dependency Injection is a design pattern.
* It provides dependencies from outside the class.
* ASP.NET Core has a built-in DI container.
* Constructor Injection is the most common approach.
* Services must be registered before they can be injected.
* DI reduces coupling and improves maintainability.
* Most ASP.NET Core features rely on Dependency Injection.

---

# 15. Summary

Dependency Injection (DI) is one of the core features of ASP.NET Core. It allows required objects to be provided automatically through the built-in DI container instead of creating them manually with the `new` keyword. This results in loosely coupled, maintainable, testable, and scalable applications. Because of these benefits, ASP.NET Core uses Dependency Injection throughout the framework for controllers, services, repositories, logging, configuration, and many other components.

---

# Module 7 – Dependency Injection

# Topic 1 – What Dependency Injection (DI) is and Why ASP.NET Core is Built on It (Part 2 – Interview Preparation)

---

# ⭐ Possible Interview Questions

## Q1. What is Dependency Injection (DI)?

### Answer

Dependency Injection (DI) is a design pattern used to provide required objects to a class instead of allowing the class to create them itself. In ASP.NET Core, the built-in DI container manages object creation and injects dependencies automatically. This reduces tight coupling between classes. It also makes the application easier to maintain and test. DI is one of the core features of ASP.NET Core. Almost every ASP.NET Core application uses Dependency Injection.

---

## Q2. Why do we use Dependency Injection?

### Answer

Dependency Injection is used to create loosely coupled applications. Instead of creating objects with the `new` keyword, required services are provided automatically by the DI container. This makes it easier to replace implementations, write unit tests, and maintain the code. It also follows SOLID principles, especially the Dependency Inversion Principle. Overall, DI improves code quality and scalability.

---

## Q3. What is a dependency?

### Answer

A dependency is an object that another class needs to perform its work. For example, an `EmployeeController` may need an `IEmployeeService` to retrieve employee data. In this case, `IEmployeeService` is the dependency. Instead of creating it manually, ASP.NET Core injects it into the controller. This keeps the code clean and loosely coupled.

---

## Q4. What is Constructor Injection?

### Answer

Constructor Injection is the most common way to implement Dependency Injection in ASP.NET Core. The required dependency is passed through the constructor of a class. The DI container automatically creates the object and passes it to the constructor. This ensures that all required dependencies are available when the object is created. Constructor Injection is recommended because it makes dependencies explicit and supports unit testing.

Example:

```csharp
public EmployeeController(IEmployeeService service)
{
    _service = service;
}
```

---

## Q5. What is the built-in DI Container in ASP.NET Core?

### Answer

ASP.NET Core includes a built-in Dependency Injection container. It is responsible for creating service objects, injecting them into classes, managing their lifetime, and disposing of them when necessary. Developers register services in `Program.cs`, and the container automatically resolves them whenever they are required. For most applications, no external DI library is needed.

---

## Q6. Why is ASP.NET Core built on Dependency Injection?

### Answer

ASP.NET Core is built on Dependency Injection because it promotes loose coupling, modular design, and better maintainability. Many framework components such as controllers, logging, configuration, Entity Framework Core, authentication, and HTTP clients rely on DI. By using a single DI container, the framework manages object creation consistently. This simplifies development and improves scalability.

---

## Q7. What happens if a service is not registered in the DI container?

### Answer

If a required service is not registered in the DI container, ASP.NET Core cannot create the object. When the application tries to resolve the dependency, it throws an exception indicating that no service has been registered for that type. Therefore, every service that needs to be injected must first be registered in `Program.cs`.

---

## Q8. What are the advantages of Dependency Injection?

### Answer

Dependency Injection provides many benefits. It reduces coupling between classes, improves code readability, and makes applications easier to test. It also allows implementations to be changed without modifying dependent classes. DI supports maintainability, scalability, and follows SOLID principles. These advantages make it an essential design pattern in ASP.NET Core development.

---

## Q9. How have you used Dependency Injection in your project?

### Answer

In my ASP.NET Core Web API project, I used Dependency Injection to inject service interfaces into controllers through constructor injection. Business logic was placed in service classes, and controllers only handled HTTP requests and responses. Repository classes and Entity Framework Core `DbContext` were also resolved using the DI container. This made the application modular, easier to maintain, and simpler to test.

---

# ⭐ Project-Based Interview Questions

## Q1. Where did you use Dependency Injection in your project?

### Answer

I used Dependency Injection in controllers to inject service interfaces. The services contained business logic, and repositories handled database operations. Entity Framework Core `DbContext` was also managed by the DI container.

---

## Q2. Why didn't you create service objects using the `new` keyword?

### Answer

Creating objects with the `new` keyword creates tight coupling between classes. Using Dependency Injection allows ASP.NET Core to manage object creation, improves testability, and makes it easy to replace implementations without changing controller code.

---

## Q3. Which ASP.NET Core components commonly use Dependency Injection?

### Answer

Common components include Controllers, Services, Repositories, Entity Framework Core `DbContext`, `ILogger<T>`, `IConfiguration`, `IOptions<T>`, `HttpClient`, and Authentication services.

---

# ⭐ Rapid Fire Questions

1. What is DI? → **A design pattern for providing dependencies**
2. Does ASP.NET Core have a built-in DI container? → **Yes**
3. Which type of injection is most common? → **Constructor Injection**
4. Where are services registered? → **`Program.cs`**
5. Which keyword should be avoided for creating services? → **`new`**
6. What does DI reduce? → **Tight coupling**
7. Does DI improve testability? → **Yes**
8. Can interfaces be injected? → **Yes**
9. Which method is commonly used to register services? → **`AddScoped()`, `AddSingleton()`, `AddTransient()`**
10. Is Dependency Injection a core feature of ASP.NET Core? → **Yes**

---

# ⭐ Follow-up Questions

## Q1. Is Dependency Injection a design pattern or a framework feature?

### Answer

Dependency Injection is a **design pattern**. ASP.NET Core provides a **built-in DI container** that implements this pattern and makes it easy to use throughout the application.

---

## Q2. Can a class have multiple dependencies?

### Answer

Yes. A class can receive multiple dependencies through its constructor. However, if a class has too many dependencies, it may indicate that it has too many responsibilities and should be refactored.

---

# ⭐ Interview Tips

* Clearly explain that **DI provides objects instead of creating them with `new`**.
* Mention that **ASP.NET Core has a built-in DI container**.
* State that **Constructor Injection is the recommended approach**.
* Explain that **services must be registered before they can be injected**.
* Mention real framework examples like **`ILogger<T>`**, **`DbContext`**, and **`IConfiguration`**.
* Connect DI with **loose coupling**, **testability**, and **maintainability**.

---

# ⭐ Revision Notes

* Dependency Injection is a design pattern.
* ASP.NET Core has a built-in DI container.
* Dependencies are provided from outside the class.
* Constructor Injection is the preferred method.
* Services must be registered in `Program.cs`.
* DI reduces tight coupling.
* DI improves maintainability and testability.
* Most ASP.NET Core components use DI.
* Avoid creating service objects with the `new` keyword.
* DI supports SOLID principles.

---

# ⭐ Keywords

**Dependency Injection (DI), Dependency, DI Container, Constructor Injection, Service Registration, `Program.cs`, `IEmployeeService`, Loose Coupling, SOLID Principles, Built-in DI Container, ASP.NET Core**

---
# Module 7 – Dependency Injection

# Topic 2 – The Built-in DI Container (Part 1 – Learning)

---

# 1. Introduction

Dependency Injection (DI) is one of the core features of ASP.NET Core.

To make DI easy to use, ASP.NET Core provides a **built-in Dependency Injection (DI) Container**.

The DI Container automatically:

* Creates objects
* Provides objects where needed
* Manages object lifetime
* Disposes objects when they are no longer needed

As developers, we only need to register our services. The DI Container handles the rest.

> **Interview Tip:** The built-in DI Container is responsible for creating, managing, and injecting service objects throughout an ASP.NET Core application.

---

# 2. Definition

The **Built-in DI Container** is the default Dependency Injection container provided by ASP.NET Core. It stores registered services and automatically creates and injects them wherever they are required.

### One-Line Interview Definition

> The built-in DI Container is a framework component that creates, manages, and injects registered services automatically.

---

# 3. Why Do We Use the Built-in DI Container?

Imagine an application with:

* EmployeeService
* ProductService
* OrderService
* PaymentService
* EmailService
* Logging Service

If every controller creates these services using the `new` keyword, the application becomes difficult to maintain.

Example

```csharp
var service = new EmployeeService();
```

Now imagine hundreds of controllers doing the same thing.

Problems:

* Repeated object creation
* Tight coupling
* Difficult to replace implementations
* Difficult testing
* Difficult lifetime management

The DI Container solves all these problems.

### Benefits

* Automatic object creation
* Automatic dependency injection
* Centralized service registration
* Lifetime management
* Cleaner code
* Better scalability
* Easier testing

---

# 4. How It Works

Application Starts

↓

Program.cs Registers Services

↓

DI Container Stores Registrations

↓

Client Sends Request

↓

ASP.NET Core Creates Controller

↓

DI Container Creates Required Services

↓

Dependencies Injected

↓

Controller Executes

↓

Response Returned

---

# 5. Key Concepts

---

# 1. Service Collection

All services are registered inside `builder.Services`.

Example

```csharp
builder.Services.AddScoped<IEmployeeService, EmployeeService>();
```

`builder.Services` is an `IServiceCollection`.

It acts like a collection that stores all service registrations.

---

# 2. Registering Services

Example

```csharp
builder.Services.AddScoped<IEmployeeService, EmployeeService>();
```

Meaning

* Interface → `IEmployeeService`
* Implementation → `EmployeeService`

The DI Container remembers this mapping.

---

# 3. Resolving Services

Controller

```csharp
public class EmployeeController : ControllerBase
{
    private readonly IEmployeeService _service;

    public EmployeeController(IEmployeeService service)
    {
        _service = service;
    }
}
```

The DI Container automatically creates `EmployeeService` and injects it into the controller.

---

# 4. Object Creation

Without DI

```csharp
var service = new EmployeeService();
```

With DI

```csharp
public EmployeeController(IEmployeeService service)
{
    _service = service;
}
```

The container creates the object automatically.

---

# 5. Dependency Graph

Sometimes one service depends on another.

Example

```
EmployeeController
        ↓
IEmployeeService
        ↓
EmployeeRepository
        ↓
ApplicationDbContext
```

The DI Container automatically creates every required object in the correct order.

---

# 6. Built-in Services

ASP.NET Core automatically registers many framework services.

Examples

* `ILogger<T>`
* `IConfiguration`
* `IHostEnvironment`
* `IWebHostEnvironment`
* `DbContext` (after registration)
* `IOptions<T>`
* `HttpClient` (after registration)

These services can be injected directly into controllers or services.

Example

```csharp
public class EmployeeController : ControllerBase
{
    private readonly ILogger<EmployeeController> _logger;

    public EmployeeController(
        ILogger<EmployeeController> logger)
    {
        _logger = logger;
    }
}
```

---

# 7. What Happens Internally?

When the application starts:

1. `Program.cs` registers services.
2. ASP.NET Core builds the DI Container.
3. The container stores all service registrations.
4. When a controller is requested, the container checks its constructor.
5. It creates all required dependencies.
6. It injects them into the controller.
7. The controller starts executing.

Everything happens automatically.

---

# 8. Complete Code Example

## Interface

```csharp
public interface IEmployeeService
{
    string GetMessage();
}
```

---

## Service

```csharp
public class EmployeeService : IEmployeeService
{
    public string GetMessage()
    {
        return "Hello from Employee Service";
    }
}
```

---

## Program.cs

```csharp
builder.Services.AddScoped<IEmployeeService, EmployeeService>();
```

---

## Controller

```csharp
[ApiController]
[Route("api/[controller]")]
public class EmployeeController : ControllerBase
{
    private readonly IEmployeeService _service;

    public EmployeeController(IEmployeeService service)
    {
        _service = service;
    }

    [HttpGet]
    public IActionResult Get()
    {
        return Ok(_service.GetMessage());
    }
}
```

---

# 9. Code Explanation

```csharp
builder.Services
```

The service collection where all services are registered.

---

```csharp
AddScoped()
```

Registers a service with the DI Container using the Scoped lifetime.

---

```csharp
IEmployeeService
```

The service contract (interface).

---

```csharp
EmployeeService
```

The implementation that the DI Container creates.

---

```csharp
EmployeeController(IEmployeeService service)
```

The constructor receives the dependency from the DI Container.

---

```csharp
_service.GetMessage()
```

Uses the injected service instead of creating a new object.

---

# 10. Real Project Usage ⭐

In my ASP.NET Core Web API project, I used the built-in DI Container to register service interfaces and their implementations in `Program.cs`. Controllers received services through constructor injection, while services accessed repositories and Entity Framework Core `DbContext` through the same DI Container. This centralized object creation, reduced tight coupling, and made the application easier to maintain and test.

---

# 11. Best Practices

* Register services through interfaces whenever possible.
* Use constructor injection instead of creating objects manually.
* Keep registrations in `Program.cs`.
* Choose the correct service lifetime.
* Let the DI Container manage object creation.

---

# 12. Common Mistakes

* Creating services with the `new` keyword.
* Forgetting to register services.
* Registering services with the wrong lifetime.
* Injecting too many dependencies into one class.
* Mixing manual object creation with Dependency Injection.

---

# 13. Key Points to Remember

* ASP.NET Core has a built-in DI Container.
* It automatically creates and injects objects.
* Services are registered in `builder.Services`.
* `IServiceCollection` stores service registrations.
* Constructor Injection is the most common way to receive services.
* The container also manages object lifetimes.
* Most ASP.NET Core framework services use the built-in DI Container.

---

# 14. Summary

The built-in DI Container is the default Dependency Injection system in ASP.NET Core. It stores service registrations, creates objects, injects dependencies, and manages object lifetimes automatically. Developers only need to register services in `Program.cs`, and the container handles the rest. This simplifies application development, reduces coupling, and makes the code more maintainable and testable.

---
# Module 7 – Dependency Injection

# Topic 2 – The Built-in DI Container (Part 2 – Interview Preparation)

---

# ⭐ Possible Interview Questions

## Q1. What is the built-in DI Container in ASP.NET Core?

### Answer

The built-in DI (Dependency Injection) Container is the default service container provided by ASP.NET Core. It is responsible for creating, managing, and injecting objects into classes that require them. Developers register services in `Program.cs`, and the container automatically resolves them when needed. It also manages the lifetime of service objects. This reduces manual object creation and keeps the application loosely coupled. It is one of the core features of ASP.NET Core.

---

## Q2. Why do we use the built-in DI Container?

### Answer

The built-in DI Container is used to manage object creation automatically. Instead of creating objects using the `new` keyword, developers register services once, and the container provides them wherever required. This reduces code duplication and tight coupling. It also improves maintainability, testability, and scalability. The container manages object lifetimes and dependencies efficiently. This makes application development simpler and cleaner.

---

## Q3. What is `builder.Services`?

### Answer

`builder.Services` is an `IServiceCollection` used to register application services with the built-in DI Container. Developers use methods such as `AddScoped()`, `AddSingleton()`, and `AddTransient()` to register services. After registration, the DI Container knows how to create and inject those services. It acts as the central place for configuring Dependency Injection. Every ASP.NET Core application uses `builder.Services` during startup.

Example:

```csharp
builder.Services.AddScoped<IEmployeeService, EmployeeService>();
```

---

## Q4. What is `IServiceCollection`?

### Answer

`IServiceCollection` is a collection that stores all service registrations in ASP.NET Core. It contains information about the service type, implementation type, and service lifetime. When the application starts, ASP.NET Core builds the DI Container using these registrations. The container then uses this information to create and inject services whenever required. Developers usually access it through `builder.Services`.

---

## Q5. How does the built-in DI Container work?

### Answer

When the application starts, services are registered in `Program.cs`. ASP.NET Core builds the DI Container using these registrations. When a controller or service is requested, the container checks its constructor for dependencies. It creates the required objects and injects them automatically. If those objects have their own dependencies, the container resolves them as well. This entire process happens automatically without manual object creation.

---

## Q6. What happens if a dependency is not registered?

### Answer

If a dependency is not registered in the DI Container, ASP.NET Core cannot create the required object. When the application tries to resolve the dependency, it throws an exception stating that no service has been registered for that type. To avoid this, every injectable service must be registered before it is used. Proper service registration is essential for Dependency Injection to work correctly.

---

## Q7. Can the built-in DI Container resolve nested dependencies?

### Answer

Yes. The built-in DI Container can resolve nested dependencies automatically. For example, if a controller depends on a service, and that service depends on a repository, and the repository depends on `DbContext`, the container creates all required objects in the correct order. This process is known as resolving the dependency graph. Developers only need to register the services properly.

---

## Q8. Which ASP.NET Core services are registered with the DI Container?

### Answer

Many framework services use the built-in DI Container. Examples include `ILogger<T>`, `IConfiguration`, `IOptions<T>`, `IHostEnvironment`, `IWebHostEnvironment`, Entity Framework Core `DbContext`, and `HttpClient` (after registration). These services can be injected directly into controllers, services, or repositories using constructor injection. This makes them easy to access throughout the application.

---

## Q9. How have you used the built-in DI Container in your project?

### Answer

In my ASP.NET Core Web API project, I registered service interfaces and implementations in `Program.cs` using the built-in DI Container. Controllers received services through constructor injection, and services accessed repositories and Entity Framework Core `DbContext` through the same container. This centralized object creation and reduced tight coupling. It also made the application easier to maintain and test.

---

# ⭐ Project-Based Interview Questions

## Q1. Where did you register your services?

### Answer

I registered my services in `Program.cs` using `builder.Services`. Service interfaces were mapped to their implementations using methods such as `AddScoped()`. This allowed the DI Container to create and inject the required services automatically.

---

## Q2. Did you manually create service objects using `new`?

### Answer

No. I relied on the built-in DI Container. Controllers received services through constructor injection, and the DI Container automatically created and injected the required objects.

---

## Q3. How does the DI Container help in large projects?

### Answer

The DI Container centralizes object creation, manages service lifetimes, resolves dependencies automatically, and reduces tight coupling. This makes large applications easier to maintain, extend, and test.

---

# ⭐ Rapid Fire Questions

1. What is the built-in DI Container? → **ASP.NET Core's default Dependency Injection container**
2. Where are services registered? → **`builder.Services`**
3. What is the type of `builder.Services`? → **`IServiceCollection`**
4. Which file is commonly used for service registration? → **`Program.cs`**
5. Does the DI Container create objects automatically? → **Yes**
6. Does it manage object lifetime? → **Yes**
7. Which injection method is most common? → **Constructor Injection**
8. Can it resolve nested dependencies? → **Yes**
9. What happens if a service is not registered? → **An exception is thrown**
10. Is the built-in DI Container part of ASP.NET Core? → **Yes**

---

# ⭐ Follow-up Questions

## Q1. Can we use another DI Container instead of the built-in one?

### Answer

Yes. Although the built-in DI Container is sufficient for most applications, ASP.NET Core also supports third-party containers such as **Autofac**, **Ninject**, and **StructureMap** if additional features are required.

---

## Q2. Does the DI Container only work with controllers?

### Answer

No. The DI Container can inject dependencies into controllers, services, repositories, middleware, background services, filters, and many other ASP.NET Core components.

---

# ⭐ Interview Tips

* Explain that the **built-in DI Container creates, manages, and injects services automatically**.
* Mention that **services are registered in `Program.cs` using `builder.Services`**.
* Remember that **`builder.Services` is an `IServiceCollection`**.
* Explain that the container **resolves nested dependencies automatically**.
* Mention that it **manages service lifetimes** and **reduces manual object creation**.
* Give examples like **`ILogger<T>`**, **`IConfiguration`**, and **`DbContext`** to show practical understanding.

---

# ⭐ Revision Notes

* ASP.NET Core provides a built-in DI Container.
* Services are registered in `Program.cs`.
* `builder.Services` is an `IServiceCollection`.
* The DI Container creates and injects services automatically.
* It resolves nested dependencies.
* It manages object lifetimes.
* Constructor Injection is the recommended approach.
* Missing service registrations cause runtime exceptions.
* Many ASP.NET Core framework services use the built-in DI Container.
* The built-in DI Container simplifies development and improves maintainability.

---

# ⭐ Keywords

**Built-in DI Container, Dependency Injection, `builder.Services`, `IServiceCollection`, Service Registration, Constructor Injection, Service Resolution, Dependency Graph, `Program.cs`, ASP.NET Core, `ILogger<T>`, `IConfiguration`, `DbContext`**

---
# Module 7 – Dependency Injection

# Topic 3 – Service Lifetimes (Singleton, Scoped, Transient) (Part 1 – Learning)

---

# 1. Introduction

When we register a service in ASP.NET Core, we must decide **how long the service object should live**.

For example:

* Should the same object be used throughout the application?
* Should a new object be created for every HTTP request?
* Should a new object be created every time it is requested?

This is called the **Service Lifetime**.

ASP.NET Core provides **three built-in service lifetimes**:

* Singleton
* Scoped
* Transient

Choosing the correct lifetime is important for application performance, memory usage, and correctness.

> **Interview Tip:** Service lifetime defines how long an object created by the DI Container will exist.

---

# 2. Definition

A **Service Lifetime** determines how long an instance of a registered service is created and reused by the Dependency Injection container.

### One-Line Interview Definition

> Service Lifetime defines how long a service instance lives and when a new instance is created by the DI Container.

---

# 3. Why Do We Use Service Lifetimes?

Different services have different requirements.

Examples:

* A logger can use one shared instance.
* A database context should be unique for each HTTP request.
* A helper service may need a new object every time.

Using the correct lifetime helps:

* Improve performance
* Reduce memory usage
* Avoid unexpected bugs
* Manage resources correctly

---

# 4. How It Works

Application Starts

↓

Services Registered with Lifetime

↓

DI Container Stores Registration

↓

HTTP Request Arrives

↓

DI Container Creates Service Based on Lifetime

↓

Service Injected

↓

Request Completed

↓

Object Reused or Destroyed Depending on Lifetime

---

# 5. Key Concepts

---

# 1. Singleton Lifetime

A **Singleton** service is created **only once** during the application's lifetime.

The same instance is reused everywhere.

Registration

```csharp
builder.Services.AddSingleton<IEmailService, EmailService>();
```

Example

```text
Application Starts
        ↓
EmailService Created
        ↓
Controller A → Same Object
Controller B → Same Object
Controller C → Same Object
```

### Characteristics

* Only one instance
* Shared across the application
* Created once
* Fast because it is reused

### Common Uses

* Logging
* Configuration
* Caching
* Application settings

---

# 2. Scoped Lifetime

A **Scoped** service is created **once per HTTP request**.

Within the same request, every class gets the same instance.

A new request gets a new instance.

Registration

```csharp
builder.Services.AddScoped<IEmployeeService, EmployeeService>();
```

Example

```text
Request 1
Controller
    ↓
EmployeeService (Object A)

Repository
    ↓
EmployeeService (Object A)

----------------------------

Request 2

Controller
    ↓
EmployeeService (Object B)
```

### Characteristics

* One instance per request
* Shared within the same request
* New object for every request

### Common Uses

* Business services
* Repository classes
* Entity Framework Core DbContext

---

# 3. Transient Lifetime

A **Transient** service creates a **new object every time it is requested**.

Registration

```csharp
builder.Services.AddTransient<IReportService, ReportService>();
```

Example

```text
Controller
     ↓
ReportService (Object A)

Repository
     ↓
ReportService (Object B)

Another Service
      ↓
ReportService (Object C)
```

Every injection creates a new object.

### Characteristics

* New object every time
* No object sharing
* Shortest lifetime

### Common Uses

* Lightweight helper classes
* Utility services
* Stateless services

---

# 4. Comparison Table

| Feature     | Singleton          | Scoped              | Transient            |
| ----------- | ------------------ | ------------------- | -------------------- |
| Instances   | One                | One per request     | New every time       |
| Shared      | Entire application | Current request     | Never                |
| Lifetime    | Application        | HTTP Request        | Until injected       |
| Performance | Fastest            | Good                | More object creation |
| Common Use  | Logger, Cache      | Services, DbContext | Helper classes       |

---

# 5. Registration Methods

Singleton

```csharp
builder.Services.AddSingleton<IEmailService, EmailService>();
```

Scoped

```csharp
builder.Services.AddScoped<IEmployeeService, EmployeeService>();
```

Transient

```csharp
builder.Services.AddTransient<IReportService, ReportService>();
```

---

# 6. Which Lifetime Should You Choose?

### Singleton

Choose when:

* Same object can be reused
* Data is shared safely
* No request-specific data

Examples:

* Logger
* Configuration
* Cache

---

### Scoped

Choose when:

* One object is needed per HTTP request
* Working with database operations
* Business logic depends on the current request

Examples:

* Services
* Repositories
* DbContext

---

### Transient

Choose when:

* Fresh object is required every time
* Object has no shared state
* Lightweight helper classes

Examples:

* Utility classes
* Report generators
* Data formatters

---

# 7. Complete Code Example

## Interface

```csharp
public interface IMessageService
{
    Guid GetId();
}
```

---

## Service

```csharp
public class MessageService : IMessageService
{
    private readonly Guid _id = Guid.NewGuid();

    public Guid GetId()
    {
        return _id;
    }
}
```

---

## Program.cs

```csharp
builder.Services.AddSingleton<IMessageService, MessageService>();

// OR

builder.Services.AddScoped<IMessageService, MessageService>();

// OR

builder.Services.AddTransient<IMessageService, MessageService>();
```

---

## Controller

```csharp
[ApiController]
[Route("api/[controller]")]
public class DemoController : ControllerBase
{
    private readonly IMessageService _service;

    public DemoController(IMessageService service)
    {
        _service = service;
    }

    [HttpGet]
    public IActionResult Get()
    {
        return Ok(_service.GetId());
    }
}
```

If you call the API multiple times:

* **Singleton:** Same GUID every time until the application restarts.
* **Scoped:** Same GUID within one request, different GUID for each new request.
* **Transient:** New GUID every time the service is injected.

---

# 8. Code Explanation

```csharp
AddSingleton()
```

Creates one instance for the entire application.

---

```csharp
AddScoped()
```

Creates one instance per HTTP request.

---

```csharp
AddTransient()
```

Creates a new instance every time the service is requested.

---

```csharp
Guid.NewGuid()
```

Generates a unique ID so we can easily see when a new service instance is created.

---

# 9. Real Project Usage ⭐

In my ASP.NET Core Web API project, I primarily used the **Scoped** lifetime for business services and repository classes because each HTTP request needed its own service instance. Entity Framework Core `DbContext` was also registered with a scoped lifetime to ensure all database operations within a request used the same context instance. Framework services like logging and configuration were provided by ASP.NET Core using appropriate lifetimes, while lightweight helper classes can be registered as transient when a fresh instance is preferred.

---

# 10. Best Practices

* Use **Scoped** for business services and `DbContext`.
* Use **Singleton** only for thread-safe shared services.
* Use **Transient** for lightweight, stateless services.
* Choose the lifetime based on the service's responsibility.
* Keep singleton services free of request-specific data.

---

# 11. Common Mistakes

* Registering `DbContext` as Singleton.
* Using Singleton for services that store request-specific data.
* Choosing Transient for expensive objects that could be reused.
* Selecting service lifetimes without understanding object sharing.
* Mixing incompatible lifetimes without considering dependencies.

---

# 12. Key Points to Remember

* ASP.NET Core provides **three service lifetimes**.
* **Singleton** → One instance for the application.
* **Scoped** → One instance per HTTP request.
* **Transient** → New instance every time it is requested.
* Register lifetimes using `AddSingleton()`, `AddScoped()`, or `AddTransient()`.
* Choosing the correct lifetime improves performance and application reliability.

---

# 13. Summary

Service lifetimes determine how long service instances live in the ASP.NET Core Dependency Injection container. **Singleton** creates one shared instance for the entire application, **Scoped** creates one instance per HTTP request, and **Transient** creates a new instance every time the service is requested. Understanding and selecting the correct lifetime is essential for building efficient, maintainable, and reliable ASP.NET Core applications.

---
# Module 7 – Dependency Injection

# Topic 3 – Service Lifetimes (Singleton, Scoped, Transient) (Part 2 – Interview Preparation)

---

# ⭐ Possible Interview Questions

## Q1. What are Service Lifetimes in ASP.NET Core?

### Answer

Service Lifetimes define how long a service instance lives in the Dependency Injection (DI) container. When a service is registered, we choose how often a new object should be created. ASP.NET Core provides three service lifetimes: **Singleton**, **Scoped**, and **Transient**. Each lifetime is designed for different scenarios. Choosing the correct lifetime improves application performance, memory usage, and reliability. It is an important concept in Dependency Injection.

---

## Q2. What is a Singleton service?

### Answer

A Singleton service is created only once during the application's lifetime. The same instance is shared across all requests and all users until the application stops. It is suitable for services that do not store request-specific data. Singleton services are efficient because the object is created only once. Examples include logging, caching, and configuration services. Singleton services should be thread-safe because multiple requests may use them at the same time.

---

## Q3. What is a Scoped service?

### Answer

A Scoped service is created once for each HTTP request. Within the same request, all classes receive the same service instance. When a new request arrives, a new instance is created. Scoped is the most commonly used lifetime in ASP.NET Core Web APIs. Business services, repositories, and Entity Framework Core `DbContext` are typically registered as Scoped. This ensures consistent data throughout a request.

---

## Q4. What is a Transient service?

### Answer

A Transient service creates a new object every time it is requested from the DI container. Even within the same HTTP request, different injections receive different instances. It is suitable for lightweight and stateless services. Since a new object is created each time, Transient services should not be used for expensive object creation unless necessary. Helper and utility classes commonly use this lifetime.

---

## Q5. What is the difference between Singleton, Scoped, and Transient?

### Answer

The main difference is how long the service instance lives. **Singleton** creates one instance for the entire application. **Scoped** creates one instance for each HTTP request. **Transient** creates a new instance every time the service is requested. Singleton shares the same object everywhere, Scoped shares it only within one request, and Transient never shares objects. Choosing the correct lifetime depends on the service's purpose.

---

## Q6. Which lifetime is used for Entity Framework Core `DbContext`?

### Answer

Entity Framework Core `DbContext` is normally registered with the **Scoped** lifetime. This means one `DbContext` instance is used throughout a single HTTP request. It ensures all database operations in the request use the same context and transaction. Registering `DbContext` as Singleton can cause concurrency and data consistency problems. Scoped is the recommended lifetime for `DbContext`.

---

## Q7. When should you use Singleton?

### Answer

Singleton should be used when the same service instance can safely be shared across the entire application. It is suitable for thread-safe services that do not depend on request-specific data. Examples include logging, application configuration, and caching. Because the object is reused, it improves performance by reducing object creation. Developers should ensure Singleton services are thread-safe.

---

## Q8. When should you use Transient?

### Answer

Transient should be used for lightweight, stateless services that require a new instance every time they are requested. Since each injection gets a fresh object, there is no shared state between different classes. It is useful for helper classes, formatters, and small utility services. Developers should avoid using Transient for expensive objects that can be reused.

---

## Q9. Which lifetime did you use in your project?

### Answer

In my ASP.NET Core Web API project, I mainly used the **Scoped** lifetime for business services and repository classes. Entity Framework Core `DbContext` was also registered as Scoped so that all database operations within a request used the same context instance. This ensured data consistency and followed ASP.NET Core best practices. Framework services like logging were provided using their appropriate lifetimes.

---

# ⭐ Project-Based Interview Questions

## Q1. Why did you use Scoped services in your project?

### Answer

I used Scoped services because each HTTP request should have its own service instance. This works well with Entity Framework Core `DbContext`, allowing all operations in a request to use the same database context while keeping different requests isolated.

---

## Q2. What problems can occur if `DbContext` is registered as Singleton?

### Answer
