> **Interview Tip:** `ILogger<T>` writes the log message, while the logging provider decides where the log is stored or displayed.

---

# 2. Definition

### Logging Provider

A Logging Provider is a component that receives log messages from `ILogger<T>` and writes them to a specific destination.

Examples of destinations include:

* Console Window
* Visual Studio Debug Window
* Log Files
* Azure Monitor
* Third-party logging systems

### One-Line Interview Definition

> A Logging Provider determines where log messages generated by `ILogger<T>` are stored or displayed.

---

# 3. Why Do We Use Logging Providers?

Without logging providers

* Log messages would have no destination.
* Developers couldn't monitor the application.
* Troubleshooting would become difficult.

With logging providers

* Logs can be viewed in different locations.
* Multiple providers can be used together.
* Easy debugging and monitoring.
* Better production support.

### Benefits

* Flexible logging
* Multiple output destinations
* Better monitoring
* Easy troubleshooting
* Provider can be changed without modifying application code

---

# 4. How It Works

Application Writes Log

↓

`ILogger<T>`

↓

Logging Abstraction

↓

Logging Provider

↓

Console / Debug Window / File

↓

Developer Reviews Logs

---

# 5. Key Concepts

---

# 1. Console Provider

The Console Provider writes log messages to the terminal or command prompt.

Example

```csharp id="7cm77e"
_logger.LogInformation(
    "Application started.");
```

Output

```text id="ptjlwm"
info: ProductService[0]
      Application started.
```

Use Cases

* Local development
* Docker containers
* Linux servers
* Quick debugging

---

# 2. Debug Provider

The Debug Provider writes log messages to the Visual Studio **Output** window.

It is mainly used while debugging.

Example

```csharp id="7ibey5"
_logger.LogInformation(
    "Fetching customer details.");
```

Output appears in

```text id="i4e26y"
Visual Studio

Output Window

Debug
```

Use Cases

* Debugging
* Local development
* Visual Studio

---

# 3. File Provider

ASP.NET Core does **not** include a built-in file logging provider.

To write logs to files, developers usually use third-party libraries such as:

* Serilog
* NLog

Example using Serilog

```csharp id="emay6e"
Log.Logger = new LoggerConfiguration()
    .WriteTo.File("Logs/app.log")
    .CreateLogger();
```

Example log file

```text id="avmhyv"
2026-07-23
Information
Application Started
```

Use Cases

* Production
* Audit logs
* Long-term log storage
* Troubleshooting

---

# 4. Multiple Providers

ASP.NET Core allows multiple providers to be used together.

Example

* Console
* Debug
* File

The same log message can be written to all three providers.

---

# 5. Configuring Logging

Logging providers are configured in `Program.cs`.

Example

```csharp id="eb3sgc"
var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args);

builder.Logging.ClearProviders();

builder.Logging.AddConsole();

builder.Logging.AddDebug();
```

This configuration

* Removes default providers.
* Adds Console logging.
* Adds Debug logging.

---

# 6. Complete Code Example

```csharp id="gdlrlg"
var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args);

builder.Logging.ClearProviders();

builder.Logging.AddConsole();

builder.Logging.AddDebug();

var app = builder.Build();
```

Service

```csharp id="cud6yi"
public class ProductService
{
    private readonly ILogger<ProductService> _logger;

    public ProductService(
        ILogger<ProductService> logger)
    {
        _logger = logger;
    }

    public void GetProduct()
    {
        _logger.LogInformation(
            "Product retrieved successfully.");
    }
}
```

---

# 7. Code Explanation

```csharp id="18wfwp"
ClearProviders()
```

Removes the default logging providers.

---

```csharp id="0o9gjb"
AddConsole()
```

Adds Console logging.

---

```csharp id="wd0uy0"
AddDebug()
```

Adds Debug logging.

---

```csharp id="mjyuzh"
ILogger<T>
```

Creates the log message.

---

```csharp id="lp0jyn"
Console Provider
```

Displays logs in the terminal.

---

```csharp id="h93kpb"
Debug Provider
```

Displays logs in the Visual Studio Output window.

---

# 8. Real Project Usage ⭐

In my ASP.NET Core Web API project, we used the built-in Console and Debug providers during development to monitor API requests, validation failures, and exceptions. These providers made it easy to debug the application while working locally. For production environments, file-based logging is commonly implemented using libraries such as Serilog to store logs for troubleshooting and auditing.

> **Note:** Based on my project experience, I used the built-in logging providers during development. File logging with Serilog is a common production approach, even if it was not directly implemented in my project.

---

# 9. Best Practices

* Use Console logging during development.
* Use Debug logging while debugging in Visual Studio.
* Use file logging or centralized logging in production.
* Configure only the providers you need.
* Regularly monitor and rotate log files when using file logging.

---

# 10. Common Mistakes

* Assuming ASP.NET Core has built-in file logging.
* Logging to too many providers unnecessarily.
* Forgetting to remove unnecessary providers.
* Not monitoring log file size.
* Logging sensitive information.

---

# 11. Key Points to Remember

* Logging providers decide where logs are stored.
* `ILogger<T>` creates log messages.
* Console Provider writes logs to the terminal.
* Debug Provider writes logs to the Visual Studio Output window.
* ASP.NET Core does not include a built-in File Provider.
* File logging is commonly implemented using Serilog or NLog.
* Multiple logging providers can be used together.

---

# 12. Summary

Logging providers determine the destination of log messages created by `ILogger<T>`. ASP.NET Core includes built-in providers such as **Console** and **Debug**, which are commonly used during development. For file logging, developers typically use third-party libraries like **Serilog** or **NLog**, since ASP.NET Core does not provide a built-in file logging provider. Using the appropriate logging provider helps with debugging, monitoring, and production troubleshooting.

---
# Module 9 – Logging

# Topic 4 – Logging Providers (Console, Debug, File) (Part 2 – Interview Preparation)

---

# ⭐ Possible Interview Questions

## Q1. What are Logging Providers in ASP.NET Core?

### Answer

Logging Providers are components that receive log messages from `ILogger<T>` and write them to a specific destination. The destination can be the Console, Visual Studio Output window, log files, or third-party logging systems. ASP.NET Core separates logging from the destination using a logging abstraction. This allows developers to change the logging provider without modifying the application code. Logging providers make monitoring and troubleshooting easier.

---

## Q2. Why do we use Logging Providers?

### Answer

Logging Providers determine where application logs are stored or displayed. Without a logging provider, log messages would have no destination. They help developers monitor application behavior, debug issues, and analyze errors. Multiple providers can be used at the same time to send logs to different locations. This provides flexibility and improves application maintenance.

---

## Q3. What is the Console Logging Provider?

### Answer

The Console Logging Provider writes log messages to the terminal or command prompt. It is commonly used during development because developers can immediately view log output while running the application. It is also useful in Docker containers and Linux servers where console logs are monitored. It is one of the built-in logging providers in ASP.NET Core.

Example:

```csharp id="ql6y5r"
builder.Logging.AddConsole();
```

---

## Q4. What is the Debug Logging Provider?

### Answer

The Debug Logging Provider writes log messages to the Visual Studio **Output** window while debugging an application. It helps developers monitor logs without using the console. It is mainly useful during development and debugging. Like the Console provider, it is built into ASP.NET Core.

Example:

```csharp id="50rc5t"
builder.Logging.AddDebug();
```

---

## Q5. Does ASP.NET Core provide built-in File Logging?

### Answer

No. ASP.NET Core does not include a built-in file logging provider. To write logs to files, developers usually use third-party libraries such as **Serilog** or **NLog**. These libraries integrate with the ASP.NET Core logging abstraction and allow logs to be stored in files or other destinations. They are commonly used in production applications.

---

## Q6. Can multiple Logging Providers be used together?

### Answer

Yes. ASP.NET Core allows multiple logging providers to be configured at the same time. For example, the same log message can be written to both the Console and the Debug window. In production, logs may also be written to files using Serilog. This makes monitoring easier because logs are available in multiple locations.

---

## Q7. What is `ClearProviders()`?

### Answer

`ClearProviders()` removes all default logging providers from the application. Developers use it when they want full control over which logging providers are enabled. After calling `ClearProviders()`, they can add only the required providers such as Console or Debug. This helps avoid unnecessary logging.

Example:

```csharp id="zjttxe"
builder.Logging.ClearProviders();
```

---

## Q8. How do you configure Logging Providers?

### Answer

Logging Providers are configured in `Program.cs` using the `builder.Logging` object. Developers can add or remove providers as needed. ASP.NET Core includes methods such as `AddConsole()` and `AddDebug()`. Third-party providers like Serilog can also be configured here. The configuration is applied when the application starts.

Example:

```csharp id="3v3x4k"
builder.Logging.ClearProviders();

builder.Logging.AddConsole();

builder.Logging.AddDebug();
```

---

## Q9. How did you use Logging Providers in your project?

### Answer

In my ASP.NET Core Web API project, I used the built-in Console and Debug logging providers during development. They helped monitor API requests, validation failures, and exceptions while testing the application. For production environments, file-based logging using tools like Serilog is a common approach for storing logs and troubleshooting issues.

---

# ⭐ Project-Based Interview Questions

## Q1. Which Logging Providers did you use in your project?

### Answer

I mainly used the built-in Console and Debug providers during development. They allowed me to monitor API execution and exceptions while working in Visual Studio.

---

## Q2. Why didn't you use file logging directly in ASP.NET Core?

### Answer

ASP.NET Core does not provide a built-in file logging provider. File logging is usually implemented using third-party libraries such as Serilog or NLog, which integrate with the ASP.NET Core logging system.

---

## Q3. Why can multiple providers be useful?

### Answer

Multiple providers allow the same log message to be sent to different destinations at the same time. For example, logs can appear in the Console during development and also be saved to files for future analysis.

---

# ⭐ Rapid Fire Questions

1. What is a Logging Provider? → **A component that stores or displays log messages**
2. Which provider writes logs to the terminal? → **Console Provider**
3. Which provider writes logs to the Visual Studio Output window? → **Debug Provider**
4. Does ASP.NET Core have built-in file logging? → **No**
5. Which library is commonly used for file logging? → **Serilog**
6. Name another file logging library. → **NLog**
7. Which method removes default logging providers? → **`ClearProviders()`**
8. Which method adds Console logging? → **`AddConsole()`**
9. Which method adds Debug logging? → **`AddDebug()`**
10. Can multiple logging providers be configured together? → **Yes**

---

# ⭐ Follow-up Questions

## Q1. Can you replace the Console provider with Serilog without changing your logging code?

### Answer

Yes. Since the application uses `ILogger<T>`, only the logging provider configuration changes. The logging statements in the application remain the same because they use the ASP.NET Core logging abstraction.

---

## Q2. Why is the logging abstraction important?

### Answer

The logging abstraction separates the application code from the logging provider. This allows developers to switch providers, such as moving from the Console provider to Serilog, without modifying business logic.

---

# ⭐ Interview Tips

* Remember that **`ILogger<T>` creates log messages**, while **Logging Providers decide where they go**.
* Mention that **Console** and **Debug** are built-in providers.
* Explain that **ASP.NET Core does not include a built-in File Provider**.
* Mention **Serilog** and **NLog** as common choices for file logging.
* In your project example, state that you used **Console** and **Debug** during development.

---

# ⭐ Revision Notes

* Logging Providers determine where logs are stored.
* `ILogger<T>` writes log messages through the logging abstraction.
* Console Provider writes logs to the terminal.
* Debug Provider writes logs to the Visual Studio Output window.
* ASP.NET Core does not provide built-in file logging.
* Serilog and NLog are popular file logging libraries.
* `ClearProviders()` removes default providers.
* `AddConsole()` enables Console logging.
* `AddDebug()` enables Debug logging.
* Multiple providers can be used together.

---

# ⭐ Keywords

**Logging Provider, `ILogger<T>`, Logging Abstraction, Console Provider, Debug Provider, File Logging, Serilog, NLog, `ClearProviders()`, `AddConsole()`, `AddDebug()`, ASP.NET Core Web API**

---
# Module 10 — Data Access with EF Core

# Topic 1 – EF Core Overview (DbContext, DbSet) (Part 1 – Learning)

---

# 1. Introduction

Most applications need to store and retrieve data from a database. Writing SQL queries for every database operation can become repetitive and difficult to maintain.

**Entity Framework Core (EF Core)** is Microsoft's Object-Relational Mapper (ORM) that allows developers to work with databases using C# objects instead of writing SQL for every operation.

The two most important classes in EF Core are:

* **DbContext**
* **DbSet**

These classes are the foundation of every EF Core application.

> **Interview Tip:** DbContext manages the database connection and changes, while DbSet represents a database table.

---

# 2. Definition

### Entity Framework Core (EF Core)

Entity Framework Core is Microsoft's lightweight, open-source ORM that simplifies database operations by mapping C# classes to database tables.

### DbContext

`DbContext` is the main class in EF Core that manages the database connection, tracks entity changes, and performs database operations.

### DbSet

`DbSet<T>` represents a table in the database. It allows developers to query and modify records.

### One-Line Interview Definition

> **EF Core** is an ORM that lets us work with databases using C# objects. **DbContext** manages the database, and **DbSet** represents a table.

---

# 3. Why Do We Use EF Core?

Without EF Core

* Write SQL manually.
* Open and close database connections.
* Map query results to C# objects.
* Handle CRUD operations manually.

With EF Core

* Less code.
* Faster development.
* Automatic object mapping.
* Easy CRUD operations.
* LINQ support.
* Database change tracking.

### Benefits

* Improves productivity.
* Reduces SQL code.
* Easy maintenance.
* Strong type checking.
* Supports multiple databases.

---

# 4. How It Works

Application

↓

Entity Class

↓

DbContext

↓

EF Core

↓

SQL Query

↓

SQL Server Database

↓

Result Returned as C# Objects

---

# 5. Key Concepts

---

# 1. Entity Class

An entity class represents a database table.

Example

```csharp
public class Product
{
    public int Id { get; set; }

    public string Name { get; set; }

    public decimal Price { get; set; }
}
```

Database Table

| Id | Name   | Price |
| -- | ------ | ----- |
| 1  | Laptop | 50000 |
| 2  | Mouse  | 700   |

Each object represents one row.

---

# 2. DbContext

`DbContext` is the heart of EF Core.

Responsibilities

* Connects to the database.
* Tracks changes.
* Executes SQL queries.
* Saves data.
* Manages transactions.

Example

```csharp
using Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore;

public class AppDbContext : DbContext
{
    public AppDbContext(
        DbContextOptions<AppDbContext> options)
        : base(options)
    {
    }

    public DbSet<Product> Products { get; set; }
}
```

Here,

`AppDbContext` represents the database.

---

# 3. DbSet

`DbSet<T>` represents a table.

Example

```csharp
public DbSet<Product> Products { get; set; }
```

Meaning

* Product class → Products table

Common Operations

* Add
* Update
* Delete
* Read

---

# 4. Registering DbContext

In `Program.cs`

```csharp
builder.Services.AddDbContext<AppDbContext>(options =>
    options.UseSqlServer(
        builder.Configuration.GetConnectionString("DefaultConnection")));
```

This registers `AppDbContext` with Dependency Injection and connects it to SQL Server.

---

# 5. Using DbContext

Example

```csharp
public class ProductService
{
    private readonly AppDbContext _context;

    public ProductService(AppDbContext context)
    {
        _context = context;
    }

    public List<Product> GetProducts()
    {
        return _context.Products.ToList();
    }
}
```

Here

* `_context` accesses the database.
* `Products` accesses the Product table.
* `ToList()` retrieves all records.

---

# 6. CRUD with DbSet

### Insert

```csharp
_context.Products.Add(product);

_context.SaveChanges();
```

---

### Read

```csharp
var products =
    _context.Products.ToList();
```

---

### Update

```csharp
_context.Products.Update(product);

_context.SaveChanges();
```

---

### Delete

```csharp
_context.Products.Remove(product);

_context.SaveChanges();
```

---

# 6. Complete Code Example

## Product Model

```csharp
public class Product
{
    public int Id { get; set; }

    public string Name { get; set; }

    public decimal Price { get; set; }
}
```

---

## AppDbContext

```csharp
using Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore;

public class AppDbContext : DbContext
{
    public AppDbContext(
        DbContextOptions<AppDbContext> options)
        : base(options)
    {
    }

    public DbSet<Product> Products { get; set; }
}
```

---

## Service

```csharp
public class ProductService
{
    private readonly AppDbContext _context;

    public ProductService(AppDbContext context)
    {
        _context = context;
    }

    public List<Product> GetProducts()
    {
        return _context.Products.ToList();
    }

    public void AddProduct(Product product)
    {
        _context.Products.Add(product);

        _context.SaveChanges();
    }
}
```

---

# 7. Code Explanation

```csharp
DbContext
```

Base class that manages database operations.

---

```csharp
DbSet<Product>
```

Represents the **Products** table.

---

```csharp
_context.Products
```

Accesses the Product table.

---

```csharp
ToList()
```

Retrieves all records.

---

```csharp
Add()
```

Adds a new entity.

---

```csharp
SaveChanges()
```

Saves all pending changes to the database.

---

# 8. Real Project Usage ⭐

In my ASP.NET Core Web API project, we used **Entity Framework Core** with **SQL Server** for database operations. We created an `AppDbContext` class to manage the database connection and defined `DbSet` properties for different entities. Services used `AppDbContext` through Dependency Injection to perform CRUD operations such as retrieving records, inserting new data, updating existing records, and deleting records. This reduced the need to write SQL queries manually and improved code maintainability.

---

# 9. Best Practices

* Register `DbContext` using Dependency Injection.
* Use one `DbContext` per request (Scoped lifetime).
* Keep entity classes simple.
* Use meaningful `DbSet` names.
* Dispose of `DbContext` automatically through DI.
* Use asynchronous methods (`ToListAsync()`, `SaveChangesAsync()`) in Web APIs.

---

# 10. Common Mistakes

* Creating `DbContext` manually using `new`.
* Forgetting to call `SaveChanges()`.
* Using one `DbContext` instance for too long.
* Not registering `DbContext` in DI.
* Mixing business logic inside `DbContext`.

---

# 11. Key Points to Remember

* EF Core is Microsoft's ORM.
* `DbContext` manages the database.
* `DbSet<T>` represents a database table.
* Entity classes represent table rows.
* `SaveChanges()` persists changes.
* `Add()`, `Update()`, `Remove()`, and `ToList()` are common `DbSet` methods.
* `DbContext` should be registered using Dependency Injection.

---

# 12. Summary

Entity Framework Core (EF Core) simplifies database access by allowing developers to work with C# objects instead of writing SQL for every operation. The **DbContext** class manages the database connection, tracks changes, and executes database operations, while **DbSet<T>** represents individual database tables. Together, they form the foundation of data access in ASP.NET Core applications, making CRUD operations simpler, more maintainable, and strongly typed.

---
# Module 10 – Data Access with EF Core

# Topic 1 – EF Core Overview (DbContext, DbSet) (Part 2 – Interview Preparation)

---

# ⭐ Possible Interview Questions

## Q1. What is Entity Framework Core (EF Core)?

### Answer

Entity Framework Core (EF Core) is Microsoft's Object-Relational Mapper (ORM) for .NET applications. It allows developers to work with databases using C# objects instead of writing SQL queries for every operation. EF Core automatically maps C# classes to database tables. It supports CRUD operations, LINQ queries, and change tracking. It helps reduce development time and improves code maintainability.

---

## Q2. What is DbContext?

### Answer

`DbContext` is the main class in EF Core that manages communication with the database. It establishes the database connection, tracks changes made to entities, executes queries, and saves changes to the database. Every EF Core application has at least one class that inherits from `DbContext`. It acts as a bridge between the application and the database.

Example:

```csharp
public class AppDbContext : DbContext
{
    public DbSet<Product> Products { get; set; }
}
```

---

## Q3. What is DbSet?

### Answer

`DbSet<T>` represents a table in the database. It allows developers to perform CRUD operations such as inserting, retrieving, updating, and deleting records. Each `DbSet` property corresponds to one database table. Developers use `DbSet` with LINQ to query data. It is accessed through the `DbContext`.

Example:

```csharp
public DbSet<Product> Products { get; set; }
```

---

## Q4. What is the relationship between DbContext and DbSet?

### Answer

`DbContext` represents the entire database, while `DbSet` represents an individual table within that database. The `DbContext` manages the database connection and operations, and each `DbSet` provides access to a specific entity table. Developers use the `DbContext` to access the required `DbSet`. Together, they form the core of EF Core.

---

## Q5. Why do we use EF Core instead of writing SQL queries?

### Answer

EF Core reduces the need to write SQL queries manually. It automatically converts LINQ queries into SQL. It provides strong type checking, change tracking, and object mapping. This reduces development time and makes the code easier to maintain. It also minimizes SQL-related errors.

---

## Q6. How do you register DbContext in ASP.NET Core?

### Answer

`DbContext` is registered in the Dependency Injection container using the `AddDbContext()` method. During registration, the database provider and connection string are also configured. Once registered, it can be injected into controllers and services. ASP.NET Core manages its lifetime automatically.

Example:

```csharp
builder.Services.AddDbContext<AppDbContext>(options =>
    options.UseSqlServer(
        builder.Configuration.GetConnectionString("DefaultConnection")));
```

---

## Q7. What is SaveChanges()?

### Answer

`SaveChanges()` saves all pending changes made through the `DbContext` to the database. Operations like `Add()`, `Update()`, and `Remove()` only modify the entity in memory. The changes are actually written to the database only when `SaveChanges()` is called. Without calling it, no data is saved.

Example:

```csharp
_context.SaveChanges();
```

---

## Q8. What are the common operations performed using DbSet?

### Answer

`DbSet` is used for CRUD operations. Developers can use `Add()` to insert records, `ToList()` or `FirstOrDefault()` to retrieve data, `Update()` to modify records, and `Remove()` to delete records. After insert, update, or delete operations, `SaveChanges()` is called to save the changes.

---

## Q9. How did you use EF Core in your project?

### Answer

In my ASP.NET Core Web API project, I used EF Core with SQL Server for database operations. We created an `AppDbContext` class and defined `DbSet` properties for different entities. The `AppDbContext` was injected into services using Dependency Injection. We used it to perform CRUD operations such as retrieving, inserting, updating, and deleting records. We also used LINQ queries to fetch data from the database.

---

## Q10. Why is DbContext registered as Scoped?

### Answer

`DbContext` is registered with a Scoped lifetime so that one instance is created for each HTTP request. This ensures that all database operations within a request use the same context. It also prevents concurrency issues and manages database connections efficiently. Scoped is the recommended lifetime for `DbContext` in ASP.NET Core.

---

# ⭐ Project-Based Interview Questions

## Q1. How did you access the database in your project?

### Answer

I used `AppDbContext`, which was injected into the service using Dependency Injection. Through the `DbSet` properties, I performed CRUD operations on SQL Server tables using EF Core.

---

## Q2. Did you write SQL queries for every operation?

### Answer

No. I used EF Core with LINQ queries for most database operations. EF Core automatically converted the LINQ queries into SQL statements.

---

## Q3. What happens if SaveChanges() is not called?

### Answer

The changes remain only in memory and are not saved to the database. Methods like `Add()`, `Update()`, and `Remove()` require `SaveChanges()` or `SaveChangesAsync()` to persist the changes.

---

# ⭐ Rapid Fire Questions

1. What does EF Core stand for? → **Entity Framework Core**
2. What is EF Core? → **An ORM for .NET**
3. Which class manages the database? → **DbContext**
4. Which class represents a table? → **DbSet**
5. Which method saves changes to the database? → **SaveChanges()**
6. Which method inserts a record? → **Add()**
7. Which method updates a record? → **Update()**
8. Which method deletes a record? → **Remove()**
9. Which method retrieves all records? → **ToList()**
10. Which lifetime is recommended for DbContext? → **Scoped**

---

# ⭐ Follow-up Questions

## Q1. Can one DbContext contain multiple DbSet properties?

### Answer

Yes. A single `DbContext` can contain multiple `DbSet` properties, where each `DbSet` represents a different table in the database.

Example:

```csharp
public DbSet<Product> Products { get; set; }

public DbSet<Customer> Customers { get; set; }

public DbSet<Order> Orders { get; set; }
```

---

## Q2. Can we use asynchronous methods with EF Core?

### Answer

Yes. EF Core provides asynchronous methods such as `ToListAsync()`, `FirstOrDefaultAsync()`, and `SaveChangesAsync()`. These methods improve application scalability by avoiding blocking database operations, especially in Web APIs.

---

# ⭐ Interview Tips

* Remember that **EF Core is an ORM**.
* Explain that **DbContext manages the database**, while **DbSet represents a table**.
* Mention that **SaveChanges()** is required to persist changes.
* State that **DbContext is registered using Dependency Injection**.
* In your project example, mention using **EF Core with SQL Server and LINQ**.

---

# ⭐ Revision Notes

* EF Core is Microsoft's ORM.
* `DbContext` manages database operations.
* `DbSet<T>` represents a database table.
* Entity classes map to database tables.
* `Add()`, `Update()`, `Remove()`, and `ToList()` are common `DbSet` methods.
* `SaveChanges()` writes changes to the database.
* `DbContext` is registered using `AddDbContext()`.
* The recommended lifetime for `DbContext` is **Scoped**.
* EF Core supports LINQ queries.
* Use asynchronous methods like `ToListAsync()` and `SaveChangesAsync()` in Web APIs.

---

# ⭐ Keywords

**Entity Framework Core (EF Core), ORM, DbContext, DbSet, Entity, LINQ, SQL Server, `AddDbContext()`, `SaveChanges()`, `SaveChangesAsync()`, CRUD, Dependency Injection**

---
# Module 10 – Data Access with EF Core

# Topic 2 – Code-First Entity Configuration (Data Annotations vs Fluent API) (Part 1 – Learning)

---

# 1. Introduction

When using **Code-First** in Entity Framework Core, we first create C# classes (entities), and EF Core automatically creates the database tables based on those classes.

Sometimes, the default table structure is not enough. We may need to:

* Make a field required.
* Set the maximum length.
* Rename a column.
* Define relationships.
* Configure composite keys.

To customize how EF Core creates the database, we use **Entity Configuration**.

There are two ways to configure entities:

1. **Data Annotations**
2. **Fluent API**

> **Interview Tip:** Data Annotations are attribute-based configurations inside the model class, while Fluent API is configuration written inside `OnModelCreating()` in the `DbContext`.

---

# 2. Definition

### Code-First

Code-First is an EF Core approach where developers create C# classes first, and EF Core generates the database schema from those classes.

### Data Annotations

Data Annotations are attributes placed directly on model properties to configure tables and columns.

### Fluent API

Fluent API is a configuration method written inside the `OnModelCreating()` method of the `DbContext`. It provides more advanced configuration options.

### One-Line Interview Definition

> Code-First creates the database from C# classes. Entity configuration can be done using **Data Annotations** or **Fluent API**.

---

# 3. Why Do We Use Entity Configuration?

Without configuration

* Default table names are generated.
* Default column names are used.
* Default data types are selected.
* Relationships may not match business requirements.

With configuration

* Control table names.
* Configure column names.
* Set maximum length.
* Make fields required.
* Configure relationships.
* Define keys and indexes.

### Benefits

* Better database design.
* Cleaner schema.
* More control.
* Easier maintenance.
* Supports business rules.

---

# 4. How It Works

Entity Class

↓

Entity Configuration

(Data Annotations or Fluent API)

↓

EF Core

↓

Migration

↓

SQL Server Database

---

# 5. Key Concepts

---

# 1. Code-First Model

Example

```csharp
public class Product
{
    public int Id { get; set; }

    public string Name { get; set; }

    public decimal Price { get; set; }
}
```

EF Core creates

```text
Products

Id

Name

Price
```

---

# 2. Data Annotations

Configuration is written directly in the model class.

Example

```csharp
using System.ComponentModel.DataAnnotations;

public class Product
{
    public int Id { get; set; }

    [Required]
    [MaxLength(100)]
    public string Name { get; set; }

    [Range(1,100000)]
    public decimal Price { get; set; }
}
```

Meaning

* Required field
* Maximum 100 characters
* Price must be between 1 and 100000

---

# 3. Common Data Annotation Attributes

| Attribute        | Purpose             |
| ---------------- | ------------------- |
| `[Key]`          | Primary Key         |
| `[Required]`     | Required field      |
| `[MaxLength]`    | Maximum length      |
| `[StringLength]` | String length limit |
| `[Range]`        | Numeric range       |
| `[EmailAddress]` | Valid email         |
| `[Phone]`        | Valid phone         |
| `[Column]`       | Column name/type    |
| `[Table]`        | Table name          |

---

# 4. Fluent API

Fluent API configuration is written inside `OnModelCreating()`.

Example

```csharp
using Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore;

public class AppDbContext : DbContext
{
    public DbSet<Product> Products { get; set; }

    protected override void OnModelCreating(ModelBuilder modelBuilder)
    {
        modelBuilder.Entity<Product>()
            .Property(p => p.Name)
            .HasMaxLength(100)
            .IsRequired();

        modelBuilder.Entity<Product>()
            .Property(p => p.Price)
            .HasColumnType("decimal(18,2)");
    }
}
```

---

# 5. Why Fluent API?

Fluent API supports advanced configurations that Data Annotations cannot handle easily.

Examples

* Composite Keys
* Relationships
* Cascade Delete
* Indexes
* Alternate Keys
* Precision
* Default Values

---

# 6. Data Annotations vs Fluent API

| Data Annotations     | Fluent API                |
| -------------------- | ------------------------- |
| Uses attributes      | Uses code                 |
| Inside model class   | Inside DbContext          |
| Simple configuration | Advanced configuration    |
| Less flexible        | More flexible             |
| Easy to learn        | Better for large projects |

---

# 7. Complete Code Example

## Product Model

```csharp
using System.ComponentModel.DataAnnotations;

public class Product
{
    public int Id { get; set; }

    [Required]
    [MaxLength(100)]
    public string Name { get; set; }

    public decimal Price { get; set; }
}
```

---

## DbContext

```csharp
using Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore;

public class AppDbContext : DbContext
{
    public DbSet<Product> Products { get; set; }

    protected override void OnModelCreating(ModelBuilder modelBuilder)
    {
        modelBuilder.Entity<Product>()
            .Property(p => p.Price)
            .HasColumnType("decimal(18,2)");
    }
}
```

---

# 8. Code Explanation

```csharp
[Required]
```

Makes the field mandatory.

---

```csharp
[MaxLength(100)]
```

Limits the column to 100 characters.

---

```csharp
OnModelCreating()
```

Method used for Fluent API configuration.

---

```csharp
HasMaxLength(100)
```

Sets the maximum column length.

---

```csharp
IsRequired()
```

Makes the column NOT NULL.

---

```csharp
HasColumnType("decimal(18,2)")
```

Specifies the SQL Server column type.

---

# 9. Real Project Usage ⭐

In my ASP.NET Core Web API project, we used the **Code-First** approach with EF Core. For simple validations such as required fields and maximum string length, we used **Data Annotations** in the entity classes. For database-specific configurations like column types and relationships, **Fluent API** is the recommended approach because it keeps entity classes clean and provides more flexibility.

> **Note:** Based on my project experience, Data Annotations were commonly used. Fluent API is widely used for advanced configurations in larger applications.

---

# 10. Best Practices

* Use Code-First for new projects.
* Use Data Annotations for simple validation and configuration.
* Use Fluent API for advanced database mapping.
* Keep entity classes clean.
* Avoid duplicate configuration in both Data Annotations and Fluent API.
* Use Fluent API for relationships and composite keys.

---

# 11. Common Mistakes

* Mixing conflicting configurations.
* Using Data Annotations for complex mappings.
* Forgetting to create a migration after configuration changes.
* Hardcoding database-specific details unnecessarily.
* Overusing attributes in entity classes.

---

# 12. Key Points to Remember

* Code-First creates the database from C# classes.
* Data Annotations use attributes.
* Fluent API uses `OnModelCreating()`.
* Data Annotations are suitable for simple configurations.
* Fluent API is more powerful and flexible.
* Fluent API is preferred for complex mappings and relationships.
* Both approaches can be used together, but avoid conflicting configurations.

---

# 13. Summary

Code-First is an EF Core approach where the database schema is generated from C# entity classes. Entity configuration can be done using **Data Annotations** or **Fluent API**. Data Annotations are simple and attribute-based, making them suitable for basic validation and configuration. Fluent API is configured inside `OnModelCreating()` and provides advanced features such as relationship mapping, composite keys, indexes, and custom column types. In real-world applications, Data Annotations are often used for simple rules, while Fluent API is preferred for complex database configurations.

---
# Module 10 – Data Access with EF Core

# Topic 2 – Code-First Entity Configuration (Data Annotations vs Fluent API) (Part 2 – Interview Preparation)

---

# ⭐ Possible Interview Questions

## Q1. What is Code-First in Entity Framework Core?

### Answer

Code-First is an approach in Entity Framework Core where developers create C# entity classes first, and EF Core generates the database schema from those classes. The database tables, columns, and relationships are created using migrations. This approach allows developers to focus on the application code before creating the database. It is commonly used in new ASP.NET Core projects because it is easy to maintain and version control.

---

## Q2. What are Data Annotations?

### Answer

Data Annotations are attributes that are applied directly to entity class properties to configure the database schema and perform validation. They are simple to use and suitable for basic configurations like required fields, maximum length, and value ranges. These attributes are placed inside the model class. They make the code easy to understand for simple scenarios.

Example:

```csharp id="je5ux4"
[Required]
[MaxLength(100)]
public string Name { get; set; }
```

---

## Q3. What is Fluent API?

### Answer

Fluent API is a configuration method in EF Core that is written inside the `OnModelCreating()` method of the `DbContext`. It provides more flexibility than Data Annotations and supports advanced configurations such as relationships, composite keys, indexes, and custom column types. It keeps entity classes clean by moving database configuration into the `DbContext`.

Example:

```csharp id="uuxrb6"
modelBuilder.Entity<Product>()
    .Property(p => p.Name)
    .HasMaxLength(100)
    .IsRequired();
```

---

## Q4. What is the difference between Data Annotations and Fluent API?

### Answer

Data Annotations use attributes inside the entity class, while Fluent API uses code inside the `OnModelCreating()` method. Data Annotations are suitable for simple configurations such as required fields and maximum length. Fluent API is more flexible and is used for advanced configurations like relationships, composite keys, and indexes. In large projects, Fluent API is generally preferred because it separates database configuration from entity classes.

---

## Q5. When should you use Data Annotations?

### Answer

Data Annotations should be used for simple validation and basic database configuration. Examples include making a property required, limiting the length of a string, validating email addresses, or setting a value range. They are easy to read and require less code. They work well for small and medium-sized projects.

---

## Q6. When should you use Fluent API?

### Answer

Fluent API should be used when advanced database configuration is required. It is commonly used for configuring relationships, composite keys, indexes, default values, custom column types, and cascade delete behavior. It provides more control over the database schema than Data Annotations. It is widely used in enterprise applications.

---

## Q7. What is the purpose of `OnModelCreating()`?

### Answer

`OnModelCreating()` is a method in the `DbContext` where Fluent API configuration is written. EF Core calls this method while building the model. Developers use it to configure tables, columns, relationships, keys, and other database settings. It centralizes all advanced database configuration in one place.

Example:

```csharp id="vn97t2"
protected override void OnModelCreating(ModelBuilder modelBuilder)
{
    modelBuilder.Entity<Product>()
        .Property(p => p.Price)
        .HasColumnType("decimal(18,2)");
}
```

---

## Q8. Can Data Annotations and Fluent API be used together?

### Answer

Yes. Both approaches can be used together in the same project. Data Annotations are often used for simple configurations, while Fluent API is used for advanced mappings. However, developers should avoid configuring the same property differently in both places. If there is a conflict, Fluent API takes precedence.

---

## Q9. How did you configure entities in your project?

### Answer

In my ASP.NET Core Web API project, I mainly used Data Annotations for simple validations such as required fields and maximum string length. EF Core generated the database schema using the Code-First approach. For advanced database configuration like custom column types or relationships, Fluent API is the recommended approach because it provides greater flexibility and keeps the entity classes clean.

---

## Q10. Which approach do you prefer and why?

### Answer

For simple validation, I prefer Data Annotations because they are easy to read and implement. For complex database configurations such as relationships, composite keys, and indexes, I prefer Fluent API because it offers more flexibility and keeps the entity classes focused on business data. In larger projects, Fluent API is generally the better choice.

---

# ⭐ Project-Based Interview Questions

## Q1. Which configuration approach did you use in your project?

### Answer

I mainly used Data Annotations for basic validation like required fields and maximum length. For advanced database mapping, Fluent API is the recommended approach in larger applications.

---

## Q2. Why didn't you put all configurations in Data Annotations?

### Answer

Data Annotations are suitable for simple configurations, but they cannot easily handle advanced features like composite keys, indexes, and complex relationships. Fluent API is designed for those scenarios.

---

## Q3. What happens if both Data Annotations and Fluent API configure the same property?

### Answer

If there is a conflict, Fluent API overrides the Data Annotation configuration. Therefore, developers should avoid duplicate or conflicting configurations.

---

# ⭐ Rapid Fire Questions

1. What is Code-First? → **Database is created from C# classes**
2. Which class contains Fluent API configuration? → **DbContext**
3. Which method is used for Fluent API? → **`OnModelCreating()`**
4. Which approach uses attributes? → **Data Annotations**
5. Which approach is more flexible? → **Fluent API**
6. Which attribute makes a field mandatory? → **`[Required]`**
7. Which attribute limits string length? → **`[MaxLength]`**
8. Which method configures a required property in Fluent API? → **`IsRequired()`**
9. Which approach is preferred for complex mappings? → **Fluent API**
10. Which configuration takes precedence during conflicts? → **Fluent API**

---

# ⭐ Follow-up Questions

## Q1. Can Fluent API configure relationships between tables?

### Answer

Yes. Fluent API is commonly used to configure one-to-one, one-to-many, and many-to-many relationships. It also allows developers to configure foreign keys, cascade delete behavior, and navigation properties.

---

## Q2. Can Data Annotations create composite keys?

### Answer

No. Composite keys are typically configured using Fluent API because Data Annotations do not provide sufficient support for this type of advanced configuration in EF Core.

---

# ⭐ Interview Tips

* Explain that **Code-First** means creating the database from C# classes.
* Remember that **Data Annotations use attributes**, while **Fluent API uses `OnModelCreating()`**.
* Mention that **Fluent API is more powerful and flexible**.
* State that **Fluent API overrides Data Annotations** if both configure the same property.
* In your project example, mention using **Data Annotations for basic validation** and **Fluent API for advanced configuration**.

---

# ⭐ Revision Notes

* Code-First generates the database from C# classes.
* Data Annotations use attributes inside entity classes.
* Fluent API uses `OnModelCreating()` in `DbContext`.
* Data Annotations are suitable for simple validation.
* Fluent API supports advanced database mapping.
* `IsRequired()` makes a column NOT NULL.
* `HasMaxLength()` sets the maximum string length.
* Fluent API can configure relationships, composite keys, and indexes.
* Fluent API overrides conflicting Data Annotations.
* Both approaches can be used together when configured carefully.

---

# ⭐ Keywords

**Code-First, Entity Configuration, Data Annotations, Fluent API, `OnModelCreating()`, `ModelBuilder`, `IsRequired()`, `HasMaxLength()`, `HasColumnType()`, `[Required]`, `[MaxLength]`, EF Core, ASP.NET Core Web API**

---
# Module 10 – Data Access with EF Core

# Topic 3 – Migrations (Add, Update, Evolving Schema Safely) (Part 1 – Learning)

---

# 1. Introduction

After creating entity classes using the **Code-First** approach, we need a way to create and update the database.

Instead of manually writing SQL scripts every time the model changes, EF Core provides **Migrations**.

A Migration records the changes made to entity classes and generates SQL to update the database safely without losing existing data.

> **Interview Tip:** Migrations keep the database schema synchronized with the EF Core model.

---

# 2. Definition

### Migration

A Migration is a feature in Entity Framework Core that tracks changes made to entity classes and updates the database schema accordingly.

### One-Line Interview Definition

> A Migration is a version-controlled database schema change that keeps the database synchronized with the EF Core model.

---

# 3. Why Do We Use Migrations?

Without Migrations

* Update database manually.
* Write SQL scripts.
* Higher chance of mistakes.
* Difficult to maintain different environments.

With Migrations

* Database updates are automatic.
* Changes are version controlled.
* Team members stay synchronized.
* Easier deployment.
* Safer schema updates.

### Benefits

* Version control for database schema.
* Automatic SQL generation.
* Easy rollback support.
* Supports team collaboration.
* Keeps the database and model synchronized.

---

# 4. How It Works

Modify Entity Class

↓

Create Migration

↓

Migration File Generated

↓

Update Database

↓

Database Schema Updated

↓

Application Uses Updated Database

---

# 5. Key Concepts

---

# 1. Initial Migration

Suppose we create a Product model.

```csharp id="4pkbpn"
public class Product
{
    public int Id { get; set; }

    public string Name { get; set; }
}
```

Create Migration

```powershell id="u9yjbo"
Add-Migration InitialCreate
```

or

```bash id="krh7ms"
dotnet ef migrations add InitialCreate
```

EF Core creates a **Migrations** folder containing migration files.

---

# 2. Update Database

Apply the migration to SQL Server.

Package Manager Console

```powershell id="bjp71q"
Update-Database
```

CLI

```bash id="2kl5gv"
dotnet ef database update
```

This creates the database and tables.

---

# 3. Modifying the Model

Suppose we add a new property.

```csharp id="ylvco5"
public class Product
{
    public int Id { get; set; }

    public string Name { get; set; }

    public decimal Price { get; set; }
}
```

Create another migration.

```powershell id="qj0vml"
Add-Migration AddPriceColumn
```

Then update the database.

```powershell id="pc80li"
Update-Database
```

EF Core automatically adds the new column.

---

# 4. Migration Files

A migration contains two important methods.

```csharp id="7n1ru8"
public partial class AddPriceColumn : Migration
{
    protected override void Up(MigrationBuilder migrationBuilder)
    {
    }

    protected override void Down(MigrationBuilder migrationBuilder)
    {
    }
}
```

### Up()

Applies the database changes.

Example

* Create table
* Add column
* Rename column

### Down()

Reverts the database changes.

Example

* Drop table
* Remove column
* Undo changes

---

# 5. Evolving Schema Safely

Applications continue to evolve.

Examples

Version 1

```text id="dv3dj4"
Product

Id

Name
```

↓

Version 2

```text id="6k6plb"
Product

Id

Name

Price
```

↓

Version 3

```text id="ed44zf"
Product

Id

Name

Price

Stock
```

Each change becomes a new migration.

This keeps every database version synchronized safely.

---

# 6. Common Migration Commands

### Create Migration

```powershell id="fpljlwm"
Add-Migration MigrationName
```

---

### Apply Migration

```powershell id="o0djlwm"
Update-Database
```

---

### Remove Last Migration

```powershell id="kxynfk"
Remove-Migration
```

(Removes the last migration **only if it has not been applied**.)

---

### List Migrations

```powershell id="jlwmf1"
Get-Migration
```

---

# 7. Complete Code Example

## Product Model

```csharp id="tz4ctg"
public class Product
{
    public int Id { get; set; }

    public string Name { get; set; }

    public decimal Price { get; set; }
}
```

---

## Create Migration

```powershell id="jlwmf2"
Add-Migration AddPriceColumn
```

---

## Apply Migration

```powershell id="jlwmf3"
Update-Database
```

---

## Result

Database

```text id="jlwmf4"
Products

Id

Name

Price
```

---

# 8. Code Explanation

```powershell id="jlwmf5"
Add-Migration
```

Creates a new migration based on model changes.

---

```powershell id="jlwmf6"
Update-Database
```

Applies pending migrations to the database.

---

```csharp id="jlwmf7"
Up()
```

Contains the code to apply schema changes.

---

```csharp id="jlwmf8"
Down()
```

Contains the code to roll back schema changes.

---

```text id="jlwmf9"
Migrations Folder
```

Stores all migration files for version control.

---

# 9. Real Project Usage ⭐

In my ASP.NET Core Web API project, we used **EF Core Migrations** to manage database schema changes. Whenever a new property or table was added, we created a new migration and applied it to the SQL Server database. This ensured that the database structure stayed synchronized with the application code. Using migrations also made it easier for all team members to keep their local and shared databases updated consistently.

---

# 10. Best Practices

* Create a new migration for every schema change.
* Give migrations meaningful names.
* Review generated migration files before applying them.
* Commit migration files to source control.
* Apply migrations in the correct order.
* Test migrations before deploying to production.
* Use `Update-Database` only after verifying the migration.

---

# 11. Common Mistakes

* Editing the database manually instead of using migrations.
* Forgetting to create a migration after changing entities.
* Deleting migration files manually.
* Using unclear migration names like `Migration1`.
* Applying migrations directly to production without testing.
* Ignoring generated migration code.

---

# 12. Key Points to Remember

* Migrations keep the database schema synchronized with entity classes.
* `Add-Migration` creates a migration.
* `Update-Database` applies pending migrations.
* `Up()` applies schema changes.
* `Down()` rolls back schema changes.
* Every model change should have a new migration.
* Migration files should be committed to source control.

---

# 13. Summary

EF Core Migrations provide a safe and version-controlled way to manage database schema changes in Code-First applications. Instead of manually writing SQL scripts, developers create migrations whenever entity classes change, and EF Core generates the necessary SQL automatically. The **`Add-Migration`** command creates migration files, while **`Update-Database`** applies them to the database. This approach keeps the database synchronized with the application code, simplifies team collaboration, and supports safe schema evolution over time.

---
# Module 10 – Data Access with EF Core

# Topic 3 – Migrations (Add, Update, Evolving Schema Safely) (Part 2 – Interview Preparation)

---

# ⭐ Possible Interview Questions

## Q1. What are Migrations in Entity Framework Core?

### Answer

Migrations are a feature in Entity Framework Core that helps manage database schema changes. They keep the database synchronized with the entity classes in a Code-First approach. Whenever the model changes, EF Core generates a migration file containing the required database changes. Developers then apply the migration to update the database. This eliminates the need to manually write SQL scripts for schema updates.

---

## Q2. Why do we use Migrations?

### Answer

Migrations are used to update the database safely whenever the application model changes. They provide version control for the database schema and automatically generate SQL statements. Migrations make it easier for teams to maintain the same database structure across different environments. They also reduce manual errors and simplify deployment. Overall, they help keep the application and database synchronized.

---

## Q3. What is the purpose of the `Add-Migration` command?

### Answer

The `Add-Migration` command creates a new migration file based on changes made to the entity classes or EF Core configuration. It compares the current model with the previous migration and generates the necessary code to update the database schema. This command does not modify the database directly. It only creates the migration file.

Example:

```powershell id="4rph7k"
Add-Migration AddPriceColumn
```

---

## Q4. What does the `Update-Database` command do?

### Answer

The `Update-Database` command applies all pending migrations to the database. It executes the SQL generated by the migration files and updates the database schema. If the database does not exist, EF Core creates it first. After running this command, the database matches the current application model.

Example:

```powershell id="wjlwm2"
Update-Database
```

---

## Q5. What are the `Up()` and `Down()` methods in a Migration?

### Answer

Every migration contains two methods: `Up()` and `Down()`. The `Up()` method contains the code that applies the database changes, such as creating tables or adding columns. The `Down()` method contains the code to reverse those changes if the migration needs to be rolled back. These methods help manage database versioning safely.

---

## Q6. What happens when you add a new property to an entity?

### Answer

When a new property is added to an entity class, EF Core does not automatically update the database. First, a new migration must be created using `Add-Migration`. Then, `Update-Database` is executed to apply the migration. EF Core generates the SQL required to add the new column to the corresponding table.

---

## Q7. What is meant by "evolving the database schema safely"?

### Answer

As an application grows, new tables, columns, and relationships are added. Instead of modifying the database manually, developers create migrations for each change. This keeps the database schema synchronized with the application code while preserving existing data. It also provides a history of all schema changes, making updates safer and easier to manage.

---

## Q8. Can a Migration be rolled back?

### Answer

Yes. Migrations can be rolled back using the `Down()` method. Developers can update the database to a previous migration if needed. This is useful when a schema change causes issues and needs to be reverted. The rollback process should always be tested before using it in production.

---

## Q9. How did you use Migrations in your project?

### Answer

In my ASP.NET Core Web API project, we used EF Core Migrations whenever the database schema changed. After modifying entity classes, we created a new migration using `Add-Migration` and applied it with `Update-Database`. This kept the SQL Server database synchronized with the application code. Migration files were also maintained in source control so that every team member could apply the same database changes.

---

## Q10. Why should migration files be committed to source control?

### Answer

Migration files represent the history of database schema changes. By committing them to source control, every developer in the team can apply the same database updates. This ensures consistency across development, testing, and production environments. It also helps track when and why schema changes were made.

---

# ⭐ Project-Based Interview Questions

## Q1. What was your migration process in your project?

### Answer

Whenever an entity class changed, we created a new migration using `Add-Migration`. After reviewing the generated migration, we ran `Update-Database` to apply the changes to the SQL Server database. The migration files were committed to Git so the whole team could use them.

---

## Q2. Did you modify the database manually?

### Answer

No. We used EF Core Migrations to manage schema changes instead of manually writing SQL scripts. This kept the database synchronized with the application model and reduced the chance of errors.

---

## Q3. What happens if you change an entity class but don't create a migration?

### Answer

The application model and the database schema become inconsistent. New properties or tables will not exist in the database, which can lead to runtime errors when the application accesses those fields.

---

# ⭐ Rapid Fire Questions

1. Which EF Core feature manages database schema changes? → **Migrations**
2. Which command creates a migration? → **`Add-Migration`**
3. Which command applies migrations? → **`Update-Database`**
4. Which method applies schema changes? → **`Up()`**
5. Which method rolls back schema changes? → **`Down()`**
6. Where are migration files stored? → **Migrations folder**
7. Should every model change have a migration? → **Yes**
8. Do migrations automatically update the database after changing entities? → **No**
9. Should migration files be committed to Git? → **Yes**
10. Does `Add-Migration` modify the database? → **No**

---

# ⭐ Follow-up Questions

## Q1. What does `Remove-Migration` do?

### Answer

`Remove-Migration` removes the last migration that was created, provided it has not already been applied to the database. It is useful when a migration was created by mistake or needs to be regenerated.

---

## Q2. What happens if there are multiple pending migrations?

### Answer

When `Update-Database` is executed, EF Core applies all pending migrations in the correct order until the database reaches the latest version.

---

# ⭐ Interview Tips

* Explain that **Migrations keep the database schema synchronized with entity classes**.
* Remember:

  * **`Add-Migration`** → Creates a migration.
  * **`Update-Database`** → Applies the migration.
* Explain the purpose of **`Up()`** and **`Down()`**.
* Mention that **migration files should be committed to source control**.
* In your project example, state that you used **EF Core Migrations with SQL Server** to manage schema changes.

---

# ⭐ Revision Notes

* Migrations manage database schema changes.
* Used with the Code-First approach.
* `Add-Migration` creates a migration file.
* `Update-Database` applies pending migrations.
* `Up()` applies schema changes.
* `Down()` rolls back schema changes.
* Every entity change should have a new migration.
* Migration files are stored in the **Migrations** folder.
* Commit migration files to Git.
* Test migrations before deploying to production.

---

# ⭐ Keywords

**EF Core Migrations, Code-First, `Add-Migration`, `Update-Database`, `Remove-Migration`, `Up()`, `Down()`, Migration File, Database Schema, SQL Server, ASP.NET Core Web API, Version Control**

---
# Module 10 – Data Access with EF Core

# Topic 4 – CRUD Operations (Part 1 – Learning)

---

# 1. Introduction

Almost every application performs four basic database operations:

* Create
* Read
* Update
* Delete

These operations are collectively known as **CRUD**.

Entity Framework Core makes CRUD operations simple by allowing developers to work with C# objects instead of writing SQL queries manually.

> **Interview Tip:** CRUD stands for **Create, Read, Update, and Delete**, which are the four basic database operations.

---

# 2. Definition

### CRUD Operations

CRUD operations are the four basic operations used to manage data in a database.

* **Create** → Insert new data.
* **Read** → Retrieve existing data.
* **Update** → Modify existing data.
* **Delete** → Remove data.

### One-Line Interview Definition

> CRUD stands for **Create, Read, Update, and Delete**, which are the basic operations used to manage database records.

---

# 3. Why Do We Use CRUD Operations?

Without CRUD

* Cannot store data.
* Cannot retrieve data.
* Cannot update information.
* Cannot remove unwanted records.

With CRUD

* Manage database records easily.
* Less code using EF Core.
* Strong type checking.
* Easy maintenance.
* Better productivity.

### Benefits

* Simple database operations.
* Less SQL code.
* Easy to understand.
* Supports LINQ.
* Faster development.

---

# 4. How It Works

Client Request

↓

Controller

↓

Service

↓

DbContext

↓

DbSet

↓

SQL Server Database

↓

Response Returned

---

# 5. Key Concepts

---

# 1. Create (Insert)

Used to add a new record to the database.

Example

```csharp
public void AddProduct(Product product)
{
    _context.Products.Add(product);

    _context.SaveChanges();
}
```

Explanation

* `Add()` marks the entity for insertion.
* `SaveChanges()` inserts the record into the database.

---

# 2. Read (Retrieve)

Used to fetch records from the database.

### Get All Records

```csharp
public List<Product> GetProducts()
{
    return _context.Products.ToList();
}
```

---

### Get Single Record

```csharp
public Product GetProduct(int id)
{
    return _context.Products
        .FirstOrDefault(p => p.Id == id);
}
```

Explanation

* `ToList()` returns all records.
* `FirstOrDefault()` returns the first matching record or `null`.

---

# 3. Update

Used to modify an existing record.

```csharp
public void UpdateProduct(Product product)
{
    _context.Products.Update(product);

    _context.SaveChanges();
}
```

Explanation

* `Update()` marks the entity as modified.
* `SaveChanges()` updates the record in the database.

---

# 4. Delete

Used to remove a record.

```csharp
public void DeleteProduct(int id)
{
    var product = _context.Products
        .Find(id);

    if(product != null)
    {
        _context.Products.Remove(product);

        _context.SaveChanges();
    }
}
```

Explanation

* `Find()` searches by primary key.
* `Remove()` marks the entity for deletion.
* `SaveChanges()` deletes the record.

---

# 5. SaveChanges()

`SaveChanges()` commits all pending changes to the database.

Example

```csharp
_context.SaveChanges();
```

Without `SaveChanges()`, no insert, update, or delete operation is saved.

---

# 6. Common CRUD Methods

| Method             | Purpose                        |
| ------------------ | ------------------------------ |
| `Add()`            | Insert a record                |
| `AddRange()`       | Insert multiple records        |
| `Find()`           | Find by primary key            |
| `FirstOrDefault()` | Retrieve first matching record |
| `ToList()`         | Retrieve all records           |
| `Update()`         | Update a record                |
| `Remove()`         | Delete a record                |
| `SaveChanges()`    | Save all changes               |

---

# 6. Complete Code Example

## Product Model

```csharp
public class Product
{
    public int Id { get; set; }

    public string Name { get; set; }

    public decimal Price { get; set; }
}
```

---

## Product Service

```csharp
public class ProductService
{
    private readonly AppDbContext _context;

    public ProductService(AppDbContext context)
    {
        _context = context;
    }

    public void AddProduct(Product product)
    {
        _context.Products.Add(product);
        _context.SaveChanges();
    }

    public List<Product> GetProducts()
    {
        return _context.Products.ToList();
    }

    public Product GetProduct(int id)
    {
        return _context.Products
            .FirstOrDefault(p => p.Id == id);
    }

    public void UpdateProduct(Product product)
    {
        _context.Products.Update(product);
        _context.SaveChanges();
    }

    public void DeleteProduct(int id)
    {
        var product = _context.Products.Find(id);

        if (product != null)
        {
            _context.Products.Remove(product);
            _context.SaveChanges();
        }
    }
}
```

---

# 7. Code Explanation

```csharp
Add()
```

Adds a new entity.

---

```csharp
ToList()
```

Returns all records.

---

```csharp
FirstOrDefault()
```

Returns the first matching record.

---

```csharp
Find()
```

Finds a record using the primary key.

---

```csharp
Update()
```

Marks an entity as modified.

---

```csharp
Remove()
```

Marks an entity for deletion.

---

```csharp
SaveChanges()
```

Saves all changes to the database.

---

# 8. Real Project Usage ⭐

In my ASP.NET Core Web API project, we used EF Core to perform CRUD operations on SQL Server. The `AppDbContext` was injected into service classes through Dependency Injection. We used `Add()` to insert new records, `ToList()` and `FirstOrDefault()` to retrieve data, `Update()` to modify records, and `Remove()` to delete records. After every insert, update, or delete operation, we called `SaveChanges()` to persist the changes to the database.

---

# 9. Best Practices

* Use Dependency Injection for `DbContext`.
* Validate data before performing CRUD operations.
* Check if a record exists before updating or deleting.
* Use asynchronous methods (`ToListAsync()`, `SaveChangesAsync()`) in Web APIs.
* Handle exceptions properly.
* Keep CRUD logic inside services or repositories.

---

# 10. Common Mistakes

* Forgetting to call `SaveChanges()`.
* Updating or deleting records without checking if they exist.
* Creating `DbContext` manually instead of using Dependency Injection.
* Mixing business logic with data access logic.
* Using synchronous methods in high-traffic Web APIs.

---

# 11. Key Points to Remember

* CRUD stands for Create, Read, Update, and Delete.
* `Add()` inserts a record.
* `ToList()` retrieves all records.
* `FirstOrDefault()` retrieves a single record.
* `Find()` searches by primary key.
* `Update()` modifies a record.
* `Remove()` deletes a record.
* `SaveChanges()` persists all changes.
* Use async methods in production Web APIs.

---

# 12. Summary

CRUD operations are the foundation of database management in EF Core. They allow applications to create, retrieve, update, and delete records using C# objects instead of writing SQL queries manually. EF Core provides methods such as `Add()`, `ToList()`, `FirstOrDefault()`, `Find()`, `Update()`, `Remove()`, and `SaveChanges()` to perform these operations efficiently. Following best practices such as using Dependency Injection, validating input, and using asynchronous methods helps build reliable and maintainable ASP.NET Core Web API applications.

---
# Module 10 – Data Access with EF Core

# Topic 4 – CRUD Operations (Part 2 – Interview Preparation)

---

# ⭐ Possible Interview Questions

## Q1. What is CRUD in Entity Framework Core?

### Answer

CRUD stands for **Create, Read, Update, and Delete**. These are the four basic operations performed on database records. EF Core provides built-in methods to perform these operations using C# objects instead of writing SQL queries. CRUD operations simplify database management and reduce development time. They are commonly used in ASP.NET Core Web API applications.

---

## Q2. How do you insert data using EF Core?

### Answer

To insert data, we create a new entity object and use the `Add()` method of `DbSet`. After calling `Add()`, we call `SaveChanges()` to save the new record to the database. Without `SaveChanges()`, the data is not stored. This is the standard way to insert records in EF Core.

Example:

```csharp id="1z83pq"
_context.Products.Add(product);

_context.SaveChanges();
```

---

## Q3. How do you retrieve data using EF Core?

### Answer

EF Core provides several methods to retrieve data. `ToList()` is used to retrieve all records, while `FirstOrDefault()` retrieves the first matching record. `Find()` is commonly used to retrieve a record by its primary key. These methods return entity objects that can be used directly in the application.

Example:

```csharp id="q4l8wy"
var products = _context.Products.ToList();
```

---

## Q4. What is the difference between `Find()` and `FirstOrDefault()`?

### Answer

`Find()` is mainly used to retrieve a record using its primary key. It first checks the EF Core change tracker before querying the database, which can improve performance. `FirstOrDefault()` retrieves the first record that matches a specified condition using a LINQ expression. If no matching record is found, both methods return `null`.

Example:

```csharp id="m7d5vn"
var product = _context.Products.Find(id);
```

```csharp id="u2k9rt"
var product = _context.Products
    .FirstOrDefault(p => p.Id == id);
```

---

## Q5. How do you update data using EF Core?

### Answer

To update a record, first retrieve the entity from the database or create an entity with the updated values. Then use the `Update()` method and call `SaveChanges()`. EF Core marks the entity as modified and updates the corresponding record in the database. Without `SaveChanges()`, the update is not applied.

Example:

```csharp id="8hv2qm"
_context.Products.Update(product);

_context.SaveChanges();
```

---

## Q6. How do you delete data using EF Core?

### Answer

To delete a record, first retrieve the entity using `Find()` or another query method. Then call the `Remove()` method and finally `SaveChanges()`. EF Core marks the entity for deletion and removes it from the database when `SaveChanges()` is executed.

Example:

```csharp id="9t5gcl"
_context.Products.Remove(product);

_context.SaveChanges();
```

---

## Q7. Why is `SaveChanges()` important?

### Answer

`SaveChanges()` commits all pending changes made through the `DbContext` to the database. Methods like `Add()`, `Update()`, and `Remove()` only track changes in memory. The database is updated only after calling `SaveChanges()`. It ensures that all changes are stored permanently.

---

## Q8. What CRUD methods have you used in your project?

### Answer

In my ASP.NET Core Web API project, I used `Add()` to insert records, `ToList()` and `FirstOrDefault()` to retrieve data, `Update()` to modify records, and `Remove()` to delete records. After every insert, update, or delete operation, I called `SaveChanges()` to persist the changes to the SQL Server database. I also used LINQ queries to filter and retrieve data.

---

## Q9. Why should you check if a record exists before updating or deleting it?

### Answer

Checking whether a record exists helps prevent exceptions and unexpected behavior. If the record is not found, the application can return an appropriate response such as **404 Not Found** instead of causing an error. This improves the reliability of the application and provides a better user experience.

---

## Q10. Why are asynchronous CRUD methods recommended in Web APIs?

### Answer

Asynchronous methods such as `ToListAsync()` and `SaveChangesAsync()` prevent the application from blocking while waiting for database operations to complete. This improves scalability and allows the server to handle more requests efficiently. They are the recommended choice for ASP.NET Core Web API applications.

---

# ⭐ Project-Based Interview Questions

## Q1. How did you perform CRUD operations in your project?

### Answer

I injected `AppDbContext` into the service using Dependency Injection. I used `Add()` for insert operations, `ToList()` and `FirstOrDefault()` for reading data, `Update()` for modifying records, and `Remove()` for deleting records. After every change, I called `SaveChanges()` to update the SQL Server database.

---

## Q2. What happens if `SaveChanges()` is not called?

### Answer

Without `SaveChanges()`, EF Core only tracks the changes in memory. The insert, update, or delete operation is not written to the database, so no permanent changes occur.

---

## Q3. Which retrieval method did you use most frequently?

### Answer

I commonly used `ToList()` to retrieve multiple records and `FirstOrDefault()` to retrieve a single record based on a condition. I also used `Find()` when retrieving records by their primary key.

---

# ⭐ Rapid Fire Questions

1. What does CRUD stand for? → **Create, Read, Update, Delete**
2. Which method inserts a record? → **`Add()`**
3. Which method retrieves all records? → **`ToList()`**
4. Which method retrieves a single matching record? → **`FirstOrDefault()`**
5. Which method finds a record by primary key? → **`Find()`**
6. Which method updates a record? → **`Update()`**
7. Which method deletes a record? → **`Remove()`**
8. Which method saves all changes? → **`SaveChanges()`**
9. Which async method retrieves all records? → **`ToListAsync()`**
10. Which async method saves changes? → **`SaveChangesAsync()`**

---

# ⭐ Follow-up Questions

## Q1. Can `Add()` save data directly to the database?

### Answer

No. `Add()` only marks the entity for insertion. The data is actually inserted into the database only after calling `SaveChanges()` or `SaveChangesAsync()`.

---

## Q2. What does `Find()` return if the record is not found?

### Answer

`Find()` returns `null` if no record with the specified primary key exists. Developers should always check for `null` before using the returned object.

---

# ⭐ Interview Tips

* Remember the CRUD order:

  * **Create → Add()**
  * **Read → ToList(), FirstOrDefault(), Find()**
  * **Update → Update()**
  * **Delete → Remove()**
* Always mention **`SaveChanges()`** after Create, Update, and Delete.
* Explain the difference between **`Find()`** and **`FirstOrDefault()`**.
* Mention using **`ToListAsync()`** and **`SaveChangesAsync()`** in production Web APIs.
* In your project example, mention using **EF Core with SQL Server through `AppDbContext`**.

---

# ⭐ Revision Notes

* CRUD stands for **Create, Read, Update, Delete**.
* `Add()` inserts a new record.
* `ToList()` retrieves all records.
* `FirstOrDefault()` retrieves the first matching record.
* `Find()` retrieves a record by primary key.
* `Update()` modifies an existing record.
* `Remove()` deletes a record.
* `SaveChanges()` persists all pending changes.
* Use `ToListAsync()` and `SaveChangesAsync()` in Web APIs.
* Always validate that a record exists before updating or deleting.

---

# ⭐ Keywords

**CRUD, Create, Read, Update, Delete, `Add()`, `ToList()`, `FirstOrDefault()`, `Find()`, `Update()`, `Remove()`, `SaveChanges()`, `ToListAsync()`, `SaveChangesAsync()`, EF Core, DbContext, DbSet, ASP.NET Core Web API**

---
# Module 10 – Data Access with EF Core

# Topic 5 – Querying (LINQ-to-Entities, AsNoTracking, Projection to DTOs) (Part 1 – Learning)

---

# 1. Introduction

Most applications need to retrieve data from the database. Instead of writing SQL queries manually, Entity Framework Core allows developers to use **LINQ (Language Integrated Query)**.

EF Core converts LINQ queries into SQL queries and executes them against the database.

While querying data, we often use:

* **LINQ-to-Entities**
* **AsNoTracking()**
* **Projection to DTOs**

These techniques improve code readability, performance, and security.

> **Interview Tip:** LINQ is used to query data, `AsNoTracking()` improves read performance, and DTO projection returns only the required data.

---

# 2. Definition

### LINQ-to-Entities

LINQ-to-Entities is a feature of EF Core that allows developers to query the database using LINQ expressions instead of SQL.

### AsNoTracking()

`AsNoTracking()` tells EF Core not to track retrieved entities because they are only being read.

### Projection to DTOs

Projection is the process of selecting only the required fields from an entity and mapping them to a DTO.

### One-Line Interview Definition

> LINQ-to-Entities queries the database using LINQ, `AsNoTracking()` improves read performance, and projection maps entity data to DTOs.

---

# 3. Why Do We Use These Features?

Without them

* Write SQL manually.
* Retrieve unnecessary columns.
* Extra memory usage.
* Slower read operations.
* Expose database entities directly.

With them

* Cleaner code.
* Better performance.
* Less memory usage.
* Faster queries.
* Better API design.

### Benefits

* Easy querying.
* Strong type checking.
* Improved performance.
* Better security.
* Cleaner API responses.

---

# 4. How It Works

Controller

↓

Service

↓

LINQ Query

↓

EF Core

↓

SQL Query

↓

SQL Server

↓

DTO Returned

---

# 5. Key Concepts

---

# 1. LINQ-to-Entities

Retrieve all products

```csharp
var products = _context.Products.ToList();
```

Generated SQL

```sql
SELECT * FROM Products
```

---

Retrieve products with price greater than 1000

```csharp
var products = _context.Products
    .Where(p => p.Price > 1000)
    .ToList();
```

Generated SQL

```sql
SELECT * FROM Products
WHERE Price > 1000
```

EF Core automatically converts LINQ into SQL.

---

# 2. Common LINQ Methods

| Method                | Purpose                  |
| --------------------- | ------------------------ |
| `Where()`             | Filter records           |
| `Select()`            | Choose specific columns  |
| `OrderBy()`           | Ascending sort           |
| `OrderByDescending()` | Descending sort          |
| `FirstOrDefault()`    | First matching record    |
| `SingleOrDefault()`   | Single matching record   |
| `Any()`               | Checks if data exists    |
| `Count()`             | Count records            |
| `Skip()`              | Skip records             |
| `Take()`              | Retrieve limited records |

---

# 3. AsNoTracking()

Normally EF Core tracks retrieved entities.

```csharp
var products = _context.Products
    .ToList();
```

Tracking consumes memory.

If data is read-only

```csharp
var products = _context.Products
    .AsNoTracking()
    .ToList();
```

Benefits

* Faster queries
* Less memory usage
* Better performance

Use it only for read-only data.

---

# 4. Projection to DTOs

Entity

```csharp
public class Product
{
    public int Id { get; set; }

    public string Name { get; set; }

    public decimal Price { get; set; }

    public string InternalCode { get; set; }
}
```

DTO

```csharp
public class ProductDto
{
    public int Id { get; set; }

    public string Name { get; set; }

    public decimal Price { get; set; }
}
```

Query

```csharp
var products = _context.Products
    .Select(p => new ProductDto
    {
        Id = p.Id,
        Name = p.Name,
        Price = p.Price
    })
    .ToList();
```

Result

Only

* Id
* Name
* Price

`InternalCode` is not returned.

---

# 5. Combining All Three

```csharp
var products = _context.Products
    .AsNoTracking()
    .Where(p => p.Price > 1000)
    .OrderBy(p => p.Name)
    .Select(p => new ProductDto
    {
        Id = p.Id,
        Name = p.Name,
        Price = p.Price
    })
    .ToList();
```

This query

* Filters records
* Doesn't track entities
* Returns DTOs
* Orders data

---

# 6. Complete Code Example

## DTO

```csharp
public class ProductDto
{
    public int Id { get; set; }
```
